section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man of black African family origin is newly diagnosed with stage 1 hypertension. His ABPM daytime average is 152/96 mmHg. He has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medication. His U&Es are within normal limits. Which is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive drug treatment for this patient?
correct_answer: amlodipine
explanation: Per NICE NG136, adults of black African or African-Caribbean family origin (any age) should be offered a calcium channel blocker as first-line treatment, regardless of age. Amlodipine is the standard first-line CCB. ACE inhibitors are not first-line in this group due to lower efficacy (lower renin levels) and a higher risk of angioedema. ARBs (e.g. losartan) are preferred over ACEi if a RAAS blocker is needed at step 2.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 52-year-old white man is newly diagnosed with stage 2 hypertension (clinic BP 168/104 mmHg, ABPM daytime 158/98 mmHg). He has no other medical conditions, takes no regular medication, and U&Es are normal. Which is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive drug treatment?
correct_answer: ramipril
explanation: Per NICE NG136, adults under 55 years not of black African/Caribbean family origin should be offered an ACE inhibitor (or ARB if not tolerated) as first-line. Ramipril is a standard first-line ACE inhibitor. CCBs are first-line for those aged 55+ or of black African/Caribbean family origin. Thiazide-like diuretics (indapamide) are first-line only if a CCB is unsuitable. Atenolol and other beta-blockers are no longer routinely recommended for primary hypertension management.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 71-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has hypertension controlled on amlodipine 10 mg OD and ramipril 10 mg OD. Her most recent clinic BP is 158/92 mmHg, confirmed by ABPM daytime average of 152/88 mmHg. She has no other comorbidities. U&Es are normal. Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_answer: add indapamide 2.5 mg OD
explanation: Per NICE NG136, step 3 antihypertensive therapy (when BP not controlled on step 2 CCB + ACEi/ARB) is to add a thiazide-like diuretic such as indapamide. Spironolactone is reserved for step 4 (resistant hypertension) and only if blood potassium is ≤4.5 mmol/L. Amlodipine doses above 10 mg are not licensed. Beta-blockers are not part of the standard step-up pathway unless there is a compelling indication.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 39-year-old pregnant woman at 14 weeks' gestation is found to have a clinic BP of 158/102 mmHg on two separate readings, confirmed as hypertension. She has no proteinuria. She has no other medical conditions and is not taking any regular medication. Which is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive treatment?
correct_answer: labetalol
explanation: Per NICE NG133 (hypertension in pregnancy), labetalol is first-line for treating hypertension in pregnancy (chronic, gestational, or pre-eclampsia). Nifedipine and methyldopa are alternatives. ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) and ARBs (losartan) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal renal toxicity, oligohydramnios and risk of fetal death. Atenolol is associated with fetal growth restriction. Amlodipine is not licensed or recommended in pregnancy due to limited safety data.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 62-year-old man with hypertension is taking ramipril 10 mg OD. He attends the pharmacy reporting a persistent dry cough that has been present for 6 weeks and is interrupting his sleep. He has no other respiratory symptoms. He has no allergies and no other medical conditions. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise to continue ramipril and review cough in 4 weeks
option_c: refer to GP to switch ramipril to losartan
option_d: refer to GP to switch ramipril to amlodipine
option_e: stop ramipril and recommend over-the-counter cough suppressant
correct_answer: refer to GP to switch ramipril to losartan
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG136, ACE inhibitor-induced dry cough affects 5-15% of patients and typically does not resolve while continuing the drug. The recommended action is to switch to an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) such as losartan or candesartan, which acts on the same RAAS pathway but does not cause cough (no bradykinin accumulation). The patient retains the cardiovascular protective benefit. Amlodipine is a different class and would not be a like-for-like substitute. The cough is unlikely to resolve with continued ramipril and may indicate a need to investigate other causes if it persists after switching.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 64-year-old man is discharged from hospital following a STEMI treated with primary PCI. He has no significant bleeding risk factors. His discharge prescription includes aspirin 75 mg OD, atorvastatin 80 mg OD, bisoprolol 2.5 mg OD, and ramipril 2.5 mg OD. Which is the most appropriate additional antiplatelet to prescribe?
option_c: dipyridamole 200 mg modified-release BD for 12 months
correct_answer: ticagrelor 90 mg BD for 12 months
explanation: Per NICE NG185, following a STEMI treated with PCI, ticagrelor 90 mg BD plus aspirin (dual antiplatelet therapy, DAPT) is recommended for 12 months unless contraindicated or the patient has a high bleeding risk. Clopidogrel is reserved for patients with high bleeding risk or those who require concurrent oral anticoagulation. Prasugrel is an alternative to ticagrelor (per NICE TA317) but is not first-line in NG185. Dipyridamole is not indicated in ACS.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 58-year-old woman is admitted with an NSTEMI. She is started on aspirin, ticagrelor, fondaparinux and atorvastatin. She has a past medical history of atrial fibrillation, for which she takes apixaban 5 mg BD. She undergoes PCI with stent placement. Which is the most appropriate antiplatelet/anticoagulation strategy for the 12 months following discharge?
option_c: aspirin + ticagrelor for 12 months, stop apixaban
option_d: clopidogrel + apixaban for up to 12 months
correct_answer: clopidogrel + apixaban for up to 12 months
explanation: Per NICE NG185, for patients with a new or existing indication for oral anticoagulation who have had PCI for ACS, clopidogrel (replacing prasugrel or ticagrelor) plus their oral anticoagulant should be offered for up to 12 months. Triple therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel + anticoagulant) is generally avoided beyond the very short term (1 week to 1 month) due to high bleeding risk. Stopping apixaban would leave her unprotected from AF-related stroke. Ticagrelor and prasugrel are not used with anticoagulants due to bleeding risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man had an MI 10 days ago and was discharged on aspirin 75 mg OD, ticagrelor 90 mg BD, atorvastatin 80 mg OD, bisoprolol 5 mg OD and ramipril 5 mg OD. He attends the pharmacy with a prescription that omits the bisoprolol. On further questioning, he reports that his GP stopped it because he 'felt tired'. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: accept the prescription change and dispense as written
option_b: contact the GP to discuss whether the bisoprolol can be reduced rather than stopped
option_c: dispense the prescription but advise the patient to take half a bisoprolol tablet each day
option_d: refer the patient to a cardiology specialist for review
option_e: tell the patient to restart the bisoprolol at the previous dose
correct_answer: contact the GP to discuss whether the bisoprolol can be reduced rather than stopped
explanation: Per NICE NG185 and the BNF, beta-blockers reduce mortality after MI and should be continued long-term in patients with reduced LV function and at least for 12 months in those with preserved LV function. They should not be stopped abruptly due to risk of rebound tachycardia and worsening ischaemia. A pharmacist receiving a prescription that omits a prognostic post-MI medication has a professional responsibility to clarify. Suggesting a dose reduction (rather than discontinuation) addresses the patient's tiredness while maintaining cardiovascular protection.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old woman attends the pharmacy 3 days after starting atorvastatin 80 mg OD following an NSTEMI. She reports significant generalised muscle pain that started 24 hours after her first dose. She has no other new symptoms. She is also taking aspirin 75 mg, ticagrelor 90 mg BD, bisoprolol 2.5 mg OD and ramipril 5 mg OD. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise the patient to continue atorvastatin and review in 2 weeks
option_b: advise the patient to take atorvastatin every other day
option_c: refer the patient to A&E urgently for assessment of rhabdomyolysis
option_d: refer the patient to the GP urgently for creatine kinase (CK) measurement
option_e: stop atorvastatin and suggest the GP switches to a different statin
correct_answer: refer the patient to the GP urgently for creatine kinase (CK) measurement
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA Drug Safety Update on statin-induced myopathy: any patient on a statin reporting unexplained muscle pain, tenderness or weakness should have CK measured before continuing. The risk of myopathy is dose-related and is highest in the first weeks of treatment. Same-day GP review for CK measurement is appropriate. Significantly elevated CK (>5x upper limit of normal) requires statin discontinuation. A&E is reserved for suspected rhabdomyolysis with red/brown urine, severe pain or systemic symptoms. Continuing or modifying the dose without checking CK is not appropriate at this stage.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man with newly diagnosed heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 30%) is being initiated on guideline-directed medical therapy. He has no contraindications. His current medication is amlodipine 5 mg OD for hypertension. Which combination of medication classes represents the 'four pillars' of treatment for HFrEF?
option_a: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker + loop diuretic + SGLT2 inhibitor
option_b: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor
option_c: ACE inhibitor + calcium channel blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor
correct_answer: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor
explanation: Per NICE NG106 and the 2021 ESC HF guidelines, the 'four pillars' of disease-modifying therapy for HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) are: (1) ACE inhibitor (or ARB or ARNI), (2) beta-blocker licensed for HF (bisoprolol, carvedilol, nebivolol), (3) mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone), and (4) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin). All four classes have independent mortality benefit. Loop diuretics (e.g. furosemide) treat congestion symptoms but do not reduce mortality. CCBs (other than amlodipine for HTN/angina) and most non-dihydropyridines are contraindicated in HFrEF.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old woman with stable HFrEF (LVEF 32%) on optimal medical therapy is reviewed in clinic. Her current medication is bisoprolol 10 mg OD, dapagliflozin 10 mg OD, ramipril 10 mg OD, and spironolactone 25 mg OD. She remains symptomatic (NYHA class II-III) and her resting heart rate is 78 bpm and in sinus rhythm. Her BP is 116/72 mmHg. Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_answer: switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan
explanation: Per NICE TA388 and NG106, sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) is recommended for patients with symptomatic HFrEF (NYHA class II-IV, LVEF ≤35%) already on optimal therapy including an ACE inhibitor or ARB. A 36-hour washout is required when switching from an ACE inhibitor to avoid angioedema. Ivabradine is only indicated if HR ≥75 bpm in sinus rhythm AND patient is on optimised therapy (this patient's HR is 78 but they have not had a full optimisation trial of sacubitril/valsartan yet). Bisoprolol is already at maximum licensed dose. Furosemide does not improve prognosis. Eplerenone is an alternative MRA but offers no advantage over spironolactone for prognosis.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 81-year-old man with chronic heart failure is admitted with acute pulmonary oedema. He is acutely breathless, has bilateral basal crackles, and his oxygen saturations are 88% on air. His regular medication is bisoprolol 5 mg OD, dapagliflozin 10 mg OD, furosemide 40 mg OD, ramipril 5 mg OD, and spironolactone 25 mg OD. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_a: high-flow oxygen, IV furosemide and consider IV GTN
option_e: intravenous magnesium sulfate and IV salbutamol
correct_answer: high-flow oxygen, IV furosemide and consider IV GTN
explanation: Per NICE NG106 (acute heart failure) and Resuscitation Council UK guidance: immediate management of acute pulmonary oedema includes sitting the patient upright, high-flow oxygen to maintain SpO₂ >94%, intravenous loop diuretic (furosemide bolus), and consideration of IV GTN for vasodilation if SBP >110 mmHg. Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP) may be added. IV fluids are contraindicated in acute pulmonary oedema (would worsen congestion). Salbutamol/adrenaline/hydrocortisone are for anaphylaxis or severe asthma. Digoxin is not first-line acute management.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 76-year-old woman with non-valvular atrial fibrillation is being started on apixaban for stroke prevention. She weighs 72 kg and her serum creatinine is 105 micromol/L. The dose-reduction criteria for apixaban in non-valvular AF are: reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily if at least 2 of the following apply — age ≥80 years, body weight ≤60 kg, serum creatinine ≥133 micromol/L. Which is the most appropriate dose of apixaban?
correct_answer: 5 mg twice daily
explanation: Per BNF apixaban monograph and the SmPC, the standard non-valvular AF dose is 5 mg twice daily. The reduced 2.5 mg twice daily dose is only used if at least 2 of 3 criteria are met: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, creatinine ≥133 micromol/L. This patient meets none of these (age 76, weight 72, creatinine 105), so the standard 5 mg BD applies.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 82-year-old woman with newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation is being assessed for stroke prevention. She has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and had a TIA 3 years ago. She has no significant bleeding history. Her CHA₂DS₂-VASc score is 7 (age ≥75 = 2, female = 1, hypertension = 1, diabetes = 1, prior TIA = 2). What does this score indicate, and what is the recommended intervention?
option_a: low risk; no antithrombotic therapy required
option_b: low-moderate risk; offer aspirin 75 mg OD
option_c: moderate risk; consider DOAC after further assessment
option_d: high risk; offer DOAC for stroke prevention
option_e: very high risk; offer warfarin in preference to DOAC
correct_answer: high risk; offer DOAC for stroke prevention
explanation: Per NICE NG196 (atrial fibrillation): a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of ≥2 in women or ≥1 in men indicates a level of stroke risk where anticoagulation should be offered. DOACs (apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, rivaroxaban) are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular AF. Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in AF. The HAS-BLED score should also be calculated to assess bleeding risk and identify modifiable risk factors, but a high HAS-BLED is not a reason to withhold anticoagulation in someone at high stroke risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man with paroxysmal AF is taking warfarin 5 mg OD with a target INR of 2.0-3.0. His most recent INR is 5.8 (no bleeding) and he reports that he started a course of clarithromycin 500 mg BD 4 days ago for a chest infection. Which is the most likely explanation for the elevated INR?
option_a: Clarithromycin causes direct inhibition of vitamin K reductase
option_b: Clarithromycin displaces warfarin from plasma protein binding sites
option_c: Clarithromycin induces CYP enzymes, increasing warfarin metabolism
option_d: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, increasing warfarin levels
correct_answer: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, increasing warfarin levels
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix and the Stockley's Drug Interactions reference: clarithromycin is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4 and to a lesser extent CYP2C9 (the main enzyme metabolising the more potent S-warfarin enantiomer). This reduces warfarin metabolism and increases INR, with a significant risk of bleeding. Macrolides such as erythromycin and clarithromycin (and similarly the 'azole' antifungals) are classic warfarin interactions. Per NICE NG196 and BNF guidance, the warfarin dose should be reduced or omitted and INR monitored, with vitamin K considered if INR ≥8 or there is bleeding. Alternative antibiotics with less interaction (e.g. doxycycline) may be preferable.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 71-year-old man taking rivaroxaban 20 mg OD for non-valvular AF is admitted with significant gastrointestinal bleeding. His last dose was 6 hours ago. He is haemodynamically unstable. Which reversal agent is most appropriate?
correct_answer: andexanet alfa
explanation: Per BNF, NICE TA697 and the rivaroxaban SmPC: andexanet alfa is the specific reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban) in life-threatening or uncontrolled bleeding. It works by binding and sequestering the factor Xa inhibitor. Idarucizumab is the specific reversal agent for dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor) — different mechanism. Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) is a non-specific reversal option that may be used if andexanet alfa is unavailable. Protamine reverses heparin, not DOACs.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 56-year-old man is reviewed for cardiovascular risk assessment in primary care. He has no past medical history of cardiovascular disease, diabetes or chronic kidney disease. His QRISK3 10-year cardiovascular risk score is calculated as 14%. His total cholesterol is 6.2 mmol/L and his BP is 138/86 mmHg. He drinks 20 units of alcohol per week and is a non-smoker. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: lifestyle advice only and reassess in 5 years
option_b: offer atorvastatin 20 mg OD for primary prevention
option_c: offer atorvastatin 80 mg OD for primary prevention
option_e: refer to a lipid clinic for specialist assessment
correct_answer: offer atorvastatin 20 mg OD for primary prevention
explanation: Per NICE NG181 (cardiovascular disease: risk assessment and reduction): adults with a 10-year QRISK3 score of ≥10% should be offered atorvastatin 20 mg OD for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease, after lifestyle advice has been discussed. The threshold was lowered from 20% to 10% in 2014. Atorvastatin 80 mg is the secondary-prevention dose (used after CVD events). Ezetimibe is reserved for patients intolerant of statins or as an add-on when LDL target is not met on maximal statin therapy. Lifestyle advice alone is inappropriate at QRISK ≥10%.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 49-year-old woman has been taking atorvastatin 80 mg OD for secondary prevention following an MI 6 months ago. Her non-HDL cholesterol on this dose remains at 3.2 mmol/L. Per NICE guidance, the target reduction from baseline non-HDL cholesterol is at least 40%. Her pre-treatment baseline non-HDL cholesterol was 5.8 mmol/L. She has no muscle symptoms. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: no change required as target reduction is achieved
option_e: switch atorvastatin to rosuvastatin 40 mg OD
correct_answer: no change required as target reduction is achieved
explanation: Per NICE NG181, the treatment goal in CVD secondary prevention is a >40% reduction in non-HDL cholesterol from baseline. Calculation: reduction = (5.8 − 3.2) ÷ 5.8 × 100% = 44.8%. This exceeds the 40% target. Ezetimibe would be considered if the target was NOT met. Switching to rosuvastatin or adding fibrates is not first-line if target is achieved. Atorvastatin 80 mg is the recommended dose for secondary prevention and should not be reduced if tolerated.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 64-year-old man with newly diagnosed stable angina is started on bisoprolol 2.5 mg OD. He attends the pharmacy 6 weeks later reporting continued exertional chest pain about 3 times per week. His bisoprolol has been titrated to 10 mg OD. He has no other medical conditions. He takes aspirin 75 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, ramipril 5 mg, and GTN spray PRN. His BP is 132/78 mmHg, pulse 68 bpm regular. Which is the most appropriate additional treatment for symptom control?
correct_answer: amlodipine
explanation: Per NICE NG126 (stable angina): if symptoms are not controlled on monotherapy with a beta-blocker or CCB, the second-line is to add the other class — i.e. add a long-acting dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine) to the beta-blocker. Rate-limiting CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) MUST NOT be combined with beta-blockers due to risk of severe bradycardia and AV block. Long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mononitrate), nicorandil, ivabradine and ranolazine are third-line options used when beta-blocker + CCB combination is not tolerated or fails.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old patient with stable angina is using GTN 400 micrograms/dose spray for chest pain. He asks the pharmacist when he should consider that the angina is no longer 'stable' and seek emergency help. Which is the most appropriate counselling advice?
option_a: call 999 if chest pain occurs more than once a week
option_b: call 999 if pain is not relieved 5 minutes after a second GTN dose, taken 5 minutes after the first
option_c: call 999 if pain is not relieved 15 minutes after the first GTN dose
option_d: call 999 if pain occurs at rest, regardless of GTN response
option_e: call 999 only if the patient also has new shortness of breath
correct_answer: call 999 if pain is not relieved 5 minutes after a second GTN dose, taken 5 minutes after the first
explanation: Per NICE NG185, NICE CKS (angina) and British Heart Foundation guidance: patients using GTN should be advised that if chest pain persists 5 minutes after a first GTN dose, a second dose should be taken, and if pain persists a further 5 minutes after the second dose (i.e. ~10 minutes total of unrelieved pain), they should call 999. This advice is critical for early recognition of acute coronary syndrome. Pain at rest may indicate unstable angina but the GTN response protocol is the standard practical advice given to patients.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 43-year-old woman attends the pharmacy with a prescription for 60 Epilim Chrono (sodium valproate m/r) 500 mg tablets, one tablet twice daily. She has been taking the same dose for 5 years for epilepsy and has an annual neurology review in 4 months. She was independently reviewed by two specialists at her last annual review, with the conclusion that there is no other effective treatment for her. She understands the medication, but you want to offer further counselling. Which action is most important for this patient?
option_a: advise that her blood pressure should be monitored regularly
option_b: advise that her liver function should be monitored regularly and counsel on symptoms of liver disorder
option_c: check she is on a Pregnancy Prevention Programme and understands the patient card and risks in pregnancy
option_d: counsel to report any visual effects to a doctor and advise that she should not drive if affected
option_e: recommend she avoids overheating during strenuous activity or in a warm environment
correct_answer: check she is on a Pregnancy Prevention Programme and understands the patient card and risks in pregnancy
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Updates (January 2024 and September 2024) and GPhC valproate guidance: women of childbearing potential taking valproate must be enrolled in the Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP). At each dispensing, the pharmacist must confirm the patient understands the risks (~11% congenital malformations, 30–40% neurodevelopmental disorders), is using effective contraception, and has signed the Annual Risk Acknowledgement Form. Since January 2024, valproate must not be initiated in any new patient under 55 (male or female) unless two specialists independently document there is no alternative — which has been completed in this case.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man with newly diagnosed epilepsy is being considered for treatment with valproate. He has had two generalised tonic-clonic seizures and EEG/MRI investigations are complete. The neurologist documents that other anti-epileptic options have been tried or considered but are not appropriate. Per current MHRA guidance, what additional regulatory requirement must be met before valproate can be initiated?
option_a: a second independent specialist must agree there is no alternative effective treatment
option_b: only a community pharmacist needs to counter-sign the prescription
option_c: the patient must complete a 6-month trial of two alternatives first
option_d: the patient must have a negative pregnancy test (despite being male)
option_e: two prescriptions from the same neurologist must be issued
correct_answer: a second independent specialist must agree there is no alternative effective treatment
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update January 2024, since 31 January 2024 valproate must not be initiated in any new patient (male or female) under 55 years unless two specialists independently consider and document that there is no other effective or tolerated treatment, or there are compelling reasons that the reproductive risks do not apply. This requirement applies to both sexes following emerging evidence of paternal valproate exposure being associated with neurodevelopmental disorders in offspring (MHRA September 2024 update).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman with epilepsy is taking lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily. She is planning pregnancy and asks about folic acid. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: no folic acid is required as lamotrigine has minimal teratogenic risk
option_b: take folic acid 400 micrograms OD from conception until 12 weeks gestation
option_c: take folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception until at least 12 weeks gestation
option_d: take folic acid 5 mg OD from the second trimester onwards
option_e: take folic acid 5 mg OD only if neural tube defect occurs in a previous pregnancy
correct_answer: take folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception until at least 12 weeks gestation
explanation: Per BNF, UK Teratology Information Service (UKTIS) and RCOG guidance: women taking anti-epileptic drugs (including lamotrigine) are at increased risk of neural tube defects and should take high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily from preconception until at least 12 weeks of pregnancy. The standard 400 micrograms dose is insufficient. Lamotrigine is one of the preferred AEDs in pregnancy but pre-conception planning is still essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 19-year-old woman presents to the pharmacy reporting a widespread red rash that started 10 days after starting lamotrigine 25 mg OD for newly diagnosed epilepsy. The rash is on her trunk and limbs, with some target lesions, and is associated with mild fever and mouth ulcers. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise to continue lamotrigine and apply hydrocortisone 1% cream
option_b: arrange same-day GP appointment for assessment
option_d: refer the patient urgently to A&E
option_e: reassure that this is a common, self-limiting side effect
correct_answer: refer the patient urgently to A&E
explanation: Per BNF lamotrigine monograph and MHRA guidance: lamotrigine is associated with a risk of serious skin reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), particularly in the first 8 weeks of treatment and when the recommended titration schedule is exceeded. Target lesions, mucosal involvement (mouth ulcers) and systemic features (fever) are red-flag features of SJS. Urgent A&E referral is mandatory — SJS/TEN have significant mortality without rapid recognition and treatment.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with no past medical history presents to the GP with a 3-month history of low mood, anhedonia and poor sleep. She is diagnosed with more severe depression (PHQ-9 score 18). She declines psychological therapy and prefers medication. She has no contraindications. Per NICE NG222, which is the most appropriate first-line drug treatment?
correct_answer: citalopram 20 mg OD
explanation: Per NICE NG222 (depression in adults), SSRIs (e.g. sertraline, citalopram, fluoxetine) are first-line antidepressant pharmacotherapy due to a favourable balance of efficacy, tolerability and safety in overdose. They have replaced tricyclic antidepressants as first-line. Mirtazapine and venlafaxine are alternative first-line options but are not the most commonly chosen. Phenelzine is an MAOI used as further-line treatment. Amitriptyline is highly toxic in overdose and not first-line.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 58-year-old man has been taking sertraline 100 mg OD for 18 months for depression. He is well, with no symptoms, and the GP has agreed to discontinue treatment. Which is the most appropriate advice on stopping sertraline?
option_a: gradually taper the dose over 4 or more weeks to minimise withdrawal symptoms
option_b: reduce the dose to 50 mg OD for 1 week then stop
option_c: stop sertraline abruptly as it has a long half-life
option_d: switch to fluoxetine 20 mg OD for 2 weeks and then stop
option_e: take alternate-day doses for 1 month then stop
correct_answer: gradually taper the dose over 4 or more weeks to minimise withdrawal symptoms
explanation: Per NICE NG222 and MHRA guidance on antidepressant discontinuation: when stopping an antidepressant, the dose should be reduced gradually (usually over 4 weeks or longer, sometimes much longer) in steps to minimise withdrawal symptoms. Sertraline has a short half-life and abrupt withdrawal commonly causes 'discontinuation syndrome' (dizziness, headache, flu-like symptoms, electric-shock sensations, mood changes). Fluoxetine switching is a strategy occasionally used for difficult discontinuations but is not first-line for routine stopping.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 76-year-old woman with depression and chronic kidney disease (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m²) develops drowsiness, confusion, and a serum sodium of 122 mmol/L (normal 133–146) 4 weeks after starting citalopram 20 mg OD. Which is the most likely cause?
option_b: citalopram-induced syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
correct_answer: citalopram-induced syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA Drug Safety Update: SSRIs (especially in older adults) can cause SIADH, leading to hyponatraemia. Risk factors include age >65, female sex, low BMI, concomitant diuretics, and renal impairment. The classic presentation is drowsiness/confusion with low serum sodium within weeks of starting therapy. NICE recommends checking sodium at baseline and if symptoms develop. Management: stop the SSRI, restrict fluid, and consider an alternative class (e.g. mirtazapine has lower SIADH risk).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 26-year-old man on sertraline 50 mg OD for depression is prescribed tramadol 50 mg PRN for an ankle sprain by his GP. Two days later, he attends A&E with agitation, sweating, tachycardia, tremor, hyperreflexia and a temperature of 38.6°C. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: serotonin syndrome
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix: tramadol has serotonergic effects (it inhibits serotonin reuptake) and combining with an SSRI substantially increases the risk of serotonin syndrome. The classic triad is mental status changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (sweating, tachycardia, hyperthermia), and neuromuscular abnormalities (tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus). Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is associated with dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics) and is slower in onset with rigidity rather than hyperreflexia. Management: stop the serotonergic drugs, supportive care, consider cyproheptadine.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old man with end-stage cancer is taking morphine sulfate immediate-release 30 mg PO every 12 hours. He becomes unable to swallow and the team wishes to convert him to a continuous subcutaneous morphine infusion via syringe driver over 24 hours. The PO to SC morphine conversion ratio is 2:1. How many mg of morphine sulfate should be loaded into the syringe driver for 24 hours?
correct_answer: 30 mg
explanation: Total daily PO morphine = 30 mg × 2 doses = 60 mg PO. SC morphine is roughly twice as potent as PO, so divide by 2: 60 ÷ 2 = 30 mg SC over 24 hours. This conversion is per BNF and the Palliative Care Formulary (PCF).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman with chronic neuropathic pain due to diabetic peripheral neuropathy is reviewed in clinic. Per NICE NG173, which is recommended first-line treatment options for neuropathic pain (other than trigeminal neuralgia)?
correct_answer: amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin, or pregabalin
explanation: Per NICE NG173 (neuropathic pain in adults), first-line options for all neuropathic pain except trigeminal neuralgia are: amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin, or pregabalin. The choice depends on patient factors (age, comorbidities, side-effect profile, interaction risk). NSAIDs and paracetamol have limited efficacy in neuropathic pain. Tramadol may be used as rescue or short-term. Strong opioids (e.g. morphine) are not recommended for chronic neuropathic pain except in specialist settings. Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia specifically.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man with chronic non-cancer pain is currently on tramadol 100 mg QDS, oxycodone modified-release 30 mg BD, and paracetamol 1 g QDS. He has been on this regimen for 18 months. NICE NG193 recommends review of long-term opioid use for chronic primary pain. Which is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: continue current regimen and review in 12 months
option_b: discuss with the patient the lack of evidence for long-term opioids in chronic primary pain and explore tapering
option_c: escalate the oxycodone dose for better pain control
option_e: switch oxycodone to fentanyl transdermal patch
correct_answer: discuss with the patient the lack of evidence for long-term opioids in chronic primary pain and explore tapering
explanation: Per NICE NG193 (chronic pain in over 16s): there is little or no evidence that opioids are effective for chronic primary pain and good evidence that they cause harm including dependence. The guideline recommends that opioids should NOT be started for chronic primary pain. Patients already on long-term opioids should be reviewed and supported to taper if possible. Abrupt cessation is not appropriate due to withdrawal risk and pain rebound. Escalating doses is contrary to NG193.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman comes into the pharmacy with a 2-day history of lower back pain after heavy lifting. She has no red flag features. She is exclusively breastfeeding her 3-month-old son who is healthy. She has NKDA, takes no regular medication, and has no other medical conditions. Which is the most appropriate management?
correct_answer: ibuprofen 400 mg tablets
explanation: Per NICE NG59 (low back pain and sciatica), NSAIDs are first-line analgesia for acute non-specific low back pain at the lowest effective dose for the shortest period. Ibuprofen is compatible with breastfeeding (minimal transfer into breast milk per UKDILAS). Co-codamol contains codeine, which is contraindicated in breastfeeding due to risk of opioid toxicity in the infant from CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers (MHRA warning 2013). Paracetamol alone is no longer first-line for low back pain per NG59. Acupuncture and TENS are not recommended.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 14-year-old boy weighing 45 kg has been admitted with an acute exacerbation of ulcerative colitis. His pre-admission medication includes Clenil Modulite (beclometasone) 200 micrograms/dose 1 puff BD, montelukast 5 mg OD, Octasa (mesalazine) m/r 2.4 g OD, Remicade (infliximab) 225 mg IV every 8 weeks, and salbutamol 100 micrograms/dose 2 puffs QDS PRN. His mother mentions he has been reporting frequent distressing nightmares. Which medication is most likely to be causing this adverse effect?
correct_answer: montelukast
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update (April 2024) and updated SmPC: montelukast carries a boxed warning for neuropsychiatric reactions, including sleep disorders (nightmares, insomnia), anxiety, depression, hallucinations, and behavioural changes. The risk applies to all age groups but is of particular concern in children. Patients (or carers) should be advised to seek urgent medical advice and discontinue montelukast if these symptoms develop. The other medications listed do not commonly cause nightmares.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 84-year-old woman with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease (MMSE 22) is being considered for pharmacological treatment. She lives at home with her husband and has no significant comorbidities. She is not taking any regular medication. Per NICE NG97, which is the most appropriate first-line drug treatment?
correct_answer: donepezil 5 mg OD
explanation: Per NICE NG97 (dementia): for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil, galantamine or rivastigmine) is first-line, monotherapy. Memantine is reserved for severe Alzheimer's disease, or for moderate AD in patients intolerant of AChE inhibitors. Antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone, quetiapine) are NOT first-line for cognitive symptoms — they are associated with increased mortality and stroke risk in dementia (MHRA warning) and are reserved for severe distress or risk of harm to self/others, at lowest effective dose for shortest duration.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man with newly diagnosed Parkinson's disease has motor symptoms (bradykinesia, rigidity) significantly affecting his quality of life. He is otherwise well. Per NICE NG71, which is the most appropriate first-line drug treatment?
correct_answer: co-careldopa (levodopa with carbidopa)
explanation: Per NICE NG71 (Parkinson's disease): levodopa is offered first-line for patients in early Parkinson's disease whose motor symptoms decrease their quality of life. Dopamine agonists (e.g. pramipexole) and MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline) are alternatives where motor symptoms do not affect quality of life as much, or where there is concern about motor complications. Amantadine has limited efficacy and is not first-line.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 24-year-old woman with newly diagnosed schizophrenia is being initiated on an antipsychotic. She is otherwise well. Per NICE NG178 (psychosis and schizophrenia in adults), which factor is most important when selecting the first-choice antipsychotic?
option_b: prescribing the most sedating antipsychotic available
option_c: selecting based on shared decision-making considering side-effect profile and patient preference
option_d: starting with a depot/long-acting injection by default
option_e: using haloperidol as first-line for all new diagnoses
correct_answer: selecting based on shared decision-making considering side-effect profile and patient preference
explanation: Per NICE NG178: there is no clear difference in efficacy between most antipsychotics, so the choice should be made through shared decision-making considering metabolic side effects (clozapine, olanzapine — high; aripiprazole — low), extrapyramidal effects (typical antipsychotics > atypicals), sedation, sexual side effects, and patient preference. Haloperidol is rarely first-line for new diagnoses due to EPS risk. Depot injections are typically reserved for adherence concerns or after oral failure. Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (after failure of 2 other antipsychotics, one being atypical).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman is started on lithium carbonate for bipolar affective disorder. Per NICE NG185 (bipolar) and the BNF, which set of investigations is most important to monitor during long-term lithium therapy?
option_a: full blood count and liver function tests, every 12 months
option_b: lithium level, renal function, thyroid function and calcium, every 3 months
option_d: thyroid function and HbA1c, every 12 months
option_e: urea and electrolytes only, every 12 months
correct_answer: lithium level, renal function, thyroid function and calcium, every 3 months
explanation: Per BNF lithium monograph and NICE: lithium has a narrow therapeutic range (0.6–1.0 mmol/L typically) and requires regular monitoring. Plasma lithium is measured every 3 months for the first year (then every 6 months if stable). Renal function (U&Es, eGFR), thyroid function (TFTs) and calcium are checked every 6 months — more frequently (every 3 months) in patients at higher risk (elderly, renal impairment, deteriorating function). LFTs and FBC are not routinely required for lithium. HbA1c is not a lithium-specific check.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man with bipolar affective disorder is taking lithium carbonate 800 mg ON (lithium level 0.7 mmol/L last month). He attends the pharmacy with a prescription for ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for a sports injury. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: accept the prescription as ibuprofen is safe with lithium
option_b: contact the prescriber to suggest paracetamol instead and explain the lithium-NSAID interaction
option_c: dispense the ibuprofen but advise the patient to drink plenty of water
option_e: reduce the lithium dose to 400 mg ON whilst on ibuprofen
correct_answer: contact the prescriber to suggest paracetamol instead and explain the lithium-NSAID interaction
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix: NSAIDs (including ibuprofen) significantly increase plasma lithium concentrations by reducing renal lithium clearance, with risk of lithium toxicity. This is a clinically significant interaction. Paracetamol is the safer choice for analgesia. If an NSAID is unavoidable, lithium levels should be monitored more frequently and the dose adjusted. The pharmacist has a professional duty to contact the prescriber and propose an alternative.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman attends the pharmacy 8 hours after taking 32 paracetamol 500 mg tablets in a suicide attempt. She is alert, has no symptoms, weighs 60 kg (total dose 16 g = 267 mg/kg). Per BNF and TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: administer activated charcoal 50 g orally now
option_b: advise to drink milk and reassess in 4 hours
option_d: prescribe N-acetylcysteine to take at home
option_e: refer the patient urgently to A&E for assessment and likely N-acetylcysteine infusion
correct_answer: refer the patient urgently to A&E for assessment and likely N-acetylcysteine infusion
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: any patient with a paracetamol overdose >75 mg/kg (or staggered overdose) requires assessment at A&E. This patient has ingested 267 mg/kg — well above the threshold. Plasma paracetamol must be measured at 4 hours post-ingestion and plotted against the treatment nomogram. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote and is most effective when given within 8 hours of ingestion. Activated charcoal is only useful within 1 hour of ingestion. The risk of fulminant hepatic failure makes urgent A&E referral essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old man with generalised anxiety disorder is reviewed in primary care. He has tried sertraline 200 mg OD for 12 weeks with no symptomatic benefit and has trialled CBT. Per NICE CG113, which is the most appropriate next-step pharmacological option?
option_c: start fluoxetine 20 mg OD as add-on
option_e: switch to duloxetine or another SSRI
correct_answer: switch to duloxetine or another SSRI
explanation: Per NICE CG113 (generalised anxiety disorder): if a patient does not respond to an adequate trial of one SSRI, switch to another SSRI or an SNRI (e.g. duloxetine). Pregabalin is recommended only if SSRIs/SNRIs have been ineffective or not tolerated, due to risk of dependence (Class C controlled drug since April 2019). Benzodiazepines (diazepam) are not recommended for long-term use in GAD. Adding fluoxetine to sertraline (two SSRIs) is contraindicated due to serotonin syndrome risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is taking atorvastatin 20 mg OD, Lantus (insulin glargine) 100 units/mL 18 units SC once daily at night, and metformin 1 g BD. His home blood glucose readings over 3 days: pre-breakfast 2.8–3.8 mmol/L (low), pre-lunch 6.8–7.1, pre-evening 6.9–7.4, pre-bedtime 9.5–9.8. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: change Lantus to 9 units SC twice daily
option_b: initiate a rapid-acting insulin before each meal
option_c: reduce the dose of Lantus administered each night
option_e: switch Lantus to a biphasic insulin
correct_answer: reduce the dose of Lantus administered each night
explanation: Pre-breakfast readings below target (NICE NG28: 4–7 mmol/L) indicate overnight hypoglycaemia from basal insulin. Per NICE NG28, the basal insulin dose should be reduced. Adding rapid-acting would worsen morning hypos. Splitting glargine is non-standard for once-daily basals.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 58-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes is commencing insulin. They have agreed a regimen using a single preparation injected subcutaneously twice daily with meals. Which is the most suitable insulin preparation?
correct_answer: Humalog Mix25 (biphasic insulin lispro)
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG28: a BD regimen with meals using a single preparation is best achieved with a biphasic (premixed) insulin combining a rapid- and intermediate-acting component. Humalog Mix25 (25% lispro + 75% lispro protamine) is appropriate. Single-component rapid/short-acting insulins require separate basal. Lantus is long-acting OD basal.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 64 mmol/mol) has a BMI of 32 kg/m² and established cardiovascular disease (previous MI). Her eGFR is 65 mL/min/1.73m². Per NICE NG28 (updated 2022), which is the most appropriate first-line drug treatment?
option_c: metformin plus an SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. empagliflozin)
correct_answer: metformin plus an SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. empagliflozin)
explanation: Per NICE NG28 (updated 2022): for adults with type 2 diabetes who have established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (CVD), heart failure, or are at high CV risk, metformin PLUS an SGLT2 inhibitor with proven CV benefit (e.g. empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) should be offered as first-line. This is a major change — previously metformin alone was first-line. SGLT2 inhibitors have demonstrated CV and renal benefits beyond glycaemic control.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes attends the pharmacy for advice. She is planning pregnancy. Her current regimen is insulin aspart (NovoRapid) with meals and insulin glargine (Lantus) at night. Her HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol. She is taking folic acid 400 micrograms OD. Which advice is most important?
option_b: increase folic acid to 5 mg OD from preconception
option_d: start metformin to improve glycaemic control
option_e: stop insulin during the first trimester
correct_answer: increase folic acid to 5 mg OD from preconception
explanation: Per NICE NG3 (diabetes in pregnancy) and BNF: women with diabetes planning pregnancy should take folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception until at least 12 weeks gestation to reduce the risk of neural tube defects (raised in diabetes). HbA1c should be optimised to <48 mmol/mol pre-pregnancy where possible. Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy. Insulin must be continued.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on metformin 1 g BD attends the pharmacy with a prescription for a new medication, dapagliflozin 10 mg OD. He asks about side effects. Which is the most important counselling point?
option_a: avoid grapefruit juice as it interacts with dapagliflozin
option_b: do not take with food as it affects absorption
option_c: if you become unwell with vomiting/diarrhoea or are not eating, temporarily stop dapagliflozin (sick day rule) and seek advice
option_d: take dapagliflozin only when you have eaten a carbohydrate-rich meal
option_e: urinary tract infections are extremely rare with this medication
correct_answer: if you become unwell with vomiting/diarrhoea or are not eating, temporarily stop dapagliflozin (sick day rule) and seek advice
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update and BNF: SGLT2 inhibitors carry a risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), sometimes with normal or only mildly elevated glucose ('euglycaemic DKA'). Patients should be counselled on 'sick day rules': stop the SGLT2 inhibitor temporarily during acute illness (vomiting, diarrhoea, dehydration, fasting, surgery) and seek medical advice. Also counsel on genital infection risk and Fournier's gangrene (rare). Grapefruit is not relevant.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman is brought to A&E by her husband. She is drowsy, has a fruity breath odour, deep rapid breathing, and a capillary blood glucose of 28 mmol/L. She has type 1 diabetes. Urine dip shows ketones 4+. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
explanation: Per Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS) guidance: DKA is defined by the triad of hyperglycaemia (or known diabetes), ketonaemia/ketonuria, and acidosis. Classical features: Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid), fruity ketone breath, dehydration, drowsiness/confusion. Common in type 1 diabetes due to insulin deficiency. HHS occurs in T2DM with marked hyperglycaemia (often >33 mmol/L) but minimal ketones. Management: fluids, IV insulin infusion, potassium replacement.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 38-year-old woman is referred for a thyroid function test after presenting with weight gain, cold intolerance and fatigue. Results: TSH 18 mU/L (normal 0.4–4.0), free T4 8 pmol/L (normal 9–25). She has no other medical conditions. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
option_d: no treatment, repeat TFTs in 6 months
correct_answer: levothyroxine 50–100 micrograms OD
explanation: Per NICE NG145 (thyroid disease) and BNF: this is overt primary hypothyroidism (low T4 with elevated TSH). Levothyroxine is first-line. Starting dose 50–100 micrograms OD (lower for elderly, those with CV disease, or severe hypothyroidism — start at 25 micrograms). Carbimazole is for hyperthyroidism. Liothyronine is not first-line. Propranolol is for symptom control in hyperthyroidism.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 67-year-old man taking levothyroxine 100 micrograms OD for hypothyroidism is admitted with new-onset atrial fibrillation. His TSH is 0.1 mU/L (low) and free T4 is 28 pmol/L (high). He reports recent palpitations and weight loss. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: continue current dose; TSH suppression is a target in elderly patients
option_d: reduce levothyroxine dose and recheck TFTs in 6–8 weeks
correct_answer: reduce levothyroxine dose and recheck TFTs in 6–8 weeks
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG145: this patient is over-replaced (TSH suppressed, T4 high, hyperthyroid symptoms). Over-replacement increases risk of atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis and cardiovascular events, especially in older patients. The levothyroxine dose should be reduced and TFTs rechecked after 6–8 weeks. Stopping entirely would cause hypothyroidism. TSH suppression is NOT a target — TSH 0.5–2.0 mU/L is the goal range.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on metformin 1 g BD has an HbA1c of 70 mmol/mol despite good adherence. He has CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m²), and a BMI of 34. He is at high cardiovascular risk. Per NICE NG28, which is the most appropriate add-on therapy?
correct_answer: add an SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. empagliflozin)
explanation: Per NICE NG28 (2022 update): in patients with type 2 diabetes at high CV risk or with established CVD or CKD, add an SGLT2 inhibitor with proven CV benefit (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin). SGLT2 inhibitors also have renal protective effects in CKD (cardio-renal benefit). They can be used down to eGFR ~25 mL/min/1.73m² (variable by drug). Sulfonylureas (gliclazide) cause weight gain and hypoglycaemia. Pioglitazone has heart failure and bladder cancer concerns.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old woman with severe symptomatic hypoglycaemia (blood glucose 2.1 mmol/L) is unable to swallow safely due to drowsiness. She is at home. Which is the most appropriate immediate management for a carer to administer?
correct_answer: glucagon 1 mg intramuscular injection
explanation: Per JBDS hypoglycaemia guidance: in severe hypoglycaemia where the patient is unable to swallow safely (drowsy, unconscious, fitting), IM glucagon 1 mg is the appropriate first-line out-of-hospital treatment, particularly for carers/family. Oral glucose is unsafe due to aspiration risk. IV dextrose is in-hospital management. Once the patient regains consciousness, give oral carbohydrate to prevent rebound hypoglycaemia. Note: glucagon may be less effective in liver disease, alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia or repeated hypoglycaemia.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman is prescribed metformin 500 mg BD for polycystic ovary syndrome with insulin resistance. She attends the pharmacy reporting severe diarrhoea and abdominal cramps since starting the medication 2 weeks ago. She is otherwise well. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: continue current dose; side effects will resolve in months
option_b: reduce to 500 mg OD with food and titrate up slowly, or consider switching to modified-release metformin
option_c: stop metformin and ask the GP for an alternative
option_d: take metformin on an empty stomach
option_e: take metformin with a glass of milk
correct_answer: reduce to 500 mg OD with food and titrate up slowly, or consider switching to modified-release metformin
explanation: Per BNF metformin monograph: gastrointestinal side effects (diarrhoea, nausea, abdominal pain) are very common at initiation. Standard practice is to start at lower dose (e.g. 500 mg OD with food), titrate slowly over weeks, and take with or after meals. If GI side effects persist, switching to modified-release metformin often improves tolerability. Stopping entirely is premature; an alternative is appropriate only if GI side effects don't resolve with these measures.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes takes gliclazide 80 mg BD, metformin 1 g BD, and ramipril 5 mg OD. He develops a chest infection and is prescribed clarithromycin 500 mg BD for 7 days. The next morning he experiences sweating, tremor, and a capillary glucose of 3.2 mmol/L. Which is the most likely cause?
option_a: acute kidney injury affecting metformin clearance
option_b: clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 increasing gliclazide levels
correct_answer: clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 increasing gliclazide levels
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix: clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor and significantly increases plasma concentrations of sulfonylureas (gliclazide, glibenclamide, glimepiride) metabolised by CYP3A4. This can precipitate severe hypoglycaemia. The combination should be avoided if possible; if unavoidable, monitor blood glucose closely and consider sulfonylurea dose reduction. Alternative antibiotics with less interaction (e.g. doxycycline) may be preferable.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is admitted with a urinary tract infection. Her usual regimen is basal-bolus insulin (insulin glargine 24 units ON, insulin aspart with meals). Per JBDS sick day rules, what general advice should patients with type 1 diabetes follow during acute illness?
option_a: continue insulin (do not stop), check blood glucose and ketones regularly, drink plenty of fluids
option_b: halve all insulin doses to prevent hypoglycaemia
option_c: stop all insulin if not eating
option_d: stop long-acting insulin and continue short-acting only
option_e: switch to oral antidiabetics during illness
correct_answer: continue insulin (do not stop), check blood glucose and ketones regularly, drink plenty of fluids
explanation: Per JBDS (Joint British Diabetes Societies) sick day rules for type 1 diabetes: continue insulin even if not eating (insulin requirements often INCREASE during illness due to stress hormones). Check blood glucose frequently (every 2–4 hours) and check blood/urine ketones if glucose >13 mmol/L. Maintain fluid intake. Seek medical advice if persistent hyperglycaemia, ketones, vomiting, or symptoms worsen. Stopping insulin in T1DM can precipitate DKA.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes attends the pharmacy with new symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss and fatigue. His most recent HbA1c was 68 mmol/mol on metformin and gliclazide. He weighs 95 kg. The GP plans to add a GLP-1 receptor agonist (e.g. semaglutide). Per NICE NG28, which criterion typically supports use of a GLP-1 RA in T2DM?
option_a: BMI ≥35 kg/m² (or ≥32.5 in some ethnic groups), or BMI <35 with occupational or psychological needs
correct_answer: BMI ≥35 kg/m² (or ≥32.5 in some ethnic groups), or BMI <35 with occupational or psychological needs
explanation: Per NICE NG28: GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g. semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) are recommended in triple therapy when triple oral therapy is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, AND the patient has BMI ≥35 kg/m² (white origin) or ≥32.5 (other ethnic groups). Lower BMI thresholds apply where there are specific psychological/occupational concerns (e.g. need to avoid weight gain from insulin). GLP-1 RAs are continued only if HbA1c reduces by ≥11 mmol/mol AND weight reduces by ≥3% in 6 months.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old woman has been taking prednisolone 30 mg OD for 6 weeks for polymyalgia rheumatica. The GP plans to start tapering. She has had no recent illness. Which is the most appropriate counselling point?
option_a: it is safe to stop prednisolone abruptly after a 6-week course
option_b: sudden cessation can cause an adrenal crisis — taper gradually
option_c: take a double dose on the day of any vaccination
option_e: take with grapefruit juice for better absorption
correct_answer: sudden cessation can cause an adrenal crisis — taper gradually
explanation: Per BNF and NICE guidance: corticosteroids taken for more than 3 weeks suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. Abrupt withdrawal can cause acute adrenal insufficiency (adrenal crisis), which is life-threatening. The dose should be tapered gradually under medical supervision. Patients on long-term steroids should carry a steroid card and may need increased doses during illness/stress ('sick day rules' for steroids).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman is taking the combined oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30, levonorgestrel/ethinylestradiol). She is starting carbamazepine 200 mg BD for newly diagnosed trigeminal neuralgia. Which is the most appropriate advice regarding contraception?
option_c: switch to a copper IUD or depot/implant due to enzyme induction
option_e: use condoms for 7 days then revert to Microgynon 30 alone
correct_answer: switch to a copper IUD or depot/implant due to enzyme induction
explanation: Per FSRH (Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare) guidance and BNF: carbamazepine is a strong CYP3A4 and UGT inducer, which significantly reduces the effectiveness of combined oral, transdermal and vaginal hormonal contraception, progestogen-only pills, and the contraceptive implant. Methods unaffected by enzyme inducers include: copper IUD, levonorgestrel IUS, and depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA). Switching to one of these is the recommended approach.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on metformin attends the pharmacy reporting his GP has stopped his metformin after his recent blood tests. His latest eGFR is 28 mL/min/1.73m² (was 38 six months ago). He is asymptomatic. Which is the most appropriate explanation?
option_a: eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² is a contraindication to metformin due to lactic acidosis risk
option_b: eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² always requires metformin to be stopped
option_c: metformin is being switched to a generic equivalent
option_d: metformin must be stopped if HbA1c is below 48 mmol/mol
option_e: metformin needs to be stopped for 3 months and then restarted
correct_answer: eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² is a contraindication to metformin due to lactic acidosis risk
explanation: Per BNF metformin monograph and MHRA Drug Safety Update (April 2022): metformin is contraindicated when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² due to the risk of lactic acidosis. The dose should be reviewed/reduced when eGFR is 30–45 mL/min/1.73m² (max 500 mg BD). Metformin should also be temporarily suspended around contrast imaging, surgery, and acute illness with dehydration. Alternatives in severe CKD include linagliptin, repaglinide, insulin, or pioglitazone (with caution).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes is being initiated on a basal-bolus insulin regimen. Per NICE NG17, which basal insulin is recommended as first-line?
option_b: insulin detemir (Levemir) once or twice daily
option_d: isophane insulin (NPH) once or twice daily
correct_answer: insulin detemir (Levemir) once or twice daily
explanation: Per NICE NG17 (type 1 diabetes in adults): the recommended first-line basal insulin is insulin detemir BD. Insulin glargine OD is an alternative. Insulin degludec is reserved for specific situations (e.g. recurrent symptomatic hypoglycaemia or requirement for support to inject by carer). NPH (isophane) insulin has historically been used but offers no advantage over modern analogues. Premixed insulin is not standard for T1DM basal-bolus regimens.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 48-year-old woman with primary hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease) is taking carbamazepine — wait, carbimazole 20 mg OD. She attends the pharmacy reporting a sore throat, mouth ulcers and fever that began yesterday. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise paracetamol and salt-water gargles, review in 48 hours
option_b: dispense additional carbimazole and counsel on infection avoidance
option_d: refer urgently to A&E/GP for an urgent full blood count to exclude agranulocytosis
option_e: reassure as these are common, non-serious side effects
correct_answer: refer urgently to A&E/GP for an urgent full blood count to exclude agranulocytosis
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA guidance: carbimazole carries a rare but serious risk of agranulocytosis (severe neutropenia), most often in the first 3 months of treatment. Patients must be counselled to seek urgent medical attention if they develop signs of infection (sore throat, fever, mouth ulcers, malaise), and to stop carbimazole pending an urgent FBC. Agranulocytosis can be fatal if missed. Similar warnings apply to propylthiouracil and clozapine.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man taking levothyroxine 100 micrograms OD is found to have his TSH suppressed (0.05 mU/L) at routine bloods. He is asymptomatic. His most recent ECG shows normal sinus rhythm. He has osteoporosis on alendronic acid. Why is over-replacement clinically concerning in this patient specifically?
option_b: it increases his risk of AF, osteoporosis-related fractures and CV events
option_d: it requires immediate switch to liothyronine
option_e: TSH suppression has no clinical significance
correct_answer: it increases his risk of AF, osteoporosis-related fractures and CV events
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG145: TSH suppression (over-replacement) increases the risk of atrial fibrillation (especially in older adults), osteoporosis and fracture (compounded by existing osteoporosis), and cardiovascular mortality. Particular caution is needed in older patients and those with osteoporosis. The dose should be reduced and TFTs rechecked in 6–8 weeks, aiming for TSH within the normal range (typically 0.4–4.0 mU/L).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 31-year-old man with newly diagnosed pulmonary tuberculosis presents to the pharmacy reporting his soft contact lenses have become discoloured. His medication includes ethambutol, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, pyridoxine, and rifampicin. Which medication is most likely causing this?
correct_answer: rifampicin
explanation: Per BNF rifampicin monograph: rifampicin causes orange-red discolouration of body fluids including urine, sweat, saliva and tears — which can permanently stain soft contact lenses. Patients should be counselled at initiation. Other TB drug ADRs: ethambutol → optic neuritis; isoniazid → peripheral neuropathy (mitigated by pyridoxine); pyrazinamide → hepatotoxicity and hyperuricaemia.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 49-year-old woman presents to A&E with dysuria, left-sided lower back pain, fever 38°C and nausea. She has rheumatoid arthritis (on methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly + folic acid 5 mg weekly), epilepsy (lamotrigine), osteoporosis (alendronic acid + colecalciferol), and depression (sertraline). She is diagnosed with pyelonephritis and prescribed cefalexin and paracetamol. Which medication is most appropriate to temporarily withhold during the acute infection?
correct_answer: methotrexate
explanation: Per BNF methotrexate monograph and BSR guidance: methotrexate is an immunosuppressant and should be temporarily withheld during acute infection due to increased risk of severe infection. Restart once infection resolved. Folic acid is continued. The other drugs do not need to be withheld.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A previously well 25-year-old woman presents to the GP with dysuria, frequency and urgency for 2 days. She has no fever, flank pain or vaginal symptoms. She is not pregnant and has NKDA. Per NICE NG109, which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
option_d: nitrofurantoin 100 mg modified-release BD for 3 days
correct_answer: nitrofurantoin 100 mg modified-release BD for 3 days
explanation: Per NICE NG109 (UTI - lower in non-pregnant women) and the UKHSA antimicrobial guidance: nitrofurantoin 100 mg m/r BD for 3 days is first-line for non-pregnant women with uncomplicated lower UTI. Trimethoprim 200 mg BD for 3 days is alternative first-line. Note: nitrofurantoin should be avoided if eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m². Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) are reserved due to MHRA warnings on serious, long-lasting adverse effects (tendon damage, neuropsychiatric). 7-day courses are reserved for complicated UTI or pyelonephritis.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks' gestation has been diagnosed with a lower UTI (E. coli sensitive on culture). Per NICE NG109 and BNF, which antibiotic is most appropriate?
option_c: nitrofurantoin 100 mg modified-release BD for 7 days
correct_answer: cefalexin 500 mg BD for 7 days
explanation: Per NICE NG109 and BNF: in pregnancy, cefalexin is the first-line antibiotic for lower UTI (7-day course). Nitrofurantoin is avoided AT TERM (last trimester) due to risk of neonatal haemolysis; trimethoprim is avoided in the FIRST trimester due to folate antagonist activity (teratogenic, neural tube defects). Tetracyclines (deposition in fetal bones/teeth) and fluoroquinolones (joint toxicity) are contraindicated in pregnancy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old woman with a chest infection (community-acquired pneumonia, CRB-65 score 1) presents to her GP. She has no penicillin allergy and is otherwise stable. Per NICE NG138, which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
correct_answer: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days
explanation: Per NICE NG138 (community-acquired pneumonia in adults): for low-severity CAP (CRB-65 score 0–1) in adults, amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days is first-line. Doxycycline 200 mg loading then 100 mg OD for 5 days is alternative in penicillin allergy. Severity assessment (CRB-65 in community, CURB-65 in hospital) guides treatment intensity. Fluoroquinolones are restricted due to MHRA warnings.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man presents with acute tonsillitis. His FeverPAIN score is 4. He has NKDA. Per NICE NG84, which is the most appropriate antibiotic?
correct_answer: phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg QDS for 5–10 days
explanation: Per NICE NG84 (sore throat): FeverPAIN score ≥4 (or Centor ≥3) indicates higher likelihood of bacterial cause (e.g. group A strep) and supports antibiotic prescribing. Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg QDS for 5–10 days is first-line. Amoxicillin should be AVOIDED if there's suspicion of glandular fever (EBV) — can cause a widespread rash. Clarithromycin is alternative in penicillin allergy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 5-year-old child with acute otitis media (perforated tympanic membrane with otorrhoea, fever, otalgia, no penicillin allergy) is brought to the pharmacy. Per NICE NG91, which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: no antibiotic; recommend ibuprofen and paracetamol
correct_answer: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days
explanation: Per NICE NG91 (acute otitis media): antibiotics are indicated in children with otorrhoea (discharging ear, suggesting perforation), systemic features (fever) or bilateral AOM in <2 year olds. Amoxicillin 5–7 days is first-line (dose adjusted for paediatric weight — adult dose used here for question simplicity but BNFc dose is 250 mg TDS for 1–4 years, 500 mg TDS for 5–17 years). Most uncomplicated AOM is managed with paracetamol/ibuprofen and watchful waiting.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman presents with thick green-yellow nasal discharge, facial pain over her cheeks, and a temperature of 38°C for 12 days. There has been no improvement with paracetamol and saline rinses. Per NICE NG79, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: continue supportive care for further 2 weeks
correct_answer: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days
explanation: Per NICE NG79 (acute sinusitis): symptoms lasting 10 days or more, with no improvement, support consideration of antibiotic treatment. Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days is first-line. Most acute sinusitis is viral and self-limiting (resolves in 7–10 days), and antibiotics are NOT indicated in the first 10 days. Intranasal corticosteroids can be considered for severe or persistent symptoms (off-label).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man with cellulitis of the left lower leg (defined area, no systemic features, no penicillin allergy) presents to the GP. Per NICE NG141, which is the most appropriate antibiotic?
option_d: flucloxacillin 500 mg–1 g QDS for 5–7 days
option_e: vancomycin orally 125 mg QDS for 10 days
correct_answer: flucloxacillin 500 mg–1 g QDS for 5–7 days
explanation: Per NICE NG141 (cellulitis): flucloxacillin is first-line for uncomplicated cellulitis (Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes coverage), typically 500 mg to 1 g QDS for 5–7 days. Alternatives in penicillin allergy: clarithromycin, erythromycin (pregnancy), or doxycycline. Co-amoxiclav is reserved for facial cellulitis or near eyes (broader cover). Severe cellulitis with systemic features requires hospital admission for IV antibiotics.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old man returned from a 2-week trip to sub-Saharan Africa 5 days ago. He presents to A&E with fever, headache, chills and myalgia. He took no malaria chemoprophylaxis. Which is the most appropriate immediate action?
option_a: await stool culture and treat as gastroenteritis
option_e: urgent investigation for malaria including thick and thin blood films
correct_answer: urgent investigation for malaria including thick and thin blood films
explanation: Per UK Health Security Agency guidance and BNF: any fever in a traveller returning from a malaria-endemic area within the last 12 months is malaria until proven otherwise. P. falciparum can rapidly become fatal. Urgent investigation includes thick and thin blood films (and/or rapid diagnostic test) on three separate occasions if initial test negative and suspicion remains high. Treatment depends on species and severity (e.g. artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated falciparum; IV artesunate for severe).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man has been diagnosed with acute hepatitis B in the GUM clinic following an exposure 6 weeks ago. His partner is HBV-negative. Which is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis for his partner?
option_b: hepatitis B immunoglobulin plus hepatitis B vaccine
correct_answer: hepatitis B immunoglobulin plus hepatitis B vaccine
explanation: Per UK Health Security Agency / Green Book guidance: for significant HBV exposure (sexual or needlestick) within 7 days, both passive (HBV immunoglobulin) and active (HBV vaccine, accelerated schedule 0, 1, 2 months with booster at 12 months) immunisation should be given. The combination provides immediate (immunoglobulin) and longer-term (vaccine) protection. The same approach is used for newborns of HBsAg+ mothers and certain needle-stick injuries.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 67-year-old man taking warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0, latest 2.4) is prescribed a 7-day course of clarithromycin for community-acquired pneumonia. Per BNF, which is the most appropriate action regarding his warfarin?
option_a: continue warfarin at the current dose and recheck INR after antibiotic course
option_b: increase warfarin dose by 25% to compensate
option_c: recheck INR within 3–5 days of starting clarithromycin and adjust dose accordingly
option_d: stop warfarin entirely during the antibiotic course
option_e: switch warfarin to a DOAC during the antibiotic course
correct_answer: recheck INR within 3–5 days of starting clarithromycin and adjust dose accordingly
explanation: Per BNF interactions: clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, increasing warfarin effect and INR — risk of bleeding. INR should be checked 3–5 days after starting (and after stopping) the antibiotic, and warfarin dose adjusted as needed. Alternative antibiotics with less interaction (e.g. doxycycline) may be considered. Empirical dose changes without monitoring are unsafe. Switching to a DOAC isn't a standard short-term solution.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old boy weighing 22 kg requires treatment for impetigo affecting his face. Per NICE NG153, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for localised non-bullous impetigo?
option_a: flucloxacillin 250 mg/5 mL 5 mL QDS PO for 5 days
option_b: fusidic acid 2% cream applied TDS for 5 days
option_c: hydrogen peroxide 1% cream applied 2–3 times daily for 5 days
correct_answer: hydrogen peroxide 1% cream applied 2–3 times daily for 5 days
explanation: Per NICE NG153 (impetigo) and updated antimicrobial guidance: hydrogen peroxide 1% cream is first-line for localised non-bullous impetigo to reduce antimicrobial resistance. Topical antibiotics (fusidic acid 2% cream or mupirocin 2% ointment) are second-line if H₂O₂ unsuitable or symptoms worsen. Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin) are reserved for widespread, bullous, or systemically unwell patients. This change reflects antimicrobial stewardship priorities.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old woman in a care home is diagnosed with Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI). She has had no prior episode. Per NICE NG199, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
option_e: vancomycin 125 mg QDS PO for 10 days
correct_answer: vancomycin 125 mg QDS PO for 10 days
explanation: Per NICE NG199 (C. difficile infection, 2021): the first-line treatment for first episode CDI is vancomycin 125 mg QDS PO for 10 days. Fidaxomicin is second-line (and first-line for recurrent CDI). Oral metronidazole is no longer recommended first-line. The offending antibiotic should also be stopped if possible. Hand hygiene with soap and water (alcohol gel is ineffective against spores) is essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
