question_text: A mother visits the pharmacy with her 6-year-old daughter who has an itchy bottom and small white thread-like worms in her faeces. The girl lives with her parents and younger brother. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: refer to GP for same-day assessment
option_d: supply oral mebendazole to treat the girl only
option_e: supply oral mebendazole to treat the whole family
correct_answer: supply oral mebendazole to treat the whole family
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS (threadworm): all household members must be treated simultaneously due to high transmissibility and asymptomatic carriage. Mebendazole 100 mg as a single dose (over 6 months age) is first-line, with a repeat dose at 2 weeks. Strict hygiene measures (handwashing, nail-cutting, washing bedlinen, separate towels) for 2 weeks are essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old woman presents to the pharmacy with classic features of vulvovaginal candidiasis: white curd-like discharge, itching, soreness. She has had similar episodes before. She is not pregnant and has no other medical conditions. Which is the most appropriate over-the-counter management?
option_a: clotrimazole 1% cream applied topically + oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
option_e: refer to GUM clinic for STI screen
correct_answer: clotrimazole 1% cream applied topically + oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS (vulvovaginal candidiasis): non-pregnant, non-recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis can be treated OTC with topical clotrimazole 1% (for vulval symptoms) plus either an intravaginal pessary (e.g. clotrimazole 500 mg single dose) or oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose. Fluconazole is contraindicated in pregnancy. Recurrent (>4/year) needs GP referral. Metronidazole is for bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis, not candida.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man with HIV is taking Stribild (elvitegravir/cobicistat/emtricitabine/tenofovir). His CD4 count is 580 and viral load undetectable. He requests rifampicin from a colleague who is being treated for tuberculosis. Which is the most likely concern?
option_a: rifampicin and Stribild can be safely co-administered
option_b: rifampicin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer that markedly reduces cobicistat-boosted antiretroviral levels — contraindicated
option_c: rifampicin causes haemolysis in HIV patients
option_e: rifampicin should be given orally with food
correct_answer: rifampicin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer that markedly reduces cobicistat-boosted antiretroviral levels — contraindicated
explanation: Per BNF and HIV Drug Interactions Database: rifampicin is a potent inducer of CYP3A4 and significantly reduces plasma levels of CYP3A4-metabolised drugs including cobicistat (the pharmacokinetic booster in Stribild), elvitegravir, and many protease inhibitors. Co-administration is contraindicated and would risk HIV treatment failure and resistance. Alternative regimens (e.g. rifabutin substitution, or different antiretroviral regimens) are used in patients with TB co-infection. Specialist input is essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pharmacy 3 days after starting amoxicillin for sore throat. He has now developed a widespread erythematous maculopapular rash all over his body. He has no facial swelling, breathing difficulty or hypotension. Which is the most likely explanation?
option_a: amoxicillin allergy — true type I hypersensitivity
option_b: glandular fever (EBV infection) with amoxicillin-induced rash
correct_answer: glandular fever (EBV infection) with amoxicillin-induced rash
explanation: Per BNF and clinical immunology: amoxicillin given to a patient with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection (glandular fever) commonly causes a widespread maculopapular rash. This is NOT a true penicillin allergy — it is a non-IgE-mediated reaction specific to the EBV state. The rash typically appears 7–10 days after starting amoxicillin. Once recovered, the patient can usually tolerate penicillins without recurrence. Important to document accurately to avoid mislabelling as 'penicillin allergic'. True IgE allergy typically presents within 1 hour with urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman is being initiated on rifampicin/isoniazid/pyrazinamide/ethambutol for active pulmonary TB. She is taking the combined oral contraceptive pill. Which is the most appropriate advice on contraception?
option_b: double the dose of the COC
option_c: rifampicin is a strong enzyme inducer — switch to a non-hormonal method or depot/IUD
option_e: use the patch instead of the pill
correct_answer: rifampicin is a strong enzyme inducer — switch to a non-hormonal method or depot/IUD
explanation: Per BNF, FSRH guidance and rifampicin SmPC: rifampicin is one of the most potent enzyme inducers in clinical use (induces CYP3A4, CYP2C9 and others) and significantly reduces the effectiveness of combined hormonal contraception, progestogen-only pills, transdermal patches, vaginal rings, and the contraceptive implant. Reliable methods unaffected by enzyme induction are: copper IUD, levonorgestrel IUS, and depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA). This applies for the duration of rifampicin therapy AND for 28 days after stopping.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 76-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with a 3-day history of worsening breathlessness, increased sputum volume, and cough with purulent sputum. She has COPD, depression and osteoporosis. Her current medication includes Anoro Ellipta (umeclidinium/vilanterol) OD, salbutamol PRN, citalopram and alendronic acid. Which is the most likely causative pathogen?
correct_answer: Haemophilus influenzae
explanation: Per NICE NG114 (acute exacerbation of COPD) and microbiology: the most common bacterial pathogens in infective COPD exacerbations are Haemophilus influenzae (most common), Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Moraxella catarrhalis. Pseudomonas is more relevant in advanced COPD with bronchiectasis or recent hospitalisation. The atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia) are less common.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A mother visits the pharmacy with her 6-year-old daughter who has an itchy bottom and small white thread-like worms in her faeces. The girl lives with her parents and younger brother. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: refer to GP for same-day assessment
option_d: supply oral mebendazole to treat the girl only
option_e: supply oral mebendazole to treat the whole family
correct_answer: supply oral mebendazole to treat the whole family
explanation: Per BNF/NICE CKS (threadworm): all household members must be treated simultaneously due to high transmissibility and asymptomatic carriage. Mebendazole 100 mg single dose (>6 months age) is first-line, with repeat at 2 weeks. Strict hygiene measures essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 56-year-old man presents with new-onset symptoms of GORD (heartburn, regurgitation) for 6 weeks. He has no alarm symptoms. He takes ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for chronic back pain. Per NICE CG184, which is the most appropriate initial management?
option_a: advise lifestyle changes, review ibuprofen necessity, and start a 4–8 week course of a full-dose PPI
correct_answer: advise lifestyle changes, review ibuprofen necessity, and start a 4–8 week course of a full-dose PPI
explanation: Per NICE CG184 (GORD): for new-onset uninvestigated dyspepsia without alarm symptoms, offer a 4-week trial of full-dose PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg OD). Concurrent NSAID use should be reviewed and stopped if possible. Lifestyle advice (weight loss, raising head of bed, avoiding triggers) should also be given. Endoscopy is reserved for alarm symptoms (dysphagia, GI bleed, weight loss, age >55 with new dyspepsia).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man is started on a 4-week course of omeprazole 20 mg OD for dyspepsia. After 1 week he develops severe watery diarrhoea (up to 10 episodes/day). He has not been on antibiotics. Which is the most likely concern?
option_a: Clostridioides difficile infection — PPIs are a known risk factor
correct_answer: Clostridioides difficile infection — PPIs are a known risk factor
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA Drug Safety Update: PPIs are associated with an increased risk of C. difficile infection (CDI) — gastric acid normally protects against ingested spores. While diarrhoea has multiple causes, the severity (10 episodes/day) and PPI use warrant urgent investigation: stool sample for C. difficile toxin, FBC, U&Es. Stop the PPI if CDI is confirmed (per NICE NG199) and treat with oral vancomycin first-line. The MHRA has issued warnings about long-term PPI complications including CDI, hypomagnesaemia and fractures.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man is taking lansoprazole 30 mg OD for confirmed H. pylori-negative GORD. After 6 months he asks about long-term safety. Per BNF and MHRA, which is a concern with long-term PPI therapy?
option_c: hypomagnesaemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, increased risk of fractures and CDI
option_e: reduced gastric acid is harmful only in pregnancy
correct_answer: hypomagnesaemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, increased risk of fractures and CDI
explanation: Per BNF PPI monographs and MHRA Drug Safety Updates: long-term PPI use is associated with hypomagnesaemia (with consequent hypokalaemia/hypocalcaemia), vitamin B12 deficiency, increased risk of osteoporotic fractures (especially hip), C. difficile infection, and possibly chronic kidney disease. PPI should be reviewed and used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest necessary duration; consider step-down to OD or PRN where possible.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman has dyspepsia with epigastric burning that is worse with eating. Her H. pylori test (stool antigen) is positive. She has no penicillin allergy. Per NICE CG184, which is the most appropriate H. pylori eradication regimen?
option_a: amoxicillin 1 g BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD + omeprazole 20 mg BD for 7 days
correct_answer: amoxicillin 1 g BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD + omeprazole 20 mg BD for 7 days
explanation: Per NICE CG184: first-line H. pylori eradication for patients not allergic to penicillin and not on clarithromycin recently is triple therapy: a PPI BD + amoxicillin 1 g BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD (or metronidazole 400 mg BD) for 7 days. In penicillin allergy: PPI + clarithromycin + metronidazole. Eradication should be confirmed (urea breath test or stool antigen) 6–8 weeks post-treatment if symptoms persist.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man takes warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) for atrial fibrillation. He presents to A&E with haematemesis and melaena. INR is 5.2. He is haemodynamically stable. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_c: give vitamin K 5 mg slow IV plus prothrombin complex concentrate IV; stop warfarin and consider endoscopy
option_e: switch warfarin to a DOAC immediately
correct_answer: give vitamin K 5 mg slow IV plus prothrombin complex concentrate IV; stop warfarin and consider endoscopy
explanation: Per BNF warfarin monograph and NICE NG196: for warfarin-related major bleeding (INR ≥5 plus significant bleeding e.g. haematemesis/melaena), the management is: stop warfarin, give vitamin K (phytomenadione) 5 mg IV slowly, AND give prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC, e.g. Beriplex/Octaplex) for immediate reversal. Endoscopy is needed for the source of bleeding. Protamine reverses heparin only. Fresh frozen plasma is no longer preferred over PCC.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman with active Crohn's disease has been newly initiated on azathioprine 2.5 mg/kg OD as steroid-sparing maintenance therapy. Per BNF and BSG guidance, which baseline test must be performed before initiation?
option_b: full blood count and liver function tests only
option_d: thiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) activity, FBC and LFTs
correct_answer: thiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) activity, FBC and LFTs
explanation: Per BNF azathioprine monograph and BSG guidance: TPMT enzyme activity should be measured before initiating azathioprine. Patients with absent or low TPMT activity are at high risk of severe (potentially fatal) myelosuppression due to impaired metabolism of 6-mercaptopurine (the active metabolite). Baseline FBC and LFTs are also required. Monitoring during therapy includes weekly FBC for the first month, then every 2 weeks, with LFTs.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman with newly diagnosed mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis (proctosigmoiditis) is being considered for treatment. Per NICE NG130, which is the most appropriate first-line induction therapy?
correct_answer: topical (rectal) mesalazine
explanation: Per NICE NG130 (ulcerative colitis): for mild to moderate proctitis or proctosigmoiditis (left-sided UC), first-line is topical (rectal) aminosalicylate (mesalazine enema, foam or suppository). If inadequate response within 4 weeks, add oral 5-ASA OR add topical corticosteroid. Oral steroids are reserved for more extensive disease or failure of 5-ASA. Biologics (infliximab, vedolizumab) and thiopurines (azathioprine) are reserved for moderate-to-severe disease unresponsive to conventional therapy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 80-year-old woman is taking metformin 1 g BD, ramipril 5 mg OD and simvastatin 20 mg OD. She presents with new constipation (no bowel motion for 6 days, bloating, mild abdominal discomfort, no nausea/vomiting). She has no red flag features. Which is the most appropriate first-line OTC management?
option_d: no laxative; recommend lifestyle changes only
option_e: senna 7.5–15 mg ON or ispaghula husk; review hydration and dietary fibre
correct_answer: senna 7.5–15 mg ON or ispaghula husk; review hydration and dietary fibre
explanation: Per BNF laxative chapter and NICE CKS (constipation): first-line for simple constipation is a bulk-forming laxative (e.g. ispaghula husk) if intake of dietary fibre and fluids cannot be increased, OR a stimulant laxative (e.g. senna) if rapid effect needed. Stool softeners (e.g. docusate) are second-line. Osmotic laxatives (macrogol/Movicol) are particularly useful when faecal impaction or hard stools. Bisacodyl is a stimulant but stronger; reserved for refractory cases. Loperamide is for diarrhoea, opposite indication. Codeine causes constipation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 24-year-old woman has been prescribed her first course of antibiotics (amoxicillin) for tonsillitis. She also takes the combined oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30). Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: additional contraception is needed for the duration of the antibiotic course and 7 days after
option_b: no additional contraception needed unless the antibiotic is rifampicin or another enzyme inducer
option_d: take the pill at a different time of day
option_e: take two pills per day during the antibiotic course
correct_answer: no additional contraception needed unless the antibiotic is rifampicin or another enzyme inducer
explanation: Per FSRH guidance and BNF: routine antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin, doxycycline) do NOT reduce the efficacy of combined hormonal contraception. Earlier advice to use additional contraception with all antibiotics has been withdrawn. The exception is enzyme-inducing antibiotics — rifampicin and rifabutin — which DO reduce contraceptive efficacy and require additional non-hormonal contraception. Also remember that severe diarrhoea or vomiting from any cause (including antibiotic-induced) reduces pill absorption.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman with chronic constipation is taking docusate sodium 200 mg BD and senna 15 mg ON without effect. She has been on these for 4 weeks. She has Parkinson's disease (on co-careldopa). Which is the most appropriate next-step laxative?
correct_answer: add a macrogol (e.g. Movicol)
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS (constipation): macrogol (polyethylene glycol, e.g. Movicol/Laxido) is an osmotic laxative effective for refractory constipation and faecal impaction. It is often added to stimulant/softener combinations. Co-careldopa is associated with constipation as Parkinson's disease itself causes slow GI transit. Liquid paraffin is generally avoided (aspiration risk, fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption). Codeine would worsen constipation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man with cirrhosis has developed hepatic encephalopathy with confusion and asterixis. He is in hospital. Per BNF and BSG guidance, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy?
option_c: lactulose 30–50 mL TDS (titrated to 2–3 soft stools per day)
correct_answer: lactulose 30–50 mL TDS (titrated to 2–3 soft stools per day)
explanation: Per BNF and BSG cirrhosis guidance: lactulose (a non-absorbable disaccharide) is first-line for hepatic encephalopathy. It works by lowering colonic pH (trapping ammonia as ammonium for excretion) and reducing gut bacterial production of ammonia. Dose titrated to achieve 2–3 soft stools per day. Rifaximin (a non-absorbed antibiotic) is added in recurrent or refractory cases. Lactulose enemas can be used if oral route compromised.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman with newly diagnosed irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-C; constipation predominant) has failed to respond to fibre and lifestyle measures. She has tried bulk-forming and osmotic laxatives without success. Per NICE CG61, which is the most appropriate next-step pharmacological option?
correct_answer: linaclotide 290 micrograms OD
explanation: Per NICE CG61 (irritable bowel syndrome) and BNF: linaclotide is licensed for moderate-to-severe IBS-C in adults who have not responded to optimal use of laxatives from different classes for at least 12 months. It is a guanylate cyclase-C agonist that increases intestinal fluid secretion. Loperamide is for IBS-D (diarrhoea-predominant). Mebeverine is an antispasmodic for IBS pain. Low-dose TCAs (amitriptyline) or SSRIs are options for IBS pain (not specifically IBS-C).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man with newly diagnosed coeliac disease asks about lifelong management. Which is the most important counselling point?
option_b: complete avoidance of gluten (wheat, barley, rye) is essential lifelong; oats may be tolerated in moderation
option_c: gluten can be reintroduced after 6 months on a gluten-free diet
option_d: take pancreatic enzyme supplements with all meals
correct_answer: complete avoidance of gluten (wheat, barley, rye) is essential lifelong; oats may be tolerated in moderation
explanation: Per NICE NG20 (coeliac disease) and BSG: lifelong strict avoidance of gluten (wheat, barley, rye) is the cornerstone of management. Pure oats are usually tolerated, but commercial oats are often cross-contaminated. Even small amounts of gluten can cause histological damage and increase risk of complications (osteoporosis, nutritional deficiencies, T-cell lymphoma). Patients should be referred to a dietitian. Annual review including FBC, ferritin, folate, B12, calcium and bone density screening is recommended.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old man taking ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for osteoarthritis is found to have melaena. He is haemodynamically stable. His Hb is 95 g/L (was 130 6 months ago). Which is the most appropriate immediate action?
option_a: continue ibuprofen and reassess in 4 weeks
option_b: refer to A&E for urgent endoscopy and to stop NSAID; commence PPI
option_c: reassure as melaena is a common side effect of ibuprofen
correct_answer: refer to A&E for urgent endoscopy and to stop NSAID; commence PPI
explanation: Per NICE NG12 (suspected cancer recognition) and NG141 (upper GI bleeding): melaena in an older patient on NSAID, with new anaemia, is an upper GI bleed until proven otherwise (likely NSAID-induced gastric or duodenal ulcer). Urgent A&E referral for endoscopy within 24 hours of presentation. NSAID should be stopped; IV PPI is given. Continued NSAID risks fatal haemorrhage. This patient also needs further investigation to exclude malignancy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old man returns from a 7-day trip to a region with poor sanitation with watery diarrhoea (6 episodes/day for 2 days) and mild abdominal cramps. He has no fever, no blood in stool, and remains well hydrated. Which is the most appropriate OTC management?
option_c: loperamide 4 mg initially then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/24h)
option_e: oral rehydration salts only; loperamide may shorten symptoms in adults without fever or blood
correct_answer: oral rehydration salts only; loperamide may shorten symptoms in adults without fever or blood
explanation: Per NICE CKS (gastroenteritis) and BNF: most acute infectious diarrhoea is self-limiting. First-line is oral rehydration with ORS. Loperamide can be used in adults to reduce symptom duration if there are NO red flags: no fever, no blood in stool, no severe abdominal pain (which suggest invasive bacterial or inflammatory cause where antimotility agents are harmful). Antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated for traveller's diarrhoea unless severe with fever/blood (in which case investigation and specialist input).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old woman is taking warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) for AF. She has constipation and her daughter buys her a herbal remedy containing St John's wort to 'help with mood and energy'. She has been taking this for 3 weeks. Her latest INR is 1.4 (was 2.5 last month). Which is the most likely explanation?
option_a: St John's wort displaces warfarin from plasma protein binding
option_b: St John's wort directly inhibits warfarin
option_c: St John's wort inhibits CYP2C9 and increases warfarin levels
option_d: St John's wort is a potent CYP3A4 inducer and reduces warfarin levels — risk of thromboembolism
option_e: vitamin K content of St John's wort is high
correct_answer: St John's wort is a potent CYP3A4 inducer and reduces warfarin levels — risk of thromboembolism
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix and MHRA Drug Safety Update: St John's wort (Hypericum perforatum) is a CYP3A4, CYP2C9 and P-glycoprotein inducer that significantly reduces plasma concentrations of many drugs including warfarin, oral contraceptives, ciclosporin, tacrolimus, antiretrovirals, and SSRIs (with risk of serotonin syndrome). The interaction with warfarin reduces anticoagulant effect — risk of clot/stroke in AF. Patient should stop St John's wort, INR should be rechecked, and warfarin dose adjusted as it re-rises (takes ~2 weeks for enzyme effect to wear off).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man with chronic alcohol-related liver disease has new ascites (mild, no encephalopathy). Per BNF and BSG, which is the most appropriate first-line diuretic management?
option_c: spironolactone 100 mg OD as monotherapy, adding furosemide if response is inadequate
correct_answer: spironolactone 100 mg OD as monotherapy, adding furosemide if response is inadequate
explanation: Per BNF and BSG cirrhosis guidance: for new ascites in liver disease, spironolactone (a potassium-sparing aldosterone antagonist) is first-line because ascites in cirrhosis is driven by secondary hyperaldosteronism. Start 100 mg OD, can titrate to 400 mg. Furosemide is added when response to spironolactone alone is inadequate (typical combination spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg). Salt restriction is also important. Refractory ascites may need paracentesis or TIPS.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman has been taking lansoprazole 30 mg OD for symptomatic GORD for 12 months with good response. She asks about stopping. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_b: do not stop — symptoms will return immediately
option_c: step down to lansoprazole 15 mg OD or take on-demand for 2–4 weeks; gradual reduction reduces rebound acid hypersecretion
correct_answer: step down to lansoprazole 15 mg OD or take on-demand for 2–4 weeks; gradual reduction reduces rebound acid hypersecretion
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CG184: long-term PPI users should be regularly reviewed and offered step-down to lowest effective dose, on-demand use, or trial of discontinuation. Abrupt PPI cessation can cause rebound acid hypersecretion (worsening symptoms for 2–4 weeks). Gradual step-down (e.g. reducing frequency, halving dose) or switching to H2-blocker prn during withdrawal reduces this. Lifestyle measures should be reinforced.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 7-year-old child has worsening diarrhoea (>10 episodes/day for 4 days), blood and mucus in stool, fever and severe abdominal cramps. The mother brings him to the pharmacy and asks for loperamide. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise oral rehydration salts and refer urgently to GP/A&E for assessment
correct_answer: advise oral rehydration salts and refer urgently to GP/A&E for assessment
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS guidance: bloody diarrhoea, high frequency, fever, severe pain and prolonged duration are red flags for invasive bacterial gastroenteritis (e.g. Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, E. coli O157), or inflammatory bowel disease. Loperamide is contraindicated in suspected invasive bacterial infection (delays toxin clearance, can precipitate toxic megacolon/HUS in E. coli O157). Children with severe acute diarrhoea need GP/A&E review for hydration assessment, stool culture, and possibly hospital admission.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with asthma. She has occasional daytime symptoms (about twice per week) but no nocturnal waking. She has no other medical conditions. Per the BTS/NICE/SIGN 2024 joint asthma guideline (NG245), which is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?
option_a: salbutamol 100 micrograms/dose inhaler 1–2 puffs as needed
option_b: beclometasone 200 micrograms/dose inhaler one puff BD
option_c: low-dose budesonide/formoterol combination inhaler as needed (AIR therapy)
option_d: low-dose budesonide/formoterol combination inhaler as MART (maintenance and reliever therapy)
correct_answer: low-dose budesonide/formoterol combination inhaler as needed (AIR therapy)
explanation: Per joint BTS/NICE/SIGN NG245 (November 2024): SABA monotherapy is no longer recommended. For newly diagnosed asthma in patients ≥12 years with mild symptoms (not highly symptomatic, no severe exacerbation), first-line is anti-inflammatory reliever (AIR) therapy: low-dose budesonide/formoterol used only when needed. Highly symptomatic patients or those with a recent severe exacerbation should start MART.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man on AIR therapy (as-needed low-dose ICS/formoterol) is still symptomatic (requiring his reliever 3–4 times per week). Per NG245, which is the most appropriate next step?
option_c: increase to MART (low-dose ICS/formoterol BD + PRN reliever doses of the same)
correct_answer: increase to MART (low-dose ICS/formoterol BD + PRN reliever doses of the same)
explanation: Per NG245: if symptoms remain inadequately controlled on AIR therapy, step up to MART (Maintenance And Reliever Therapy) — the patient takes low-dose ICS/formoterol as regular BD maintenance AND additional doses of the same inhaler as reliever. MART has stronger evidence of reducing exacerbations than the older 'ICS + separate SABA' approach. Montelukast is third-line. Theophylline is reserved for specialist use.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old woman is admitted with a 3-day history of worsening breathlessness, increased sputum volume, and cough with purulent sputum. She has COPD, depression, and osteoporosis. Her medication includes Anoro Ellipta (umeclidinium/vilanterol) OD, salbutamol PRN, citalopram, alendronic acid, prednisolone 30 mg OD for 5 days, and amoxicillin. Which is the most likely causative pathogen?
correct_answer: Haemophilus influenzae
explanation: Per NICE NG114: the most common bacterial pathogens in infective COPD exacerbations are H. influenzae (most common), Strep pneumoniae, and Moraxella catarrhalis. Pseudomonas is more relevant in advanced COPD with bronchiectasis or recent hospitalisation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 55% predicted, MRC dyspnoea 2) has 2 exacerbations in the past year (1 requiring hospitalisation). His current treatment is salbutamol PRN only. Per NICE NG115, which is the most appropriate next-step inhaler therapy?
correct_answer: LABA + LAMA + ICS triple therapy
explanation: Per NICE NG115 (COPD): patients with day-to-day symptoms or exacerbations DESPITE non-pharmacological measures, plus features of asthma/steroid responsiveness (raised eosinophils, previous asthma, FEV1 variation), should start LABA+ICS. Patients without features of steroid responsiveness should start LABA+LAMA. If exacerbations continue (≥1 hospitalisation, or ≥2 moderate exacerbations/year) on LABA+LAMA → escalate to LABA+LAMA+ICS triple therapy. This patient has recurrent exacerbations including hospitalisation, supporting triple therapy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man with asthma uses Symbicort 200/6 (budesonide/formoterol) one puff BD plus PRN. He attends the pharmacy reporting persistent oral thrush despite using a spacer. Which is the most appropriate counselling action?
option_a: advise rinsing mouth and gargling with water after each ICS dose
option_c: prescribe nystatin oral suspension to use indefinitely
option_e: take the inhaler on an empty stomach
correct_answer: advise rinsing mouth and gargling with water after each ICS dose
explanation: Per BNF and inhaler counselling guidance: oral candidiasis is a common ICS side effect from local oropharyngeal deposition. Prevention/management: rinse mouth and gargle with water after each dose (do not swallow); use a spacer (already in use here). Active candidiasis is treated with nystatin oral suspension for 7 days (not indefinitely). Stopping ICS would worsen asthma. Some patients benefit from switching to a different ICS or formulation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old child with asthma uses a beclometasone 100 micrograms/dose pMDI inhaler. Which is the most appropriate device for delivery?
option_c: spacer device (with or without face mask depending on age/coordination)
option_d: take a deep breath in straight from the pMDI
option_e: use the pMDI without any device
correct_answer: spacer device (with or without face mask depending on age/coordination)
explanation: Per BTS/NICE/SIGN paediatric guidance: children should use pMDI with a spacer device for all routine asthma inhaler administration. Children under 3 years typically need a spacer with face mask; older children with a mouthpiece. Spacers improve drug delivery to the lungs, reduce oropharyngeal deposition (and thrush risk), and overcome coordination difficulties. Dry powder inhalers can be considered from age 8+ if technique is adequate. Home nebulisers are not routine.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old woman with asthma is using salbutamol 100 micrograms/dose pMDI as her reliever (now switching to an ICS/formoterol combination as per NG245). She asks how to recognise that her asthma is poorly controlled. Which is the most useful indicator?
option_b: use of any ICS at all
option_c: using an inhaler with a spacer
option_d: using more than one puff per dose
option_e: using the inhaler in the morning
correct_answer: needing reliever (SABA) ≥3 times a week
explanation: Per BTS/NICE/SIGN guidance: indicators of poor asthma control include using SABA ≥3 times per week, night-time waking due to asthma, exacerbations needing oral steroids, or interference with daily activities. The Asthma Control Test or Royal College of Physicians 3 Questions can be used to assess this systematically. Note: per NG245 (2024), reliance on SABA is now itself a feature warranting step-up to inhaled steroid containing reliever therapy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man with severe acute asthma exacerbation has been given 5 mg nebulised salbutamol, 500 micrograms nebulised ipratropium, and 40 mg prednisolone PO. He remains tachypnoeic with SpO₂ 91%. PEF is 35% predicted. Which is the most appropriate next step per BTS/NICE/SIGN?
option_a: continue current management and review in 12 hours
option_b: give IV magnesium sulfate 1.2–2 g over 20 minutes (consider critical care input)
correct_answer: give IV magnesium sulfate 1.2–2 g over 20 minutes (consider critical care input)
explanation: Per BTS/NICE/SIGN acute asthma guidance: severe/life-threatening asthma not responding to initial bronchodilators and steroids warrants IV magnesium sulfate 1.2–2 g over 20 minutes (single dose) per SIGN/BTS. Continuous nebulised salbutamol may also be used. IV aminophylline is occasionally added with senior input. Critical care should be involved. Markers of life-threatening asthma include SpO₂ <92%, PEF <33%, silent chest, exhaustion, altered consciousness — this patient meets the severe/life-threatening criteria.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old man with COPD has been admitted with a severe infective exacerbation. He requires controlled oxygen therapy. Per BTS oxygen guidance, what is the target SpO₂ range for patients with COPD at risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure?
explanation: Per BTS Oxygen Guideline: for patients with COPD or other risk factors for hypercapnic respiratory failure (e.g. CF, neuromuscular disease, obesity hypoventilation), target SpO₂ 88–92%. Higher oxygen targets can worsen hypercapnia by reducing hypoxic respiratory drive and worsening V/Q mismatch (oxygen-induced hypercapnia). For most acutely unwell patients without hypercapnic risk, target SpO₂ 94–98%. Arterial blood gases should be checked early.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with mild asthma asks about using over-the-counter ibuprofen 400 mg for period pain. She has previously had a mild asthma exacerbation triggered by an NSAID. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: avoid NSAIDs entirely; recommend paracetamol or codeine if needed
option_b: it is safe to use ibuprofen alongside her inhaler
option_c: take ibuprofen with a PPI to prevent asthma flare
option_e: use ibuprofen at half the usual dose
correct_answer: avoid NSAIDs entirely; recommend paracetamol or codeine if needed
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA: in 'aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease' (AERD; also called Samter's triad — asthma, NSAID intolerance, nasal polyps), or any patient with a history of asthma exacerbation triggered by NSAIDs, all NSAIDs (including aspirin and ibuprofen) should be avoided. Paracetamol is safe. Reaction is due to cyclooxygenase inhibition shifting arachidonic acid metabolism to leukotrienes. Cross-reactivity occurs between all NSAIDs.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the pharmacy with a 'barking' cough, hoarse voice and stridor. She is otherwise well-looking, no respiratory distress, no drooling. She is afebrile. The mother thinks she 'choked on something' yesterday. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: advise the mother that this is viral croup; give one dose of oral dexamethasone if available, otherwise refer to GP same day
option_c: recommend a Heimlich manoeuvre at home
option_d: reassure as common cold and discharge
option_e: refer urgently to A&E for foreign body assessment and possible bronchoscopy
correct_answer: refer urgently to A&E for foreign body assessment and possible bronchoscopy
explanation: While the symptoms (barking cough, stridor) suggest croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), the history of possible choking ('choked on something yesterday') in a 5-year-old raises concern for foreign body aspiration, which requires urgent ENT/paediatric assessment for bronchoscopy. Foreign bodies can mimic croup and cause persistent symptoms. The 'classic' croup management (dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg PO single dose per NICE CKS) only applies once foreign body has been excluded.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman has just started using salmeterol (Serevent) 50 micrograms BD as add-on therapy for her asthma. She is also on beclometasone 200 micrograms BD. Per MHRA Drug Safety Update, what is the major safety concern with LABA monotherapy in asthma?
option_a: LABAs are safe as monotherapy in asthma
option_c: LABA monotherapy (without concurrent ICS) is associated with increased risk of severe asthma exacerbations and asthma-related death
correct_answer: LABA monotherapy (without concurrent ICS) is associated with increased risk of severe asthma exacerbations and asthma-related death
explanation: Per MHRA and BNF: LABAs (e.g. salmeterol, formoterol) must never be used as monotherapy in asthma — they must always be combined with an ICS. LABA monotherapy is associated with increased risk of severe asthma exacerbations and asthma-related death. Fixed-dose combinations (e.g. Symbicort, Seretide, Fostair) prevent inadvertent LABA monotherapy. This is a key MHRA safety message and a common exam point.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman who is 24 weeks pregnant has worsening asthma despite her ICS/LABA combination inhaler. Her GP wants to add a leukotriene receptor antagonist. Per MHRA and the BNF, which is the most appropriate option in pregnancy?
option_a: add montelukast — usually continued if she was already on it before pregnancy, but new initiation should weigh benefits vs risks (and counsel on neuropsychiatric risk)
option_d: no add-on options are safe in pregnancy
correct_answer: add montelukast — usually continued if she was already on it before pregnancy, but new initiation should weigh benefits vs risks (and counsel on neuropsychiatric risk)
explanation: Per BNF, UKTIS and MHRA Drug Safety Update (April 2024) on montelukast: montelukast is generally continued in pregnancy if benefits outweigh risks (poorly controlled asthma is itself a significant risk to mother and fetus). All patients (especially in pregnancy) must be counselled on the boxed warning for neuropsychiatric reactions (sleep disorders, hallucinations, anxiety, depression, suicidal thoughts) — discontinue if these occur. Inhaled corticosteroids, SABA, and LABA are all safe in pregnancy. Oral corticosteroids may be needed for severe exacerbations.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old woman has COPD on triple inhaler therapy (Trimbow — beclometasone/formoterol/glycopyrronium). She is using her inhaler 'morning and night' but symptoms are worsening. The pharmacist observes that she takes her inhaler while standing without using a spacer. She has mild hand tremor and difficulty actuating the pMDI. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise to use a dry powder inhaler instead
option_b: assess inhaler technique formally; consider spacer device or breath-actuated/DPI inhaler if technique cannot be optimised
correct_answer: assess inhaler technique formally; consider spacer device or breath-actuated/DPI inhaler if technique cannot be optimised
explanation: Per NICE NG115, BTS guidance and pharmacy practice: inhaler technique should be checked at every review, as poor technique is a major cause of treatment failure. Older patients with arthritis, hand tremor, or coordination problems may struggle with pMDIs; alternatives include breath-actuated inhalers, DPI, or pMDI + spacer (improves coordination). Inhaler review is a key role of community pharmacists (often delivered via New Medicines Service or structured medication reviews). Doubling the dose without optimising technique is inappropriate.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 9-year-old boy is brought to A&E with sudden onset wheeze, breathlessness, urticaria and lip swelling 10 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_a: adrenaline 1 in 1000 (1 mg/mL) 300 micrograms IM (0.3 mL) to the anterolateral thigh, IV fluids, high-flow oxygen, urgent A&E transfer
correct_answer: adrenaline 1 in 1000 (1 mg/mL) 300 micrograms IM (0.3 mL) to the anterolateral thigh, IV fluids, high-flow oxygen, urgent A&E transfer
explanation: Per Resuscitation Council UK anaphylaxis algorithm: IM adrenaline (anterolateral thigh) is first-line and life-saving in anaphylaxis. Adult dose 500 micrograms (0.5 mL of 1:1000); child 6–12 years 300 micrograms; under 6 years 150 micrograms. Repeat after 5 minutes if no response. IV fluids, oxygen, salbutamol nebs for bronchospasm. Adjunctive antihistamines and steroids no longer routinely recommended in immediate management per 2021 RCUK update — they have no role in initial life-saving treatment.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 24-year-old man has been prescribed an EpiPen (adrenaline auto-injector 300 micrograms) for severe peanut allergy. Which is the most important counselling point?
option_a: call 999 or go to A&E after using the auto-injector, even if symptoms improve
option_b: carry one device only; a second is unnecessary
option_c: the device can be used multiple times in a single emergency
option_d: take antihistamine before using the device
option_e: use only if symptoms persist for over 30 minutes
correct_answer: call 999 or go to A&E after using the auto-injector, even if symptoms improve
explanation: Per Resuscitation Council UK and MHRA guidance: patients prescribed adrenaline auto-injectors MUST call 999 immediately after using the device, even if symptoms improve, because: (1) biphasic reactions can occur hours later; (2) further doses may be needed. The MHRA recommends carrying TWO devices at all times. Counselling should also cover technique (anterolateral thigh, through clothing), expiry checks, and recognition of anaphylaxis. The patient should also have an anaphylaxis action plan.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with seasonal allergic rhinitis (sneezing, rhinorrhoea, nasal itch) seeks OTC advice. She has mild symptoms only and has not tried treatment. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment per BNF?
option_c: oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. loratadine or cetirizine) — or intranasal corticosteroid if symptoms moderate-severe
correct_answer: oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. loratadine or cetirizine) — or intranasal corticosteroid if symptoms moderate-severe
explanation: Per BNF and BSACI guideline (allergic rhinitis): mild intermittent symptoms — non-sedating oral antihistamine (loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) PRN. Moderate-persistent symptoms — regular intranasal corticosteroid (most effective for nasal congestion). Severe — combine. Sedating antihistamines (chlorphenamine) should be avoided due to sedation. Pseudoephedrine has limited evidence and significant cardiovascular side effects. Intranasal ipratropium is reserved for prominent rhinorrhoea.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man has been started on roflumilast 500 micrograms OD for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis. Which is the most likely common side effect to counsel him about?
option_d: sleep disturbance, weight loss, diarrhoea and nausea
correct_answer: sleep disturbance, weight loss, diarrhoea and nausea
explanation: Per BNF roflumilast monograph: roflumilast (a PDE4 inhibitor) is licensed for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations. Common side effects include weight loss (often significant), gastrointestinal effects (diarrhoea, nausea, decreased appetite), insomnia and headache. It can also cause psychiatric effects (depression, anxiety, suicidal thoughts — MHRA warning). Use with caution in patients with depression and counsel patients/families to recognise mental health changes.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man with stable COPD has been on triple inhaler therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) for 12 months. He has had no exacerbations in the past year and his eosinophil count is 0.1 × 10⁹/L (very low). Per NICE NG115 and the recent IMPACT/DESCOVID withdrawal trials, what should be considered?
option_b: continue triple therapy and add roflumilast
option_c: consider stepping down by withdrawing the ICS (back to LAMA + LABA), as ICS benefit is low and pneumonia risk is increased in low-eosinophil COPD
correct_answer: consider stepping down by withdrawing the ICS (back to LAMA + LABA), as ICS benefit is low and pneumonia risk is increased in low-eosinophil COPD
explanation: Per NICE NG115 (updated) and PCRS guidance: in stable COPD without recent exacerbations, particularly with low blood eosinophils (<0.1 or <0.3 × 10⁹/L depending on guideline), ICS withdrawal can be considered. ICS in COPD increases pneumonia risk and benefit is concentrated in patients with eosinophilic inflammation. Stepping down to LAMA + LABA (dual bronchodilation) with monitoring for exacerbations is appropriate. Patient should be advised to seek review if symptoms worsen.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old non-smoker presents with acute pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness on exertion for 24 hours. He is tall and thin. Chest X-ray shows a 30% right-sided spontaneous pneumothorax. He is haemodynamically stable. Per BTS pleural disease guidelines (2023), which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: conservative management (observation) with monitoring; needle aspiration if symptoms worsen
correct_answer: conservative management (observation) with monitoring; needle aspiration if symptoms worsen
explanation: Per BTS pleural disease guideline 2023 (significant change from older 2010 algorithm): symptom-led approach to primary spontaneous pneumothorax. If patient is stable and not significantly breathless, conservative management (observation, outpatient follow-up) is preferred — even for larger pneumothoraces — based on the PSP RCT (Brown 2020, NEJM). If significantly symptomatic, options are needle aspiration, ambulatory device, or chest drain. The older 'size-based' decision tree is no longer the primary approach.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man presents with a hot, swollen, painful big toe joint. He has stable angina and hypertension. He takes aspirin 75 mg OD, atenolol 100 mg OD, ezetimibe 10 mg OD, GTN spray PRN, ramipril 10 mg OD. Which is the most appropriate initial management of this acute presentation?
correct_answer: colchicine 500 micrograms BD–QDS
explanation: Per NICE CKS gout: first-line acute gout treatment is NSAID OR colchicine. In this patient, NSAIDs are best avoided due to CV disease (stable angina) and concurrent ACEi (ramipril) — risk of CV events, reduced ACEi efficacy and AKI. Colchicine is preferred. Allopurinol and febuxostat are urate-lowering therapies and must NOT be started during an acute attack (can prolong/worsen). They can be considered for prevention after the acute episode resolves.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man with frequent attacks of gout (4 in the past year) and serum urate of 480 micromol/L has just completed treatment of an acute flare. He has no CKD. He is ready to start urate-lowering therapy. Per NICE NG219, which is the most appropriate initial choice and prophylaxis?
option_a: allopurinol 100 mg OD, increase gradually with colchicine 500 micrograms BD prophylaxis for 6 months
option_b: allopurinol 600 mg OD as a single starting dose
correct_answer: allopurinol 100 mg OD, increase gradually with colchicine 500 micrograms BD prophylaxis for 6 months
explanation: Per NICE NG219 (gout) and BNF: urate-lowering therapy (ULT) is recommended for patients with recurrent gout (≥2 flares/year), tophi, joint damage, or renal impairment. First-line is allopurinol, start 100 mg OD (50 mg in CKD), titrate up every 2–4 weeks aiming for serum urate <360 micromol/L (<300 if tophi). Colchicine 500 mcg BD prophylaxis is given for up to 6 months when initiating/titrating ULT to prevent flares precipitated by urate mobilisation. NSAIDs are an alternative prophylactic option.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman with osteoporosis has been started on alendronic acid 70 mg once weekly. Which is the most important counselling point?
option_a: take in the morning, on an empty stomach, with a full glass of plain water, and remain upright for at least 30 minutes after taking
option_b: take with a milky breakfast for better absorption
option_c: take at bedtime with a heavy meal
option_d: take with calcium supplement at the same time
option_e: take with a hot drink to aid absorption
correct_answer: take in the morning, on an empty stomach, with a full glass of plain water, and remain upright for at least 30 minutes after taking
explanation: Per BNF alendronic acid monograph and SmPC: bisphosphonates must be taken in the morning, on an empty stomach, with a FULL glass of PLAIN water (not mineral water, milk, juice, tea or coffee), at least 30 minutes before breakfast and any other medication. The patient must remain upright (sitting or standing) for 30 minutes after to prevent oesophageal irritation/ulceration. Calcium-containing products significantly reduce absorption. Annual review of need is recommended.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old woman has been taking alendronic acid 70 mg once weekly for 6 years for osteoporosis. She attends for review. Her DEXA shows stable bone density. She has no fractures. Per NICE and NOGG guidance, what is the most appropriate next step?
option_b: double the dose to 140 mg weekly
option_c: review the need for continued treatment ('drug holiday' may be considered after 5–10 years depending on fracture risk)
correct_answer: review the need for continued treatment ('drug holiday' may be considered after 5–10 years depending on fracture risk)
explanation: Per NOGG (National Osteoporosis Guideline Group) and NICE: oral bisphosphonates should be reviewed after 5 years (alendronate, risedronate) or 3 years (zoledronic acid). For patients at lower ongoing risk, a treatment 'holiday' (1–2 years off) can be considered, with periodic DEXA review. Higher-risk patients continue. Bisphosphonates have long bone retention and protective effects persist for some time after discontinuation. Long-term use is associated with rare adverse effects including atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old woman with newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis is being started on methotrexate 10 mg PO once weekly plus folic acid 5 mg PO once weekly (different day). Per the MHRA Drug Safety Update on methotrexate dosing errors, what is the most important counselling point?
option_a: methotrexate is taken ONCE WEEKLY, NOT daily — taking daily can be fatal
option_b: take methotrexate at exactly the same time every day
option_c: take methotrexate after a heavy meal
option_e: two doses can be taken if one is missed
correct_answer: methotrexate is taken ONCE WEEKLY, NOT daily — taking daily can be fatal
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update (multiple, most recent 2020): weekly methotrexate dosing is a recurrent source of fatal medication errors. Patients have died from inadvertent daily dosing of methotrexate. The pharmacist must explicitly counsel: (1) methotrexate is ONCE WEEKLY — never daily; (2) take on the same day each week; (3) folic acid is on a different day (typically 24h after methotrexate or every day except methotrexate day); (4) if a dose is missed, take when remembered if within 1–2 days, otherwise skip. Yellow boxes and patient cards are now standard.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly + folic acid develops a productive cough and breathlessness. CXR shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: methotrexate-induced pneumonitis (uncommon but serious ADR)
explanation: Per BNF methotrexate monograph and MHRA: methotrexate-induced pneumonitis is an uncommon but potentially fatal adverse effect. Onset can be acute or insidious. Patients must be counselled to report new dyspnoea, dry cough or fever. CXR shows bilateral infiltrates. Methotrexate must be stopped immediately, the patient referred urgently, and high-dose corticosteroids initiated. Differential includes infection (immunosuppression risk) and PCP pneumonia — investigation excludes both.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman is taking methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly for psoriasis. She and her partner are planning pregnancy. Per BNF, when can she stop methotrexate prior to conception?
option_a: methotrexate is safe in pregnancy and can be continued
option_b: methotrexate should be stopped at least 3 months before attempting conception (for both male and female partners)
option_c: methotrexate should be stopped on the day she finds out she is pregnant
option_d: methotrexate should be stopped 1 week before conception
option_e: no need to stop methotrexate; just add 5 mg folic acid daily
correct_answer: methotrexate should be stopped at least 3 months before attempting conception (for both male and female partners)
explanation: Per BNF and BSR guidance: methotrexate is highly teratogenic (causes major malformations) and an abortifacient. Both men and women should stop methotrexate at least 3 months before attempting conception, to allow drug elimination and recovery of gametogenesis. Effective contraception is mandatory during treatment. Alternative DMARDs compatible with pregnancy (e.g. sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine) can be substituted. 5 mg folic acid weekly is given alongside methotrexate to reduce side effects.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman has been diagnosed with mild rheumatoid arthritis (active in two joints). Per NICE NG100, which is the most appropriate first-line DMARD?
option_c: methotrexate (or, if contraindicated, leflunomide or sulfasalazine)
correct_answer: methotrexate (or, if contraindicated, leflunomide or sulfasalazine)
explanation: Per NICE NG100 (rheumatoid arthritis in adults): first-line conventional synthetic DMARD (csDMARD) monotherapy is methotrexate, with alternatives being leflunomide or sulfasalazine if methotrexate is contraindicated. Hydroxychloroquine is reserved for mild/palindromic disease. Biologics (TNF-α inhibitors, etc.) are second-line after csDMARDs fail. Short-term low-dose corticosteroids are used as 'bridging' therapy to control symptoms while DMARDs take effect (typically 8–12 weeks).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman with knee osteoarthritis is taking paracetamol 1 g QDS with limited benefit. She has no comorbidities. Per NICE NG226 (2022 update), which is the most appropriate next step?
option_c: offer a topical NSAID (e.g. ibuprofen gel) and review oral NSAID with PPI cover if needed
correct_answer: offer a topical NSAID (e.g. ibuprofen gel) and review oral NSAID with PPI cover if needed
explanation: Per NICE NG226 (osteoarthritis, 2022 update): there is significant change from previous guidance. The strongest recommendation is for exercise and weight management. Pharmacological options should be considered alongside non-pharmacological. Topical NSAIDs are recommended in preference to oral NSAIDs where the affected joint is suitable (e.g. knee, hand). Oral NSAIDs are second-line, with PPI cover (especially in older patients). Paracetamol and opioids are no longer routinely recommended for OA pain due to limited efficacy and side effect profile. Intra-articular steroid injections may be offered.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 76-year-old man with osteoarthritis of the hip has chronic pain inadequately controlled by paracetamol and topical NSAIDs. He has hypertension and CKD (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m²) and takes ramipril 5 mg OD and amlodipine 5 mg OD. Per NICE NG226, what is the most appropriate next step?
option_b: add oral ibuprofen 400 mg TDS with PPI cover
option_c: add oral naproxen 500 mg BD with PPI cover
option_d: avoid oral NSAIDs; consider intra-articular steroid injection or refer for joint replacement assessment
correct_answer: avoid oral NSAIDs; consider intra-articular steroid injection or refer for joint replacement assessment
explanation: Per NICE NG226 and CKS: oral NSAIDs should be avoided in patients with significant CKD (eGFR <30, used cautiously 30–60), hypertension, heart failure, or those on ACEi/ARB + diuretic. The 'triple whammy' (ACEi/ARB + diuretic + NSAID) significantly increases AKI risk. In this patient (CKD eGFR 38 + ACEi), oral NSAIDs are best avoided. Alternative options include intra-articular steroid injection for short-term relief, and referral for joint replacement assessment if symptoms warrant.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 58-year-old man with severe back pain has been taking codeine phosphate 30 mg QDS for 4 weeks. He attends the pharmacy reporting persistent constipation despite using docusate. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: add bisacodyl 10 mg PRN, ensure adequate fluid and fibre, and review codeine necessity (NICE NG193 recommends against long-term opioids for chronic primary pain)
option_b: add codeine to the laxative regimen
correct_answer: add bisacodyl 10 mg PRN, ensure adequate fluid and fibre, and review codeine necessity (NICE NG193 recommends against long-term opioids for chronic primary pain)
explanation: Per BNF: opioid-induced constipation is common and predictable — patients on opioids should have a stimulant laxative (e.g. senna or bisacodyl) co-prescribed; bulk-forming laxatives (ispaghula) are less effective and can worsen constipation if dehydrated. The pharmacist should also flag that NICE NG193 advises against long-term opioids for chronic primary pain — codeine should be reviewed and ideally tapered. Naloxegol (peripheral opioid antagonist) is an option for refractory opioid-induced constipation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old man presents with severe pain and swelling in his right knee that started 6 hours ago. He has no history of trauma but recently returned from a hiking holiday. He has a tick bite on his thigh from 2 weeks ago that has developed a target (bull's-eye) rash. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_d: prescribe oral doxycycline 100 mg BD for 21 days for Lyme disease
option_e: refer to A&E for urgent assessment of septic arthritis
correct_answer: prescribe oral doxycycline 100 mg BD for 21 days for Lyme disease
explanation: Per NICE NG95 (Lyme disease): erythema migrans (bull's-eye rash) at the site of a tick bite is pathognomonic for Lyme disease. Oral doxycycline 100 mg BD for 21 days is first-line for adults and children >12 (amoxicillin if doxycycline contraindicated, e.g. pregnancy/under 12). Lyme arthritis (large joint, knee most common, monoarticular) is a manifestation of disseminated disease and is treated with 28 days of doxycycline. Tick-borne arthritis differs from septic arthritis in presentation but specialist input may be warranted.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman with severe rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate + hydroxychloroquine + sulfasalazine has inadequately controlled disease. Per NICE NG100 and TA375, which is the most appropriate next step?
option_b: consider a biological DMARD (e.g. TNF-α inhibitor) per NICE TAs
option_c: double the methotrexate dose to 30 mg weekly
correct_answer: consider a biological DMARD (e.g. TNF-α inhibitor) per NICE TAs
explanation: Per NICE NG100 and Technology Appraisals (TA375, TA130 etc.): patients with severe active RA (DAS28 >5.1) who have had an inadequate response to combination conventional synthetic DMARDs are eligible for biological DMARD therapy (TNF-α inhibitors — adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab; or alternatives like rituximab, abatacept, tocilizumab, JAK inhibitors). Biologics are continued only if a good response is achieved at 6 months (DAS28 reduction ≥1.2). Screen for TB, hepatitis B/C, and HIV before biologic initiation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old woman has bone density T-score of -2.6 (osteoporosis) and a fragility fracture of the wrist. She has been initiated on alendronic acid 70 mg weekly. Per NICE NG157, which adjunctive measures should be ensured?
option_b: adequate dietary calcium intake (or supplement) and vitamin D 800–1000 units OD
correct_answer: adequate dietary calcium intake (or supplement) and vitamin D 800–1000 units OD
explanation: Per NICE NG157 and NOGG: in patients on osteoporosis treatment, adequate calcium (700–1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800–1000 units/day) intake should be ensured — supplemented if dietary intake is insufficient. Bisphosphonates work less well in vitamin D deficiency. Other measures: smoking cessation, alcohol reduction, weight-bearing exercise, falls assessment. HRT is reserved for younger postmenopausal women with significant menopausal symptoms (not just osteoporosis), with care due to CV/breast cancer risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man has acute lower back pain after lifting a heavy box. No red flags. Per NICE NG59, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: bed rest for at least 1 week
option_b: encourage normal activity, exercise, manual therapy, and NSAIDs if needed at lowest effective dose for shortest duration
correct_answer: encourage normal activity, exercise, manual therapy, and NSAIDs if needed at lowest effective dose for shortest duration
explanation: Per NICE NG59 (low back pain and sciatica in over 16s): encourage self-management including continued normal activities and exercise. NSAIDs are first-line pharmacological treatment at the lowest effective dose for shortest time (with PPI in older patients). Paracetamol monotherapy is no longer recommended. Manual therapy (spinal manipulation, mobilisation, soft tissue) can be considered as part of a treatment package. Opioids should generally be avoided. Bed rest is harmful. Routine imaging is not indicated without red flags or persistent symptoms.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman has had ongoing back pain for 4 weeks following a fall. She reports sudden onset bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, and new urinary incontinence today. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_b: call 999 / immediate A&E referral as this presentation suggests cauda equina syndrome
option_e: refer for outpatient MRI in 6 weeks
correct_answer: call 999 / immediate A&E referral as this presentation suggests cauda equina syndrome
explanation: Per NICE NG59 and CKS: cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring same-day MRI and neurosurgical assessment. Red flags include: bilateral leg weakness/sciatica, saddle anaesthesia (numbness in perianal/perineal area), bladder or bowel dysfunction (urinary retention or new incontinence, faecal incontinence), and severe/progressive neurological deficit. Delay can result in permanent paralysis and incontinence. Other 'red flags' for back pain that require urgent assessment: history of cancer, fever/IV drug use (infection), significant trauma, age <20 or >55 with new onset.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man with chronic ankylosing spondylitis on adalimumab (anti-TNF) attends the pharmacy reporting a 3-week history of dry cough, night sweats and weight loss. Which is the most likely concern?
option_a: anti-TNF therapy increases the risk of TB reactivation; refer urgently for assessment
option_b: common viral infection, no action needed
correct_answer: anti-TNF therapy increases the risk of TB reactivation; refer urgently for assessment
explanation: Per BNF and rheumatology guidance: TNF-α inhibitors significantly increase the risk of TB reactivation (and other intracellular infections — listeria, salmonella, fungal). All patients are screened for latent TB before initiation (chest X-ray, IGRA test). New onset of cough, night sweats, weight loss in a patient on anti-TNF therapy should prompt urgent assessment for active TB. Other concerns include increased risk of skin cancer, serious infections, lymphoma (rare).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman with severe acne is being initiated on isotretinoin 30 mg OD by a dermatologist. She is sexually active. Per MHRA and the Pregnancy Prevention Programme for isotretinoin, what is the most important counselling point?
option_b: isotretinoin must NOT be taken in pregnancy (highly teratogenic); two reliable forms of contraception are required from 1 month before, throughout, and for 1 month after treatment
correct_answer: isotretinoin must NOT be taken in pregnancy (highly teratogenic); two reliable forms of contraception are required from 1 month before, throughout, and for 1 month after treatment
explanation: Per MHRA and SmPC: isotretinoin is highly teratogenic (major birth defects in ~25% of exposed pregnancies). The UK Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) requires: monthly pregnancy testing before each prescription, two reliable forms of contraception (one primary e.g. COCP/IUD/IUS + one barrier) from 1 month before initiation, during treatment, and for 1 month after. Patients sign acknowledgement of risks. MHRA also requires monitoring for psychiatric reactions (mood changes, depression, suicidal ideation) and sexual dysfunction — discontinue if these occur (April 2023 update).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old woman with active rheumatoid arthritis is taking methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly + folic acid 5 mg PO weekly (different day). She develops a chest infection and is prescribed trimethoprim 200 mg BD by her GP. What is the most important concern?
option_c: trimethoprim potentiates methotrexate-induced bone marrow suppression — risk of severe pancytopenia and potentially fatal myelosuppression
correct_answer: trimethoprim potentiates methotrexate-induced bone marrow suppression — risk of severe pancytopenia and potentially fatal myelosuppression
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix and MHRA: methotrexate and trimethoprim (or co-trimoxazole) BOTH have anti-folate effects. Co-administration significantly increases the risk of pancytopenia, megaloblastic anaemia and bone marrow failure, which can be FATAL. This combination should be avoided. The pharmacist must contact the prescriber to choose an alternative antibiotic (e.g. doxycycline or nitrofurantoin depending on indication and renal function).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man taking long-term low-dose oral corticosteroid (prednisolone 7.5 mg OD) for polymyalgia rheumatica attends the pharmacy. Per BNF, what additional medication should the GP consider for him to prevent steroid-induced osteoporosis?
option_b: bisphosphonate (e.g. alendronic acid 70 mg weekly) with calcium and vitamin D
correct_answer: bisphosphonate (e.g. alendronic acid 70 mg weekly) with calcium and vitamin D
explanation: Per NICE NG157, NOGG and BNF: patients on long-term oral corticosteroids (prednisolone ≥7.5 mg/day, or equivalent, for ≥3 months) are at high risk of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. Co-prescription of a bisphosphonate (alendronic acid or risedronate) plus calcium and vitamin D is recommended for older patients (≥70) without DEXA, or for younger patients based on DEXA + FRAX/QFracture risk. Other measures: smoking cessation, alcohol reduction, weight-bearing exercise. Lifestyle assessment of falls risk is also key.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A father brings his 6-year-old son into the pharmacy. The child has had a cold for 2 days and now complains of mild headache, general aches and tiredness. This morning he has developed an itchy rash on his face and body — small fluid-filled vesicles on a red base, in different stages (some vesicles, some crusted). NKDA, no regular medication, no other conditions. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: refer to A&E for urgent assessment
option_b: refer to the GP practice for same-day antiviral treatment
option_c: supply chlorphenamine 1 mg/5 mL oral solution and advise on supportive care
correct_answer: supply chlorphenamine 1 mg/5 mL oral solution and advise on supportive care
explanation: Per NICE CKS (chickenpox) and Green Book: classic chickenpox (varicella) presentation — prodromal mild systemic symptoms followed by itchy vesicular rash with lesions in different stages. In immunocompetent children, chickenpox is self-limiting: paracetamol for fever (AVOID ibuprofen — increased risk of severe skin/soft tissue infection), oral antihistamine (sedating chlorphenamine licensed from 1 year) for itch, calamine for symptomatic relief, fluids. Same-day GP/A&E referral reserved for immunocompromised, neonates, pregnant contacts, or signs of serious complication.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old woman with moderate facial acne (inflammatory lesions on cheeks and chin, no scarring) has been using benzoyl peroxide 5% gel for 8 weeks with minimal improvement. She is not pregnant. Per NICE NG198, which is the most appropriate next step?
option_b: oral lymecycline 408 mg OD + topical benzoyl peroxide for 12 weeks
correct_answer: oral lymecycline 408 mg OD + topical benzoyl peroxide for 12 weeks
explanation: Per NICE NG198 (acne vulgaris, 2021): for moderate inflammatory acne, options include fixed combinations of topical retinoid + benzoyl peroxide, topical antibiotic + benzoyl peroxide, or oral tetracycline (lymecycline, doxycycline) + topical adapalene or benzoyl peroxide (NEVER oral antibiotic alone — to prevent resistance). Oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe acne or where conventional therapy has failed (specialist initiated). Azithromycin only if tetracyclines contraindicated (e.g. pregnancy, age <12). Treatment review at 12 weeks.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman is started on isotretinoin by a dermatologist for severe nodulocystic acne. She is on the Pregnancy Prevention Programme. Per MHRA, which additional adverse effects must be discussed at initiation?
option_b: none, isotretinoin has no significant ADRs
option_c: psychiatric reactions (mood changes, depression, suicidal ideation) and sexual dysfunction; discontinue if these occur
correct_answer: psychiatric reactions (mood changes, depression, suicidal ideation) and sexual dysfunction; discontinue if these occur
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update April 2023: isotretinoin is associated with risks of psychiatric reactions (depression, anxiety, mood changes, suicidal ideation) and sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, vulvovaginal dryness) — sometimes persisting after discontinuation. Patients (and family) must be counselled on these risks at initiation. Discontinue if symptoms develop. New requirements introduced 2023: two prescribers must agree initiation for under-18s, with monthly monitoring of mental health.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with mild-to-moderate plaque psoriasis on her elbows and knees presents to the pharmacy. She has tried emollients alone. Per NICE CG153, which is the most appropriate next-line topical treatment?
option_b: potent topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone valerate 0.1%) OD + vitamin D analogue (e.g. calcipotriol) OD applied at different times
correct_answer: potent topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone valerate 0.1%) OD + vitamin D analogue (e.g. calcipotriol) OD applied at different times
explanation: Per NICE CG153 (psoriasis): for plaque psoriasis on trunk/limbs, first-line is a potent topical corticosteroid (OD) + a vitamin D analogue (OD), used at different times of day, for up to 4 weeks. Once-daily combination products (e.g. Dovobet — calcipotriol with betamethasone) are also available. Topical coal tar and dithranol are alternatives. Phototherapy and systemic therapy (methotrexate, ciclosporin, biologics) are reserved for more severe disease unresponsive to topicals.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-month-old infant has moderate atopic eczema affecting her cheeks. The mother seeks OTC advice. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: frequent emollient use (2–3 times daily and at bathtime) and hydrocortisone 1% cream OD–BD to inflamed areas, with referral to GP if not improving
correct_answer: frequent emollient use (2–3 times daily and at bathtime) and hydrocortisone 1% cream OD–BD to inflamed areas, with referral to GP if not improving
explanation: Per NICE NG198 atopic eczema in under 12s: foundation of treatment is frequent emollient use (at least 2–3 times daily and at bathtime, even when skin is clear). For facial use in infants, use ONLY mild topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%, OD–BD, short course of 3–5 days) — moderate/potent steroids are contraindicated on facial skin in young children due to skin atrophy. Steroid choice depends on age, site, severity. Severe eczema may need GP/specialist for topical immunomodulators (tacrolimus) or systemic therapy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old farmer presents with a small (8 mm) pearly papule with central ulceration on his nose that has been growing slowly over 18 months. There are visible blood vessels (telangiectasia) on the surface. He has significant sun exposure history. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: basal cell carcinoma (BCC)
explanation: Per NICE NG12 (suspected cancer recognition) and dermatology guidance: pearly papule with central ulceration ('rodent ulcer'), telangiectasia, slow growth on sun-exposed skin in older patient is classic for nodular basal cell carcinoma — the most common skin cancer. Rarely metastasises but can cause significant local destruction. Should be referred to dermatology under non-urgent suspected cancer pathway (or 2-week wait if suspected SCC or melanoma). Treatment usually excisional surgery, Mohs micrographic surgery for high-risk sites, or topical/photodynamic therapy for superficial lesions.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 42-year-old woman notices a mole on her back that has changed over the past 3 months: it has grown larger, become asymmetric, and has irregular borders with multiple colours. Per NICE NG12, which is the most appropriate action?
option_d: refer urgently (2-week wait) under the suspected skin cancer pathway
correct_answer: refer urgently (2-week wait) under the suspected skin cancer pathway
explanation: Per NICE NG12 (suspected cancer): a melanocytic lesion with features of malignant melanoma — using the 'ABCDE' criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution/change) — should be referred urgently under the 2-week wait pathway. Weighted '7-point checklist' is also used. Early diagnosis is critical as melanoma is highly aggressive but curable when caught early. Pharmacists should know skin cancer red flags and refer appropriately.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 26-year-old woman has had a small, red, itchy patch on her elbow for 3 weeks. It is well-demarcated with silvery scales. She has no other medical conditions and no family history of skin disease. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: plaque psoriasis
explanation: The description (well-demarcated red plaque with silvery-white scale, classic site — extensor surfaces such as elbows, knees, scalp, lower back) is characteristic of plaque psoriasis (chronic plaque psoriasis is the most common type). Other clinical features include the Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale removed) and Koebner phenomenon (new lesions at sites of trauma). Eczema is poorly demarcated and usually involves flexures. Differentiation can be challenging — refer if unclear.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man has scaly, itchy patches between his toes that have spread to the soles of both feet over 4 weeks. The skin is white, macerated and itchy. Which is the most appropriate OTC management?
option_c: topical antifungal (e.g. clotrimazole 1% cream or terbinafine 1% cream) applied BD for 1–4 weeks
correct_answer: topical antifungal (e.g. clotrimazole 1% cream or terbinafine 1% cream) applied BD for 1–4 weeks
explanation: Per BNF and CKS: this is classical tinea pedis (athlete's foot) — a dermatophyte infection. Topical antifungals are first-line: clotrimazole or miconazole (imidazoles, BD for 4–6 weeks), or terbinafine 1% cream (allylamine, BD for 1–2 weeks — usually quicker). Counselling: keep feet dry, change socks daily, wear breathable footwear, avoid sharing towels. Severe/extensive disease, nail involvement (onychomycosis) or treatment failure needs oral terbinafine prescription.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
