question_text: A 32-year-old woman has a fungal nail infection (onychomycosis) affecting two toenails confirmed on nail clippings (T. rubrum). She is not pregnant. Per BNF and BAD guidance, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: oral fluconazole 50 mg OD for 7 days
option_c: oral terbinafine 250 mg OD for 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails)
correct_answer: oral terbinafine 250 mg OD for 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails)
explanation: Per BNF and BAD onychomycosis guidance: confirmed dermatophyte onychomycosis with nail involvement requires systemic therapy. Oral terbinafine 250 mg OD is first-line: 6 weeks for fingernails, 12 weeks for toenails. LFTs should be checked before and during prolonged courses. Topical antifungals are largely ineffective for nail infections due to poor penetration (amorolfine nail lacquer can be used for very mild distal disease). Specialist referral if treatment fails.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman has been using hydrocortisone 1% cream on her face daily for 6 months for 'sensitive skin'. She now presents with persistent redness, visible blood vessels, and small papules around her mouth and nose. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: peri-oral dermatitis (steroid-induced rosacea-like dermatitis)
explanation: Per BNF and dermatology guidance: chronic topical steroid use on the face (especially for non-specific 'sensitive skin') commonly causes peri-oral dermatitis — a rosacea-like eruption with papules, pustules, and erythema around mouth/nose, sparing the vermilion border. Management: STOP topical steroid (rebound flare expected but resolves), avoid all face creams temporarily, oral tetracycline (lymecycline 408 mg OD or doxycycline 100 mg OD for 6–8 weeks) is effective. Topical metronidazole or pimecrolimus are alternatives.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old man has been started on oral lymecycline 408 mg OD by his GP for moderate acne. Per BNF and MHRA, what counselling point is most important?
option_a: avoid sun exposure / use sun protection (lymecycline causes photosensitivity)
option_e: take with food and avoid lying flat
correct_answer: avoid sun exposure / use sun protection (lymecycline causes photosensitivity)
explanation: Per BNF tetracycline monograph: photosensitivity is a common side effect of tetracyclines (doxycycline > lymecycline > minocycline). Patients should be counselled to avoid prolonged direct sunlight, use high-factor sun protection, and wear protective clothing. Other counselling: take with plenty of water to reduce oesophageal irritation; avoid taking with antacids, iron, calcium, milk (impair absorption) — though lymecycline is less affected than tetracycline itself. Tetracyclines are contraindicated in pregnancy and children <12 years (tooth discolouration).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man has facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia and inflammatory papules/pustules on the central face. Symptoms are worsened by alcohol, spicy food and sunlight. Per BAD and NICE CKS, which is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?
option_d: rosacea — trigger avoidance, gentle skincare, sunscreen, and topical metronidazole or ivermectin (or oral doxycycline for severe)
correct_answer: rosacea — trigger avoidance, gentle skincare, sunscreen, and topical metronidazole or ivermectin (or oral doxycycline for severe)
explanation: Per BAD and NICE CKS rosacea: the description is classic rosacea — facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, papules/pustules, with characteristic triggers (alcohol, spicy food, sunlight, hot drinks, temperature changes). Management: trigger avoidance, gentle skincare, daily sun protection, and topical metronidazole 0.75% (BD) or ivermectin 1% (OD) for inflammatory lesions. Severe disease: oral doxycycline 40 mg modified-release OD (sub-antimicrobial dose). Brimonidine gel for persistent erythema. Laser therapy for telangiectasia.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man develops a unilateral painful, vesicular rash in a band distribution on his left chest wall, preceded by 2 days of pain in the same area. Which is the most likely diagnosis and management?
option_c: shingles (herpes zoster) — oral aciclovir 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours of rash onset
correct_answer: shingles (herpes zoster) — oral aciclovir 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours of rash onset
explanation: Per BNF, NICE CKS (shingles): unilateral vesicular rash in a dermatomal distribution preceded by neuropathic pain is classic herpes zoster (shingles, reactivation of varicella-zoster virus). Oral aciclovir 800 mg 5x/day for 7 days reduces severity, duration and risk of post-herpetic neuralgia, ideally started within 72 hours of rash onset. Alternatives: valaciclovir 1 g TDS, famciclovir 500 mg TDS. Pain may need amitriptyline/gabapentin for post-herpetic neuralgia. Shingles in immunocompromised or with ophthalmic involvement (V1) needs specialist input.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman with established eczema reports that her skin has become red, weeping, crusted (golden-yellow crust) and painful over 3 days. She has a fever of 38.2°C. Which is the most likely diagnosis and management?
option_a: allergic contact dermatitis — strong topical steroid
option_b: impetigo / eczema herpeticum (rule out HSV) / bacterial infection (Staph aureus); urgent GP/A&E for assessment — oral antibiotics likely required; aciclovir if HSV
option_c: psoriasis flare — topical vitamin D analogue
correct_answer: impetigo / eczema herpeticum (rule out HSV) / bacterial infection (Staph aureus); urgent GP/A&E for assessment — oral antibiotics likely required; aciclovir if HSV
explanation: Per NICE CKS atopic eczema: golden-yellow crusts in established eczema suggest Staphylococcus aureus superinfection (impetigo on eczema). Painful punched-out vesicles, severe pain, systemic symptoms, or rapid spread raises suspicion for eczema herpeticum (HSV infection) — a dermatology emergency requiring IV aciclovir. Both require urgent GP/A&E referral. Empirical oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS is first-line for bacterial superinfection in non-allergic patients. Aciclovir is critical if HSV suspected.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy has a flare of severe atopic eczema. She has been using emollients alone. Per UKTIS and dermatology guidance, which is the most appropriate next-line treatment?
option_a: avoid all topical steroids in pregnancy
option_b: frequent emollients PLUS mild-to-moderate potency topical steroid (e.g. hydrocortisone 1% or clobetasone butyrate 0.05%) — short course; avoid potent steroids over large areas in pregnancy
correct_answer: frequent emollients PLUS mild-to-moderate potency topical steroid (e.g. hydrocortisone 1% or clobetasone butyrate 0.05%) — short course; avoid potent steroids over large areas in pregnancy
explanation: Per UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service) and NICE: emollients and mild-to-moderate potency topical steroids are considered safe in pregnancy. Potent/very potent steroids should be used with caution and ideally avoided in large amounts or over large surface areas (some systemic absorption — small risk of low birth weight with prolonged use of potent steroids). Oral methotrexate is highly teratogenic and contraindicated. Phototherapy (UVB) can be considered for severe cases. Topical tacrolimus has limited data but is sometimes used by specialists.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman has urticaria (raised itchy weals) and angioedema (lip swelling) 30 minutes after eating in a restaurant. She has no breathing difficulty, no hypotension, no tongue swelling. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_b: cetirizine 10 mg PO STAT (single dose), observe for progression; if progressing or any feature of anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline + 999
correct_answer: cetirizine 10 mg PO STAT (single dose), observe for progression; if progressing or any feature of anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline + 999
explanation: Per Resuscitation Council UK and BSACI: isolated urticaria and angioedema without airway/breathing/circulation features can be managed with oral non-sedating antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg) — second dose 4–6 hours later if needed. Patient must be observed and counselled to seek urgent help (999) if breathing difficulty, throat tightness, tongue swelling, voice changes, dizziness or any anaphylaxis features develop — treat as anaphylaxis with IM adrenaline. Underlying food trigger should be identified and avoided. Allergy referral may be appropriate.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 40-year-old woman has been using emollient cream from a tub by scooping with her hands for 2 months. She develops a worsening eczema flare with weeping and crusts. The pharmacist suspects bacterial contamination of the tub. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: continue using the same tub but boil it daily
option_b: discard the contaminated tub, use a clean spatula or pump dispenser to avoid contamination, and seek GP review for the eczema flare (may need antibiotics)
option_c: heat the cream in a microwave
correct_answer: discard the contaminated tub, use a clean spatula or pump dispenser to avoid contamination, and seek GP review for the eczema flare (may need antibiotics)
explanation: Per emollient safety guidance and CKS: emollients in tubs can become contaminated with bacteria (including Staphylococcus aureus) when scooped repeatedly with bare hands, predisposing to eczema infection. Pump dispensers or use of a clean spatula prevent contamination. Patients should be advised to discard any visibly contaminated product. Note also the MHRA fire risk warning for paraffin-based emollients — patient bedding and clothing can become impregnated and pose serious fire risk; never smoke or use naked flames.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old woman has had a hard, scaly, slowly enlarging plaque on her lower lip for several months. She has a history of significant sun exposure and smokes 20 cigarettes/day. The lesion has recently ulcerated. Which is the most likely diagnosis and action?
option_c: squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the lip — urgent (2-week wait) referral under suspected skin cancer pathway
correct_answer: squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the lip — urgent (2-week wait) referral under suspected skin cancer pathway
explanation: Per NICE NG12 (suspected cancer): persistent ulcerated lesion on sun-damaged lip or skin in a smoker is suspicious for squamous cell carcinoma. Lip SCCs (especially lower lip) have higher metastatic potential than typical cutaneous SCCs. Urgent referral under the 2-week wait pathway is required. Risk factors: UV exposure, smoking, immunosuppression, HPV, fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II). Differential: actinic cheilitis (precancerous) — may need biopsy to differentiate.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old healthcare worker presents to occupational health with worsening hand dermatitis. She does frequent handwashing and uses alcohol gel. The skin on her hands is dry, cracked and itchy, sparing the wrists. Which is the most likely cause and management?
option_b: irritant contact dermatitis from frequent handwashing/sanitiser — generous emollient use (during and at end of shifts), reduced washing frequency where possible, glove use for wet work, and short-term topical steroid for flares
correct_answer: irritant contact dermatitis from frequent handwashing/sanitiser — generous emollient use (during and at end of shifts), reduced washing frequency where possible, glove use for wet work, and short-term topical steroid for flares
explanation: Per CKS (occupational dermatitis): irritant contact dermatitis from frequent handwashing and alcohol sanitiser is very common in healthcare workers. Management: regular use of bland emollients (during and after shifts), use of soap substitutes, glove use for prolonged wet work (cotton-lined latex/nitrile, change frequently), short courses of topical corticosteroids (moderate potency, e.g. clobetasone or betamethasone) for active dermatitis. Patch testing reserved if allergic contact suspected. Occupational health referral important to identify and modify triggers.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 73-year-old woman attends the GP practice for a medication review. Her regular medication: atorvastatin 20 mg OD, clopidogrel 75 mg OD, lansoprazole 15 mg OD, metformin 500 mg BD, ramipril 1.25 mg OD. Recent results: creatinine 155 micromol/L (was 132 12 months ago), eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m² (was 34). Which medication is the highest priority to review?
correct_answer: metformin
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA: metformin should be avoided when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² due to risk of lactic acidosis. Stop metformin and consider an alternative oral antidiabetic suitable for severe CKD (e.g. linagliptin, repaglinide) or insulin. Ramipril dose may need review and creatinine monitoring, but ACEi has prognostic benefit in CKD if tolerated. Statins and clopidogrel are not contraindicated.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman presents with fatigue. Her Hb is 92 g/L (115–155), MCV 72 fL (low, normal 80–100), ferritin 8 micrograms/L (normal 15–150). She has heavy menstrual bleeding. She has no GI symptoms. Which is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?
option_a: anaemia of chronic disease — treat the underlying condition
option_b: folate deficiency anaemia — folic acid 5 mg OD
option_c: iron deficiency anaemia — ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS (or ferrous fumarate)
correct_answer: iron deficiency anaemia — ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS (or ferrous fumarate)
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS (iron deficiency anaemia): microcytic anaemia (low MCV) with low ferritin is iron deficiency anaemia. First-line is oral iron — ferrous sulfate 200 mg (= 65 mg elemental iron) TDS, OR ferrous fumarate 210 mg TDS. Counselling: take on empty stomach with vitamin C source for absorption; avoid tea/coffee/dairy/antacids near doses; GI side effects common (constipation, dark stools); 3–6 months treatment then 3 months after Hb normalised to replenish stores.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old vegan woman has tingling in her hands and feet, glossitis and macrocytic anaemia (Hb 95 g/L, MCV 110 fL). Her ferritin is normal. Per BNF, what is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?
option_a: folate deficiency — folic acid 5 mg OD
option_e: vitamin B12 deficiency — hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM 3 times weekly for 2 weeks, then maintenance every 3 months
correct_answer: vitamin B12 deficiency — hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM 3 times weekly for 2 weeks, then maintenance every 3 months
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS: macrocytic anaemia with neurological features (paraesthesia, peripheral neuropathy) in a vegan strongly suggests vitamin B12 deficiency (animal products are the dietary source). Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM 3x weekly for 2 weeks (or until no further improvement) then 1 mg every 3 months indefinitely (for dietary deficiency or pernicious anaemia). If neurological involvement, alternate-day until no further improvement, then maintenance. NEVER give folic acid alone in suspected B12 deficiency — risk of subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 80-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (eGFR 25 mL/min/1.73m²) has Hb 96 g/L. He has no iron deficiency (ferritin 250). Which is the most appropriate management?
option_d: referral to renal team for erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (e.g. darbepoetin) with iron status optimisation
correct_answer: referral to renal team for erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (e.g. darbepoetin) with iron status optimisation
explanation: Per NICE NG203 (CKD anaemia management): anaemia in CKD is often due to reduced erythropoietin production. Once iron deficiency is excluded (or treated), patients with Hb <100 g/L can be considered for an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) — e.g. darbepoetin alfa, epoetin. Target Hb 100–120 g/L. Higher targets associated with thrombotic risk. Iron status (transferrin saturation, ferritin) must be optimised before/during ESA therapy. Blood transfusion reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia or unresponsive cases.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is on metformin 1 g BD for 8 years. She presents with fatigue and a confirmed B12 deficiency. Which is the most likely link?
option_a: metformin causes B12 deficiency through interference with B12-intrinsic factor uptake in the terminal ileum
correct_answer: metformin causes B12 deficiency through interference with B12-intrinsic factor uptake in the terminal ileum
explanation: Per BNF metformin monograph and NICE: long-term metformin (>4 years, or high doses) is associated with vitamin B12 deficiency due to interference with calcium-dependent absorption of B12-intrinsic factor in the terminal ileum. Recommendation (NICE NG28): annual B12 measurement in patients on long-term metformin, particularly with risk factors (vegetarian/vegan, gastric surgery, age). Treatment: oral cyanocobalamin or IM hydroxocobalamin. Metformin can usually be continued with B12 supplementation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man taking warfarin (target INR 2–3) for AF presents to the pharmacy. He reports starting a 'green vegetable smoothie' daily 2 weeks ago. His INR has dropped from 2.4 to 1.5. Which is the most likely explanation?
option_b: high vitamin K intake from green vegetables antagonising warfarin
option_e: warfarin tablets are out of date
correct_answer: high vitamin K intake from green vegetables antagonising warfarin
explanation: Per BNF warfarin monograph: warfarin acts by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. A sudden increase in dietary vitamin K (green leafy vegetables — kale, spinach, broccoli; green tea) antagonises warfarin and reduces INR. Patients should aim for consistent, moderate vitamin K intake rather than fluctuations. Pharmacist should counsel on consistent diet, recheck INR, and adjust dose if needed.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman is pregnant (6 weeks gestation). She has no medical conditions and is not on any regular medication. She did not take folic acid pre-conception. Per BNF and NICE NG201, which is the most appropriate advice on folic acid?
option_a: no folic acid required after 6 weeks
option_b: take folic acid 400 micrograms OD up to 12 weeks gestation (5 mg if higher-risk: BMI ≥30, diabetes, AED use, sickle cell, family history NTD)
option_d: take folic acid only if vomiting
option_e: take folic acid throughout pregnancy in unrestricted dose
correct_answer: take folic acid 400 micrograms OD up to 12 weeks gestation (5 mg if higher-risk: BMI ≥30, diabetes, AED use, sickle cell, family history NTD)
explanation: Per NICE NG201 (antenatal care) and BNF: all women should take folic acid 400 micrograms OD from preconception to 12 weeks of gestation to reduce neural tube defect (NTD) risk. The dose is increased to 5 mg OD if higher risk: BMI ≥30, diabetes, taking anti-epileptic drugs, sickle cell/thalassaemia, previous baby with NTD, or both parents are NTD carriers. Higher-dose folate is also given in conjunction with methotrexate, sulfasalazine and trimethoprim use.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman taking ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS for iron deficiency anaemia attends the pharmacy reporting severe constipation, nausea and abdominal cramps. Which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: change to ferrous fumarate 210 mg TDS or ferrous gluconate, take with food, consider once-daily dosing or alternate-day dosing — these may improve tolerability
correct_answer: change to ferrous fumarate 210 mg TDS or ferrous gluconate, take with food, consider once-daily dosing or alternate-day dosing — these may improve tolerability
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS: oral iron commonly causes GI side effects. Strategies: try a different oral iron salt (ferrous fumarate, ferrous gluconate), take with food (slightly reduces absorption but improves tolerability), reduce frequency to once-daily or alternate-day (evidence supports equivalent absorption with better tolerability). If still intolerant or severe deficiency, IV iron (e.g. ferric carboxymaltose, iron isomaltoside) is an option. Patients should be counselled on dark stools (normal) and not to take iron with tea, coffee, dairy, or antacids near doses.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man with newly diagnosed polycythaemia vera (haematocrit 0.55, Hb 195 g/L, JAK2-positive) is reviewed by haematology. He is at low cardiovascular risk. Per BSH guidelines, which is the most appropriate initial management?
option_a: aspirin 75 mg OD plus regular venesection to maintain haematocrit <0.45
correct_answer: aspirin 75 mg OD plus regular venesection to maintain haematocrit <0.45
explanation: Per BSH (British Society for Haematology) guideline on polycythaemia vera: low-risk patients (age <60, no prior thrombosis) are managed with aspirin 75 mg OD (thrombosis prevention) and regular venesection to maintain haematocrit <0.45. Cytoreductive therapy (hydroxycarbamide) is added for high-risk patients (age ≥60 or prior thrombosis), or if disease progresses. Ruxolitinib (JAK inhibitor) is used in hydroxycarbamide-resistant disease. Aspirin reduces thrombotic events including thrombotic stroke and MI.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 72-year-old man with COPD is on long-term oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 7.5 mg OD). He has been losing weight (>5% in 3 months), with poor appetite and reduced muscle mass. He is referred for nutrition assessment. Per NICE CG32 (nutrition support), which is the most appropriate intervention?
option_b: first-line dietary advice (food fortification, small frequent meals); add oral nutritional supplements (e.g. high-calorie/protein drinks) if dietary advice insufficient
correct_answer: first-line dietary advice (food fortification, small frequent meals); add oral nutritional supplements (e.g. high-calorie/protein drinks) if dietary advice insufficient
explanation: Per NICE CG32: malnutrition (unintentional weight loss >10%, or >5% in 3–6 months, or BMI <18.5) requires nutrition assessment using a screening tool (e.g. MUST). First-line is dietary advice — food first approach (fortification with butter/cream, small frequent meals, finger foods). Oral nutritional supplements (ONS) like Ensure, Fortisip are added if dietary advice alone is insufficient. Enteral feeding (NG, PEG) is reserved for those unable to swallow safely or meet needs orally. Parenteral nutrition is for those unable to absorb (e.g. short bowel).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman with newly diagnosed adult-onset coeliac disease has been on a strict gluten-free diet for 12 months. She remains symptomatic with persistent steatorrhoea and weight loss. Anti-tTG antibodies are now negative. Which is the most likely cause to investigate?
option_a: continued inadvertent gluten exposure or rare refractory coeliac disease (specialist referral); also check for B12, iron, folate, vitamin D, calcium, magnesium deficiencies and lactose intolerance
correct_answer: continued inadvertent gluten exposure or rare refractory coeliac disease (specialist referral); also check for B12, iron, folate, vitamin D, calcium, magnesium deficiencies and lactose intolerance
explanation: Per BSG coeliac guidelines: persistent symptoms after 1 year of strict GFD raise concerns for: (1) inadvertent gluten exposure (most common — review dietitian/labels); (2) coexisting conditions (lactose intolerance, microscopic colitis, IBS, SIBO); (3) refractory coeliac disease (rare, specialist referral); (4) ongoing micronutrient deficiencies common in coeliac (B12, folate, iron, vitamin D, calcium). Annual review with FBC, ferritin, B12, folate, calcium, LFTs, bone density is standard.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 80-year-old woman with severe heart failure is taking furosemide 80 mg OD. Her recent blood tests: K+ 2.9 mmol/L (3.5–5.3), Na+ 130 mmol/L (133–146), Mg²⁺ 0.5 mmol/L (low). She reports muscle cramps. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: correct hypokalaemia (oral or IV potassium) and hypomagnesaemia, review furosemide dose, consider potassium-sparing diuretic (e.g. spironolactone)
correct_answer: correct hypokalaemia (oral or IV potassium) and hypomagnesaemia, review furosemide dose, consider potassium-sparing diuretic (e.g. spironolactone)
explanation: Per BNF and clinical management: loop diuretics commonly cause hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia. Hypomagnesaemia worsens hypokalaemia and is often missed — magnesium must also be replaced (oral magnesium glycerophosphate or aspartate; IV if severe). Adding a potassium-sparing diuretic (e.g. spironolactone, amiloride) reduces ongoing loss and has prognostic benefit in HF. Severe hypokalaemia (<2.5 or symptomatic) requires IV potassium. Note: severe hypokalaemia increases digoxin toxicity risk and predisposes to arrhythmias.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is starting chemotherapy. The team is concerned about tumour lysis syndrome. Which is the most appropriate prophylaxis?
option_a: aggressive hydration plus allopurinol (or rasburicase in high-risk patients) and electrolyte monitoring
correct_answer: aggressive hydration plus allopurinol (or rasburicase in high-risk patients) and electrolyte monitoring
explanation: Per BSH and BNF: tumour lysis syndrome (TLS) is a metabolic emergency from rapid tumour cell breakdown — hyperuricaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, AKI. Prophylaxis: aggressive IV hydration (2–3 L/m²/day) + allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor reduces uric acid production) — start 24–48h before chemotherapy and continue for ~7 days. For high-risk patients (very high WCC, bulky tumours, high LDH): rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase that rapidly degrades existing uric acid). Strict electrolyte monitoring during induction.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 38-year-old woman taking lithium for bipolar affective disorder takes a serum lithium level. Her result is 1.6 mmol/L (target 0.6–1.0 for maintenance). She is asymptomatic. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_c: reduce the lithium dose, check renal function and hydration status, recheck level in 7 days; refer urgently if symptoms develop
correct_answer: reduce the lithium dose, check renal function and hydration status, recheck level in 7 days; refer urgently if symptoms develop
explanation: Per BNF lithium monograph: lithium has a narrow therapeutic range. Levels 1.5–2.0 mmol/L: mild toxicity (often asymptomatic). Levels >2.0: moderate (vomiting, tremor, ataxia). Levels >2.5: severe (seizures, coma, death). Asymptomatic mild elevation: reduce dose, hold dose temporarily, check renal function (lithium is renally excreted), assess hydration/precipitants (dehydration, NSAID/ACEi co-use, low salt), recheck level in 5–7 days. Symptomatic toxicity or levels >2.0 mmol/L typically need hospital admission for IV fluids and may need dialysis.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old man with newly diagnosed sickle cell disease (HbSS) attends the pharmacy. Per BSH and NICE guidance, what is the most appropriate routine prophylactic medication for adults?
option_a: adult prophylactic penicillin V 500 mg BD and folic acid 5 mg OD
correct_answer: adult prophylactic penicillin V 500 mg BD and folic acid 5 mg OD
explanation: Per BSH and BNF: patients with sickle cell disease (especially HbSS, sickle β⁰ thalassaemia, hyposplenism) require lifelong prophylactic penicillin V 500 mg BD to prevent pneumococcal infection (functional asplenia). Folic acid 5 mg OD is also given due to chronic haemolysis and increased folate requirements. Vaccinations include pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, hepatitis B, influenza annually. Hydroxycarbamide reduces crises in moderate-severe disease. Crisis management: hydration, analgesia (typically opioids), oxygen, treatment of infection/triggers.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man with hypertension on ramipril 10 mg OD attends the pharmacy with a prescription for spironolactone 25 mg OD (added for resistant hypertension). Which monitoring is most important?
option_d: renal function (U&Es) and potassium monitoring, especially in first 2 weeks
correct_answer: renal function (U&Es) and potassium monitoring, especially in first 2 weeks
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG136: the combination of ACEi (or ARB) + potassium-sparing diuretic (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride) significantly increases the risk of hyperkalaemia and renal impairment. U&Es and potassium should be checked at baseline, within 1–2 weeks of initiation, after dose changes, and regularly during treatment. Particularly important in older adults, CKD, diabetes, NSAID use. K+ >5.5 mmol/L typically triggers spironolactone dose reduction or discontinuation.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old child has been diagnosed with iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 100 g/L). The dietitian recommends iron-rich foods plus oral iron supplementation. Per BNF for Children, which is the most appropriate paediatric iron formulation and key counselling?
option_a: ferrous fumarate liquid or sodium feredetate liquid, taken with vitamin C source; can stain teeth — use a straw or rinse mouth
correct_answer: ferrous fumarate liquid or sodium feredetate liquid, taken with vitamin C source; can stain teeth — use a straw or rinse mouth
explanation: Per BNFc: paediatric oral iron preparations include ferrous fumarate syrup, sodium feredetate (Sytron). Counsel: liquid iron can stain teeth temporarily — give via straw, rinse mouth or brush teeth after dose. Take with vitamin C source (orange juice) to enhance absorption. Avoid with milk/dairy. Treat for 3 months and continue 3 months after Hb normalised to replenish stores. Investigate cause if no obvious dietary cause (rare — coeliac, Meckel's diverticulum, etc.).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (eGFR 30) is taking apixaban 5 mg BD for atrial fibrillation. He develops black tarry stools and dizziness. Hb has dropped from 130 to 78 g/L. He is haemodynamically unstable. Which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: stop apixaban; resuscitate (IV fluids, transfuse blood); consider andexanet alfa for reversal if available; arrange urgent endoscopy
correct_answer: stop apixaban; resuscitate (IV fluids, transfuse blood); consider andexanet alfa for reversal if available; arrange urgent endoscopy
explanation: Per BNF and NICE: major bleeding on a DOAC requires urgent management — stop the DOAC, supportive care (fluids, blood products), and consider specific reversal: andexanet alfa for apixaban and rivaroxaban (factor Xa inhibitors), idarucizumab for dabigatran. If specific reversal unavailable, prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) is an alternative. The bleeding source must be identified and treated (e.g. endoscopy for GI bleeding). Vitamin K is for warfarin, not DOACs.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman with newly diagnosed coeliac disease is reviewed 12 months after gluten-free diet initiation. Her ferritin is low (8 micrograms/L), vitamin D 18 nmol/L (insufficient <30), and bone density T-score -2.4 (osteopenia). Which is the most appropriate management plan?
option_b: correct iron and vitamin D/calcium deficiencies; check B12/folate; consider bone protection (lifestyle ± bisphosphonate based on fracture risk and DEXA); continue strict GFD
correct_answer: correct iron and vitamin D/calcium deficiencies; check B12/folate; consider bone protection (lifestyle ± bisphosphonate based on fracture risk and DEXA); continue strict GFD
explanation: Per BSG coeliac guidance and NOGG: patients with coeliac disease have impaired absorption of iron, calcium, vitamin D, folate, B12 and other nutrients — deficiencies must be screened and replaced. Reduced bone density (osteopenia/osteoporosis) is common due to malabsorption of calcium/vitamin D — DEXA at diagnosis and at follow-up. Bone protection follows NOGG: lifestyle (weight-bearing exercise, smoking cessation, alcohol reduction), calcium/vitamin D, and bisphosphonate if FRAX/QFracture indicates high fracture risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman in her first trimester of pregnancy presents with severe nausea and vomiting (hyperemesis gravidarum). She has lost 4 kg in 2 weeks and is unable to keep fluids down. She has tried oral cyclizine but it is not effective. Per RCOG Green-top Guideline 69, which is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: accept that vomiting in pregnancy is normal
option_b: admit to hospital for IV fluids and IV antiemetics (e.g. cyclizine, prochlorperazine, ondansetron with shared decision-making after first trimester); thiamine replacement
option_e: prescribe oral ondansetron 8 mg TDS without further assessment
correct_answer: admit to hospital for IV fluids and IV antiemetics (e.g. cyclizine, prochlorperazine, ondansetron with shared decision-making after first trimester); thiamine replacement
explanation: Per RCOG Green-top Guideline 69 (nausea and vomiting in pregnancy): hyperemesis gravidarum (>5% weight loss, dehydration, ketosis) requires hospital admission for IV fluids (with thiamine to prevent Wernicke's), IV antiemetics, and electrolyte correction. First-line antiemetics: cyclizine, promethazine. Second-line: metoclopramide (short courses only — EPS risk), prochlorperazine. Ondansetron is effective but newer data suggest a small risk of cleft palate in first trimester; shared decision-making after first trimester. Corticosteroids reserved for refractory cases.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman attends the pharmacy 50 hours after unprotected sexual intercourse, requesting emergency contraception. Her last menstrual period was 14 days ago (regular 28-day cycle). She has no contraindications. Per FSRH guidance, which is the most appropriate option?
option_a: copper IUD (most effective) — can be inserted up to 120h post-UPSI or up to 5 days after estimated ovulation; otherwise ulipristal acetate (EllaOne) 30 mg PO single dose preferred (effective up to 120h) over levonorgestrel (only up to 72h)
correct_answer: copper IUD (most effective) — can be inserted up to 120h post-UPSI or up to 5 days after estimated ovulation; otherwise ulipristal acetate (EllaOne) 30 mg PO single dose preferred (effective up to 120h) over levonorgestrel (only up to 72h)
explanation: Per FSRH emergency contraception guideline: copper IUD is the most effective EC method, can be inserted up to 120h post-UPSI or up to 5 days after estimated ovulation. If IUD not suitable/wanted: ulipristal acetate 30 mg PO single dose (effective up to 120h, more effective than levonorgestrel after ovulation) — but must avoid hormonal contraception for 5 days after ulipristal as it competes with progestogens. Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg (3 mg if BMI >26 or weight >70 kg) effective up to 72h, less effective close to ovulation. At 50h, in mid-cycle, the patient is near ovulation — copper IUD or ulipristal preferred.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 26-year-old woman starts a combined oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30, levonorgestrel 150 micrograms + ethinylestradiol 30 micrograms). She presents on Day 8 of her first pack reporting that she vomited 1 hour after taking her pill yesterday. Per FSRH guidance, what advice should she be given?
option_a: take an extra pill from a different pack and continue normally; if persistent vomiting/diarrhoea for ≥24h, use additional contraception (condoms) for 7 days
option_b: double her dose for the rest of the pack
option_e: take EC even though no sex occurred
correct_answer: take an extra pill from a different pack and continue normally; if persistent vomiting/diarrhoea for ≥24h, use additional contraception (condoms) for 7 days
explanation: Per FSRH: if vomiting occurs within 2–3 hours of COC (or POP), the pill is considered not absorbed — take another pill ASAP. If unable to keep pill down, treat as a missed pill (use condoms for 7 days, consider EC if needed). Severe vomiting/diarrhoea (>24h) also reduces absorption — additional precautions (condoms) for the duration plus 7 days. Specific 'missed pill' rules depend on which pill week missed in.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 38-year-old woman is on the combined oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30). She presents to the pharmacy with new-onset unilateral leg swelling and calf pain. She is otherwise well. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: immediate urgent referral to A&E to exclude deep vein thrombosis (DVT); stop the COC
correct_answer: immediate urgent referral to A&E to exclude deep vein thrombosis (DVT); stop the COC
explanation: Per BNF and FSRH: COC use significantly increases VTE risk (especially in older women, BMI >35, smokers, family history). Unilateral leg swelling/pain in a COC user must be assessed urgently for DVT (D-dimer, Doppler USS). Other red flags for COC users: sudden breathlessness/chest pain (PE), severe headache/visual changes (CVA), sudden severe abdominal pain (mesenteric thrombosis). COC should be stopped and alternative contraception considered (progestogen-only methods or non-hormonal).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman is breastfeeding her 8-week-old baby. She wants to start contraception. She has no contraindications. Per FSRH guidance, which option is most suitable?
option_a: copper IUD (or LNG-IUS), progestogen-only pill (POP), progestogen implant, or DMPA injection — these are not associated with reduced milk supply or harm to baby
correct_answer: copper IUD (or LNG-IUS), progestogen-only pill (POP), progestogen implant, or DMPA injection — these are not associated with reduced milk supply or harm to baby
explanation: Per FSRH contraception after pregnancy guideline: combined hormonal contraception (oestrogen-containing) is generally avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum (VTE risk) and not recommended in breastfeeding for the first 6 weeks (theoretical impact on milk supply). Progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS) and non-hormonal methods (copper IUD, condoms) are safe in breastfeeding and can be started from 3 weeks (or immediately for IUD if criteria met). Most efficacious 'fit and forget' methods (LARC): IUD, IUS, implant.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 51-year-old woman with severe menopausal vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats) is being considered for HRT. She has a uterus, no personal/family history of breast cancer or VTE, BP 130/82. Per NICE NG23, which HRT regimen is most appropriate?
option_a: combined HRT — oestrogen plus continuous progestogen (for amenorrhoeic women >12 months without periods) or sequential progestogen (for perimenopausal women still having periods)
option_c: oestrogen monotherapy in a woman with uterus
correct_answer: combined HRT — oestrogen plus continuous progestogen (for amenorrhoeic women >12 months without periods) or sequential progestogen (for perimenopausal women still having periods)
explanation: Per NICE NG23 (menopause): women with a uterus require combined HRT (oestrogen + progestogen) — oestrogen-only HRT causes endometrial hyperplasia and cancer. Regimen depends on menopausal status: sequential combined (cyclical progestogen) for perimenopausal women still having periods; continuous combined ('bleed-free') for women postmenopausal (>12 months amenorrhoea or >54 years). Oestrogen monotherapy is reserved for women who have had a hysterectomy. Transdermal route preferred over oral if VTE risk concerns.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 51-year-old woman is considering HRT. She is a non-smoker but has a BMI of 32 kg/m² and a personal history of unprovoked DVT 8 years ago. She is severely symptomatic with menopausal hot flushes. Per NICE NG23 and FSRH, what is the most appropriate HRT route if HRT is to be used?
option_a: avoid HRT entirely; offer SSRI/SNRI (e.g. venlafaxine) for vasomotor symptoms
option_d: transdermal HRT (lower VTE risk than oral) plus progestogen; specialist haematology input may be helpful given personal VTE history
correct_answer: transdermal HRT (lower VTE risk than oral) plus progestogen; specialist haematology input may be helpful given personal VTE history
explanation: Per NICE NG23 and BMS guidance: oral HRT increases VTE risk (especially first-pass hepatic metabolism — oral oestrogen increases hepatic clotting factor production). Transdermal HRT (patches, gel) bypasses first-pass and has a much lower (probably no increased) VTE risk. For women at increased VTE risk, transdermal is preferred. For women with prior unprovoked VTE, haematology input is helpful — generally transdermal HRT acceptable with risk discussion. Non-hormonal alternatives for hot flushes: SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine), SNRIs (venlafaxine), clonidine, gabapentin.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks gestation has confirmed asymptomatic bacteriuria (E. coli on MSU). She has no penicillin allergy. Per NICE NG109, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: amoxicillin or cefalexin (per sensitivities) for 7 days; nitrofurantoin avoided at term
correct_answer: amoxicillin or cefalexin (per sensitivities) for 7 days; nitrofurantoin avoided at term
explanation: Per NICE NG109 and antenatal guidance: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy must be treated (untreated → pyelonephritis, preterm labour, low birth weight). 7-day course based on sensitivities. Antibiotic safety in pregnancy: amoxicillin and cefalexin SAFE throughout. Nitrofurantoin: avoid AT TERM (last trimester) — neonatal haemolysis risk. Trimethoprim: avoid FIRST trimester — folate antagonist, neural tube defects. Quinolones, tetracyclines, aminoglycosides: avoid (fetal toxicity).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman has heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) without anaemia or structural abnormality. She does not currently want pregnancy. Per NICE NG88, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
option_e: tranexamic acid PRN during periods only
correct_answer: hormonal IUS (Mirena — levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system)
explanation: Per NICE NG88 (HMB): first-line for HMB without structural abnormality is the LNG-IUS (Mirena), if long-term contraception is acceptable. It significantly reduces menstrual blood loss (>90% in many women), provides contraception, and protects against endometrial hyperplasia. Second-line options: COC, tranexamic acid (anti-fibrinolytic, for women wanting non-hormonal), NSAIDs (mefenamic acid). Norethisterone has more side effects. Surgical options (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy) reserved for failed medical treatment or completed family.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 26-year-old woman with confirmed bacterial vaginosis (BV) — thin grey-white discharge, fishy odour, clue cells on microscopy — is not pregnant. Per BASHH and BNF, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
option_d: metronidazole 400 mg BD PO for 5–7 days (or 2 g PO single dose if adherence concerns)
correct_answer: metronidazole 400 mg BD PO for 5–7 days (or 2 g PO single dose if adherence concerns)
explanation: Per BASHH (British Association for Sexual Health and HIV) guideline: bacterial vaginosis is overgrowth of anaerobes (e.g. Gardnerella vaginalis). First-line: metronidazole 400 mg BD PO for 5–7 days. Alternatives: 2 g PO single dose (lower cure but better adherence), intravaginal metronidazole gel, intravaginal clindamycin cream. Counsel: avoid alcohol with metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). Clotrimazole and fluconazole are for candida. Sexual partner treatment not routinely needed.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 24-year-old sexually active woman has confirmed chlamydia (Chlamydia trachomatis on NAAT). She is asymptomatic. She has no allergies and is not pregnant. Per BASHH, which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: doxycycline 100 mg BD PO for 7 days; abstain from sex (including with condoms) for 7 days; partner notification and treatment
correct_answer: doxycycline 100 mg BD PO for 7 days; abstain from sex (including with condoms) for 7 days; partner notification and treatment
explanation: Per BASHH chlamydia guideline (updated): first-line is doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days (replaced single-dose azithromycin due to concerns about Mycoplasma genitalium resistance and lower efficacy in rectal infection). Alternatives in pregnancy or doxycycline contraindication: azithromycin 1 g STAT then 500 mg OD for 2 days. Counsel: abstain from sex (including with condoms) for 7 days after starting treatment AND until partner(s) treated. Partner notification and treatment essential. Test of cure not routine for uncomplicated genital infection except in pregnancy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman has confirmed gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae on NAAT, sensitivities pending). She is not pregnant. Per BASHH 2024 guidance, which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated genital infection?
option_e: penicillin V 500 mg QDS for 7 days
correct_answer: ceftriaxone 1 g IM single dose
explanation: Per BASHH gonorrhoea guideline (updated due to rising resistance): first-line is ceftriaxone 1 g IM single dose (single dose increased from 500 mg). Antimicrobial resistance to gonorrhoea is a major concern (extensively drug-resistant strains emerging globally). Test of cure essential 2 weeks after treatment. Co-treat for chlamydia if NAAT positive or empirically. Partner notification and treatment, abstinence advice as for chlamydia. Specialist GUM clinic referral preferred.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 17-year-old girl attends the community pharmacy and requests the progestogen-only pill (POP) over-the-counter under the new pharmacy service. She has not had her blood pressure or BMI taken. Per the PGD/service specification, which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: decline the request as POPs cannot be sold OTC
option_b: perform blood pressure and BMI as required by the service, undertake medical/contraceptive history, document the consultation, sign a consent form for under-18s, and provide POP if eligible (or refer to GP/sexual health)
correct_answer: perform blood pressure and BMI as required by the service, undertake medical/contraceptive history, document the consultation, sign a consent form for under-18s, and provide POP if eligible (or refer to GP/sexual health)
explanation: Per the NHS England Pharmacy Contraception Service (launched 2023): community pharmacists can initiate or supply ongoing contraception (POP) following a structured consultation. The service requires assessment using GMC Fraser/Gillick competence in under-16s, medical and contraceptive history, BP and BMI, drug interactions check, contraindications screen (UKMEC criteria), counselling on use/missed pills/STI risk. Documentation in shared health record. Patients not eligible or with red flags are referred to GP or sexual health.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman with epilepsy on lamotrigine 200 mg BD is planning pregnancy. Her seizures are well controlled. Per BNF, MHRA and UKTIS, which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: continue lamotrigine, start folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception until at least 12 weeks gestation, optimise control before conception, refer to specialist for pre-conception counselling
correct_answer: continue lamotrigine, start folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception until at least 12 weeks gestation, optimise control before conception, refer to specialist for pre-conception counselling
explanation: Per BNF and UKTIS: lamotrigine is one of the preferred AEDs in pregnancy (lower teratogenicity than valproate or phenobarbital). Folic acid 5 mg OD from preconception to at least 12 weeks. Uncontrolled seizures pose risk to mother and fetus — continuing effective treatment is essential. Levetiracetam is another preferred AED. Sodium valproate must be avoided unless no alternative (MHRA Pregnancy Prevention). Lamotrigine levels often drop during pregnancy (induced metabolism) — closer monitoring and dose adjustments may be needed.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old man with newly diagnosed benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) has moderate symptoms (IPSS 14, poor stream, nocturia x3). He has stable angina (on aspirin, GTN, ramipril, bisoprolol). Per NICE NG97, which is the most appropriate initial drug treatment?
option_a: 5α-reductase inhibitor (e.g. finasteride) — particularly if prostate is enlarged
correct_answer: alpha-1 antagonist (e.g. tamsulosin 400 micrograms OD)
explanation: Per NICE NG97 (lower urinary tract symptoms in men): for moderate-severe LUTS related to BPH, an alpha-1 antagonist (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, doxazosin) is first-line — works rapidly to relax smooth muscle in bladder neck/prostate. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) shrink the prostate over months — added if prostate clearly enlarged (PSA >1.4) or as combination therapy. Side effects of alpha-1: postural hypotension (especially first dose — counsel to take at night initially), retrograde ejaculation. Caution in patients on antihypertensives. Counsel about intra-operative floppy iris syndrome if cataract surgery planned (BNF warning).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman with recurrent uncomplicated UTIs (4 in past year) has been investigated and no structural abnormality found. Per NICE NG109 and BASHH, which is the most appropriate option for recurrent UTI prevention?
option_a: continuous prophylactic antibiotics for life (e.g. trimethoprim 100 mg ON) — first-line
option_b: behavioural advice (hydration, post-coital voiding), vaginal oestrogen if postmenopausal, D-mannose or methenamine hippurate as non-antibiotic options; if antibiotic prophylaxis used: low-dose nightly or post-coital (with review and stewardship)
correct_answer: behavioural advice (hydration, post-coital voiding), vaginal oestrogen if postmenopausal, D-mannose or methenamine hippurate as non-antibiotic options; if antibiotic prophylaxis used: low-dose nightly or post-coital (with review and stewardship)
explanation: Per NICE NG109 (UTI: recurrent): a stepped approach — behavioural and self-care advice, non-antibiotic options (vaginal oestrogen if postmenopausal/atrophic; D-mannose; methenamine hippurate per the ALTAR trial 2022). If antibiotic prophylaxis used: low-dose continuous (e.g. trimethoprim 100 mg ON, nitrofurantoin 50–100 mg ON) — short courses with review (6 monthly). Post-coital prophylaxis if UTIs are post-coital. Stewardship considerations: long-term antibiotics increase resistance and adverse effects.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old woman with primary dysmenorrhoea (painful periods, no underlying pathology) attends the pharmacy for OTC advice. She has tried paracetamol with minimal benefit. She has no medical conditions. Per BNF and NICE CKS, which is the most appropriate first-line option?
option_b: ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (or naproxen 250 mg TDS) — taken at onset of pain through first 2–3 days of period
correct_answer: ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (or naproxen 250 mg TDS) — taken at onset of pain through first 2–3 days of period
explanation: Per NICE CKS (dysmenorrhoea) and BNF: NSAIDs are first-line for primary dysmenorrhoea (work by inhibiting prostaglandins responsible for menstrual cramping). Take from start of period (or before if pain is predictable) for 2–3 days. If NSAIDs ineffective or contraindicated, hormonal contraception (COC or LNG-IUS) is highly effective (suppresses ovulation/endometrium). Tranexamic acid is for heavy menstrual bleeding, not pain. Heat therapy and exercise are useful adjuncts.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman with confirmed pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) has acute pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysuria, and cervical motion tenderness. She is not pregnant. Per BASHH PID guideline, which is the most appropriate outpatient regimen?
option_b: ceftriaxone 1 g IM single dose + doxycycline 100 mg BD PO for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg BD PO for 14 days
correct_answer: ceftriaxone 1 g IM single dose + doxycycline 100 mg BD PO for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg BD PO for 14 days
explanation: Per BASHH PID guideline: outpatient regimen for mild-moderate PID is ceftriaxone 1 g IM single dose (covers gonorrhoea, including resistant strains) + doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days (chlamydia, Mycoplasma genitalium) + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days (anaerobes). Severe PID, pregnancy, abscess, sepsis → admit for IV. Empirical treatment is started before culture results due to risk of complications (infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain). Sexual partners need empirical testing and treatment.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old woman with severe menopausal vasomotor symptoms cannot use HRT (history of breast cancer). She seeks pharmacological alternatives. Per NICE NG23, which is the most appropriate option?
option_a: SSRI (e.g. paroxetine, fluoxetine, citalopram) or SNRI (venlafaxine) for vasomotor symptoms — except in patients on tamoxifen (avoid paroxetine and fluoxetine which inhibit CYP2D6 and reduce tamoxifen efficacy)
option_b: combined HRT despite breast cancer history
correct_answer: SSRI (e.g. paroxetine, fluoxetine, citalopram) or SNRI (venlafaxine) for vasomotor symptoms — except in patients on tamoxifen (avoid paroxetine and fluoxetine which inhibit CYP2D6 and reduce tamoxifen efficacy)
explanation: Per NICE NG23: non-hormonal options for vasomotor symptoms include SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine, citalopram), SNRIs (venlafaxine), gabapentin, clonidine. CRITICAL interaction: paroxetine and fluoxetine are potent CYP2D6 inhibitors and reduce tamoxifen efficacy (tamoxifen is a prodrug activated by CYP2D6 to endoxifen) — must be avoided in patients on tamoxifen. Citalopram, escitalopram, venlafaxine are safer alternatives. Vaginal symptoms in breast cancer survivors: low-dose vaginal oestrogen can be considered with oncology input.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy (32 weeks) develops a new headache, visual disturbance and BP 165/108 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows protein 3+. Which is the most likely diagnosis and immediate management?
option_d: pre-eclampsia — urgent obstetric assessment; labetalol PO/IV first-line antihypertensive; magnesium sulfate for severe disease/eclampsia prevention
correct_answer: pre-eclampsia — urgent obstetric assessment; labetalol PO/IV first-line antihypertensive; magnesium sulfate for severe disease/eclampsia prevention
explanation: Per NICE NG133 (hypertension in pregnancy): pre-eclampsia is new-onset hypertension ≥20 weeks gestation + proteinuria or end-organ damage. Severe features: BP ≥160/110, severe headache, visual disturbance, RUQ pain, oliguria, thrombocytopenia, abnormal LFTs. Urgent obstetric assessment required. First-line antihypertensives: labetalol (oral or IV). Alternatives: nifedipine, methyldopa. Magnesium sulfate IV reduces eclampsia risk in severe pre-eclampsia. Definitive treatment is delivery — timing depends on gestational age and severity.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman has erectile dysfunction in her husband (52, type 2 diabetic on metformin, ramipril, atorvastatin). The GP has prescribed sildenafil 50 mg PRN. Which is the most important counselling point?
option_b: sildenafil is contraindicated with nitrates (e.g. GTN, isosorbide mononitrate) — risk of severe hypotension; do not take within 24h of nitrates (or 48h for tadalafil); also caution with alpha-blockers
option_c: take with grapefruit juice for better effect
correct_answer: sildenafil is contraindicated with nitrates (e.g. GTN, isosorbide mononitrate) — risk of severe hypotension; do not take within 24h of nitrates (or 48h for tadalafil); also caution with alpha-blockers
explanation: Per BNF and SmPC: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) are contraindicated with nitrates and nicorandil due to risk of severe, potentially fatal hypotension. Patients with angina who may need GTN should be aware: avoid nitrates for at least 24 hours after sildenafil/vardenafil, or 48 hours after tadalafil. Caution with alpha-blockers (used in BPH) — additive hypotension, take separated. Other counselling: take 30–60 min before sex, sexual stimulation needed, side effects (headache, flushing, dyspepsia, visual changes), seek help if priapism (erection >4h).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 25-year-old man has a unilateral red, watery, mildly itchy eye for 2 days. There is a watery discharge. He has had a recent cold. His vision is normal, no photophobia, no pain. Per NICE CKS, which is the most likely diagnosis and management?
option_e: viral conjunctivitis — supportive (lubricants, hygiene measures); usually self-limiting
correct_answer: viral conjunctivitis — supportive (lubricants, hygiene measures); usually self-limiting
explanation: Per NICE CKS (conjunctivitis): viral conjunctivitis is characterised by watery discharge, mild redness, often unilateral, may be associated with URTI. Usually self-limiting (resolves in 1–2 weeks). Lubricant eye drops for comfort, hand hygiene to prevent spread. Bacterial conjunctivitis typically has purulent discharge — treated with chloramphenicol eye drops (over-the-counter for >2 years). Red flags requiring urgent assessment: severe pain, photophobia, reduced vision, contact lens wearer, foreign body sensation — could indicate keratitis, iritis, angle-closure glaucoma.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 65-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe right eye pain, headache, nausea, and seeing 'halos around lights'. His right pupil is mid-dilated and unreactive. His eye is red and feels hard on palpation. Which is the most likely diagnosis and management?
option_a: acute angle-closure glaucoma — urgent (immediate) ophthalmology referral; IV acetazolamide and topical agents (pilocarpine, timolol, etc.) to lower IOP; definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy
correct_answer: acute angle-closure glaucoma — urgent (immediate) ophthalmology referral; IV acetazolamide and topical agents (pilocarpine, timolol, etc.) to lower IOP; definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy
explanation: Per NICE CKS and Royal College of Ophthalmologists: acute angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmological emergency. Classic features: sudden severe eye pain, nausea/vomiting, headache, hazy vision, halos around lights, mid-dilated unreactive pupil, red 'stoney' hard eye. Management: urgent (immediate) ophthalmology referral, IV acetazolamide and topical IOP-lowering agents (timolol, pilocarpine, brimonidine). Definitive treatment: laser peripheral iridotomy. Delay risks permanent vision loss. Risk factors: female, age >60, Asian/Inuit ancestry, hypermetropia, family history.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 60-year-old woman with newly diagnosed primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is being initiated on a prostaglandin analogue eye drop (e.g. latanoprost 50 micrograms/mL). What is the most important counselling point?
option_b: common side effects include darkening of the iris colour (especially in mixed-colour eyes), eyelash lengthening, eye irritation, and periorbital skin pigmentation; instil once daily, usually evening
correct_answer: common side effects include darkening of the iris colour (especially in mixed-colour eyes), eyelash lengthening, eye irritation, and periorbital skin pigmentation; instil once daily, usually evening
explanation: Per BNF and SmPC: prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost, travoprost, bimatoprost) are first-line for POAG/ocular hypertension (NICE NG81). Once-daily dosing (usually evening) — most effective IOP reduction. Side effects: irreversible iris pigmentation (especially light/mixed irises — important counselling if asymmetric or only one eye treated for cosmesis), eyelash growth and pigmentation, periorbital skin pigmentation, conjunctival hyperaemia, eye irritation. Systemic absorption is low. Punctal occlusion (close eyes, press inner corner for 1–2 min after instillation) reduces systemic absorption further.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 22-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a 1-day history of painful red right eye, photophobia, blurred vision, and increased tearing. She has been swimming in the local pool while wearing her lenses. Which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: advise to remove lens and use lubricant
option_e: urgent same-day ophthalmology referral — high risk of microbial (bacterial, viral, or Acanthamoeba) keratitis from contaminated water; do NOT delay
correct_answer: urgent same-day ophthalmology referral — high risk of microbial (bacterial, viral, or Acanthamoeba) keratitis from contaminated water; do NOT delay
explanation: Per NICE CKS and Royal College of Ophthalmologists: microbial keratitis is a sight-threatening emergency in contact lens wearers. Red flags: pain, photophobia, reduced vision, history of swimming/showering in lenses, sleeping in lenses, prolonged wear. Acanthamoeba (water-borne protozoan) is a serious risk — causes severe keratitis difficult to treat. Same-day ophthalmology referral for slit-lamp examination, corneal scrape, and intensive topical antimicrobial therapy. DO NOT prescribe topical steroids before specialist review (can worsen infection).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 70-year-old woman is reading newsprint with difficulty despite glasses. She reports central distorted vision in her right eye over the past 2 weeks — straight lines appear wavy. Her left eye sees clearly. Which is the most likely diagnosis and action?
option_a: age-related macular degeneration (likely wet AMD given rapid onset/distortion) — urgent referral to ophthalmology (within 1 week) for anti-VEGF injection assessment
correct_answer: age-related macular degeneration (likely wet AMD given rapid onset/distortion) — urgent referral to ophthalmology (within 1 week) for anti-VEGF injection assessment
explanation: Per Royal College of Ophthalmologists and NICE NG82: rapid-onset central visual distortion (metamorphopsia) — straight lines wavy, central blind spot — strongly suggests wet (neovascular) AMD. Early treatment with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) preserves vision; delay reduces benefit. Urgent referral (within 1 week, often within days) to ophthalmology rapid access clinic. Dry AMD (atrophic) progresses more slowly with central vision loss. AREDS-2 supplements (vitamins, zinc, lutein) may slow progression of intermediate dry AMD.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 4-year-old boy has had 3 days of one eye stuck shut on waking, with thick yellow-green discharge. The eye is mildly red, not painful, vision normal. He attends nursery. Per NICE CKS, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops (1 drop QDS for first 2 days then BD for 5 more days) OR if not improving in 7 days seek further advice — also strict hygiene and exclusion from nursery is not always required for bacterial conjunctivitis
correct_answer: chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops (1 drop QDS for first 2 days then BD for 5 more days) OR if not improving in 7 days seek further advice — also strict hygiene and exclusion from nursery is not always required for bacterial conjunctivitis
explanation: Per NICE CKS bacterial conjunctivitis: purulent discharge with eye stuck shut on waking suggests bacterial conjunctivitis. Most cases are mild and self-limiting (within 5–7 days even without antibiotic). Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops (or 1% ointment at night) shorten duration and are appropriate. Counsel: hand hygiene, separate towels. Exclusion from school/nursery is no longer recommended for conjunctivitis (Public Health England). Refer if: contact lens wearer, severe redness/pain, photophobia, reduced vision, neonatal conjunctivitis, no improvement after 7 days.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 28-year-old man has a small, painful lump on his eyelid (top eyelid margin) for 2 days. It is red and tender. There is no spreading infection of the surrounding skin. Per NICE CKS, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: reassurance, warm compresses (5–10 min, several times daily) — usually self-limiting within 7 days; consider topical antibiotic if there is associated conjunctivitis or for very large lesions; refer if not resolving
correct_answer: reassurance, warm compresses (5–10 min, several times daily) — usually self-limiting within 7 days; consider topical antibiotic if there is associated conjunctivitis or for very large lesions; refer if not resolving
explanation: Per NICE CKS (stye / hordeolum): a stye is an acute infection of an eyelid gland (usually Staphylococcus aureus). Management: warm compresses several times daily, avoid squeezing/lancing (self-discharges within 7 days), gentle eyelid hygiene. Most resolve without antibiotic. Topical antibiotic (chloramphenicol) if associated conjunctivitis or very large/inflamed. Oral antibiotics if cellulitis (orbital cellulitis is a serious differential — refer if eyelid swelling, fever, eye movement pain, proptosis).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 60-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate develops bilateral burning, gritty, dry eyes. Schirmer test confirms reduced tear production. Per BNF and NICE CKS, which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
option_a: artificial tears (e.g. hypromellose 0.3%, carmellose, hyaluronate-based) PRN; lubricant ointment (e.g. Lacri-Lube) at night for severe cases; rule out secondary Sjogren syndrome and trigger medications
correct_answer: artificial tears (e.g. hypromellose 0.3%, carmellose, hyaluronate-based) PRN; lubricant ointment (e.g. Lacri-Lube) at night for severe cases; rule out secondary Sjogren syndrome and trigger medications
explanation: Per NICE CKS (dry eye disease): first-line is artificial tear substitutes (preservative-free preparations for frequent use), hypromellose, carmellose, polyvinyl alcohol, sodium hyaluronate. Use as needed (typically 4-8 times/day or more). Lubricant ointments at night. In autoimmune disease (RA), check for secondary Sjogren syndrome (anti-Ro/La, salivary involvement). Review medications that worsen dryness (anticholinergics, antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics). Severe cases: ciclosporin eye drops (Ikervis 0.1%), punctal plugs, omega-3 supplements.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 25-year-old woman with allergic conjunctivitis (red, itchy, watery eyes during hayfever season) seeks OTC advice. She has tried oral antihistamines with partial relief. Per BNF, which is the most appropriate additional treatment?
option_c: topical antihistamine or mast cell stabiliser eye drops (e.g. sodium cromoglicate 2%, olopatadine 1 mg/mL)
correct_answer: topical antihistamine or mast cell stabiliser eye drops (e.g. sodium cromoglicate 2%, olopatadine 1 mg/mL)
explanation: Per BNF and NICE CKS: for allergic conjunctivitis insufficiently controlled by oral antihistamines, topical ophthalmic antihistamines (antazoline, azelastine, olopatadine — dual action), or mast cell stabilisers (sodium cromoglicate — prophylactic, takes several weeks to fully work) are added. Severe cases may need topical steroid (specialist initiated only — risk of glaucoma, cataract, infection with prolonged use). Cool compresses also helpful. Avoid eye rubbing.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 12-year-old boy presents with severe ear pain, hearing loss, and discharge from the right ear that has lasted 4 days. He has fever 38.5°C. Otoscopy shows perforation of the tympanic membrane. Per NICE NG91, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS PO for 5 days; paracetamol/ibuprofen for pain; keep ear dry
option_d: topical aminoglycoside ear drops (contraindicated with perforation)
correct_answer: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS PO for 5 days; paracetamol/ibuprofen for pain; keep ear dry
explanation: Per NICE NG91 (otitis media): acute otitis media (AOM) with perforation and discharge (otorrhoea) is an indication for antibiotic therapy. Amoxicillin 5–7 days is first-line. The perforation generally heals within 6 weeks. Counsel: keep ear dry (avoid swimming, plug ear when bathing). Aminoglycoside ear drops (gentamicin, neomycin) are CONTRAINDICATED if perforation due to ototoxicity. Quinolone ear drops (ciprofloxacin) are safe in perforation. Recurrent AOM may need ENT referral for grommets.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 30-year-old man has been experiencing painful, itchy ear with discharge. Otoscopy shows the external auditory canal is red, swollen and full of debris. The tympanic membrane is intact. He swims regularly. Per NICE CKS, which is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?
option_d: otitis externa — topical antibiotic/steroid combination ear drops (e.g. neomycin/dexamethasone or ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone) for 7 days; keep ear dry; consider acetic acid 2% spray for mild cases
correct_answer: otitis externa — topical antibiotic/steroid combination ear drops (e.g. neomycin/dexamethasone or ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone) for 7 days; keep ear dry; consider acetic acid 2% spray for mild cases
explanation: Per NICE CKS (otitis externa): swimmer's ear (acute otitis externa) is inflammation of the external auditory canal — often associated with water exposure ('swimmer's ear'), eczema, trauma. Treatment: topical antibiotic + steroid combination (e.g. Sofradex — framycetin/dexamethasone/gramicidin, or ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone — Cetraxal Plus, or otomize spray neomycin/dexamethasone/acetic acid). Acetic acid 2% spray is alternative for mild cases. Keep ear dry. Aminoglycoside drops are safe if TM intact (confirm by otoscopy). Severe/diabetic patients: consider malignant otitis externa (Pseudomonas, refer urgently).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: An 80-year-old woman with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with severe deep ear pain, foul-smelling discharge, and granulation tissue visible in the ear canal. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
option_c: malignant (necrotising) otitis externa caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa — urgent ENT referral for IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin or tazocin) and imaging
correct_answer: malignant (necrotising) otitis externa caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa — urgent ENT referral for IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin or tazocin) and imaging
explanation: Per NICE CKS and ENT UK: malignant (necrotising) otitis externa is a severe, life-threatening infection — usually Pseudomonas — that extends from the external ear canal into the skull base. Risk factors: diabetes (especially poorly controlled), immunosuppression, elderly. Features: severe nocturnal pain, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, cranial nerve palsies in advanced disease. Requires urgent ENT referral, imaging (CT/MRI), IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (ciprofloxacin or piperacillin-tazobactam), debridement. Mortality if untreated.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 28-year-old man has thick green-yellow nasal discharge, facial pain over his cheeks, and a temperature of 38°C for 12 days. No improvement despite paracetamol and saline rinses. Per NICE NG79, which is the most appropriate management?
correct_answer: amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days
explanation: Per NICE NG79 (acute sinusitis): symptoms lasting ≥10 days without improvement support consideration of antibiotic treatment. Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days is first-line. Most acute sinusitis is viral and self-limiting (resolves in 7–10 days), and antibiotics are NOT indicated in the first 10 days. Intranasal corticosteroids can be considered for severe or persistent symptoms (off-label). Penicillin-allergic: doxycycline or clarithromycin.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 25-year-old woman has a 6-week history of nasal congestion, runny nose, sneezing and itchy eyes that worsens when she is around pollen and grass. She has tried oral cetirizine 10 mg OD with partial benefit. Per NICE CKS (allergic rhinitis), which is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: add an intranasal corticosteroid (e.g. fluticasone, mometasone, beclometasone) — most effective for nasal congestion; can be combined with oral antihistamine
correct_answer: add an intranasal corticosteroid (e.g. fluticasone, mometasone, beclometasone) — most effective for nasal congestion; can be combined with oral antihistamine
explanation: Per NICE CKS and BSACI guideline (allergic rhinitis): for moderate-persistent or severe symptoms, intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective single therapy, especially for nasal congestion. Common options: beclometasone, fluticasone, mometasone, budesonide. Counsel on correct technique (head slightly forward, spray away from septum) and that maximum effect takes 1–2 weeks of regular use. Oral non-sedating antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) are first-line for mild/intermittent. Severe cases may need both, intranasal antihistamine (azelastine), or referral for immunotherapy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 60-year-old man presents with sudden-onset unilateral painless complete vision loss in his left eye lasting 30 minutes which then fully resolved. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Which is the most likely diagnosis and immediate action?
option_b: amaurosis fugax (transient retinal ischaemia) — urgent same-day TIA assessment in suspected stroke pathway; aspirin 300 mg STAT, refer to TIA clinic
correct_answer: amaurosis fugax (transient retinal ischaemia) — urgent same-day TIA assessment in suspected stroke pathway; aspirin 300 mg STAT, refer to TIA clinic
explanation: Per NICE NG128 and Royal College of Physicians stroke guidelines: amaurosis fugax (transient monocular vision loss, classically described as a 'curtain coming down') is a form of TIA affecting the ophthalmic/retinal artery — usually embolic from carotid or cardiac source. Requires urgent assessment in TIA pathway (within 24 hours): aspirin 300 mg STAT (then 75 mg OD), TIA clinic (urgent carotid Doppler, ECG, brain imaging, etc.). Risk factors as for any TIA/stroke. Differential includes giant cell arteritis in older patients (urgent ESR/CRP, temporal artery biopsy, steroid).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 68-year-old woman attends for diabetic retinopathy screening. The pharmacist asks about the National Diabetic Eye Screening Programme. Per NICE NG17/28, what is the recommended screening interval for a patient with type 2 diabetes and NO retinopathy?
option_c: every 2 years (in some areas if low-risk per NHS criteria) but annual is the most common standard
correct_answer: every 2 years (in some areas if low-risk per NHS criteria) but annual is the most common standard
explanation: Per NHS Diabetic Eye Screening Programme: all patients aged 12+ with diabetes are invited annually for retinal screening to detect diabetic retinopathy/maculopathy. From 2023, some areas have extended to every 2 years for low-risk patients (two consecutive negative screens, type 2, no other concerns). This is being rolled out nationally. The aim is early detection and treatment to prevent vision loss (laser, anti-VEGF). Pharmacy plays a role in encouraging attendance and managing glycaemic control to slow progression.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 30-year-old man presents with sudden onset bilateral painful, red, gritty eyes after using a chemical cleaner without eye protection. Splash to both eyes occurred 10 minutes ago. Which is the most appropriate immediate action?
