option_b: immediate copious irrigation of both eyes with water or saline for at least 15–30 minutes; urgent A&E referral for further assessment and pH testing
correct_answer: immediate copious irrigation of both eyes with water or saline for at least 15–30 minutes; urgent A&E referral for further assessment and pH testing
explanation: Per Royal College of Ophthalmologists and emergency guidance: chemical eye injury is a true emergency. First action is immediate, copious irrigation (water if no saline) for at least 15–30 minutes — DO NOT delay to find specific antidote. Alkali burns are particularly serious (penetrate deeper than acid). Urgent A&E referral, pH testing of conjunctival sac, ophthalmology assessment. Continue irrigation until pH neutralised. Delay in irrigation is a major determinant of poor outcome.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 7-year-old child has hoarseness and 'barking' cough that started overnight along with inspiratory stridor. The child is alert, no drooling, no respiratory distress at rest. Per NICE CKS (croup), which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose (alternative: oral prednisolone) — most cases can be managed at home; supportive care; safety-net with red flags for return
correct_answer: oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose (alternative: oral prednisolone) — most cases can be managed at home; supportive care; safety-net with red flags for return
explanation: Per NICE CKS (croup): croup (laryngotracheobronchitis, usually parainfluenza) presents with barking cough, hoarseness, stridor — typically worse at night. Mild-moderate cases managed at home or with single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (or prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg). Severe cases (significant stridor at rest, respiratory distress) need hospital admission and may need nebulised adrenaline. Safety-net advice for parents: return if increased work of breathing, drooling, drowsiness, going blue — could indicate complications or alternative diagnosis like epiglottitis or foreign body.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 50-year-old man has a 6-week history of one-sided nasal blockage and intermittent bleeding from the right nostril. He smokes. He has had no recent trauma. Per NICE NG12, which is the most appropriate action?
option_e: urgent (2-week wait) ENT referral for suspected nasal/sinus cancer
correct_answer: urgent (2-week wait) ENT referral for suspected nasal/sinus cancer
explanation: Per NICE NG12 (suspected cancer recognition): unilateral nasal symptoms (especially blockage and bleeding) persisting >3 weeks, especially in smokers, warrant urgent (2-week wait) ENT referral to exclude nasal/sinus malignancy. Other ENT 2WW indications: persistent unexplained neck lump, persistent hoarseness >3 weeks, persistent unexplained mouth ulcer >3 weeks, persistent unilateral discharge/middle ear effusion in adults. Always treat unilateral persistent symptoms with high index of suspicion in adults.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
topic_title: Eye, Ear, Nose and Oropharynx Scenarios
question_text: A 25-year-old woman has had recurrent nosebleeds over 6 weeks. They are spontaneous, last 5–10 minutes, but recur. Examination shows Little's area is friable. Per NICE CKS (epistaxis), which is the most appropriate first-line management?
option_a: cautery (silver nitrate or electrocautery) of bleeding point at Little area, with prior local anaesthetic; counsel on first-aid (pinch soft part of nose, lean forward, ice on neck) and avoid nose picking, vigorous blowing
correct_answer: cautery (silver nitrate or electrocautery) of bleeding point at Little area, with prior local anaesthetic; counsel on first-aid (pinch soft part of nose, lean forward, ice on neck) and avoid nose picking, vigorous blowing
explanation: Per NICE CKS (epistaxis): recurrent anterior epistaxis is usually from Little area (Kiesselbach plexus) where vessels are superficial. Management: identify bleeding point, cauterise (silver nitrate stick under local anaesthetic) - typically by ENT/GP with appropriate training. Counsel on first-aid: pinch soft part of nose for 10-15 minutes, lean forward (not back), ice on bridge of nose/neck. Avoid hot drinks/exercise after bleed for 24h. Posterior nosebleeds (severe, brisk, posterior pharynx) need urgent ENT (posterior packing). Check FBC, coagulation if recurrent/severe.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 13-year-old girl has been offered a human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine as part of the routine UK immunisation schedule. Which disease does this vaccine primarily protect against?
correct_answer: cervical cancer
explanation: Per UK Green Book: the HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9 in the UK schedule since 2021) protects against HPV strains responsible for cervical, anal, vulval, vaginal, penile and oropharyngeal cancers, and genital warts (types 6 and 11). The primary public health rationale for the schedule was prevention of cervical cancer. Since 2019, the programme is universal (both boys and girls aged 12–13 years). The schedule was reduced to a single dose in 2023 for those under 25 based on equivalent efficacy data.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-month-old infant is brought for routine immunisation. Per the current UK childhood immunisation schedule, which vaccines are given at the 12–13 month visit?
option_c: Hib/MenC, MMR, pneumococcal (PCV13) booster, MenB booster
correct_answer: Hib/MenC, MMR, pneumococcal (PCV13) booster, MenB booster
explanation: Per UK Green Book and NHS childhood immunisation schedule (2024 update): the 12-13 month vaccinations include Hib/MenC combined, first dose of MMR (measles, mumps, rubella), PCV13 booster (pneumococcal conjugate), and MenB booster. Earlier schedule (8 weeks, 12 weeks, 16 weeks) includes 6-in-1 (DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB), MenB, rotavirus, PCV13. The schedule continues with preschool boosters at 3y4m and adolescent vaccinations.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old woman with COPD attends the pharmacy. She has not had her flu vaccine yet this year. Per the UK Green Book and NHS immunisation programme, which annual influenza vaccine should be offered to her?
option_a: enhanced (high-dose or adjuvanted) inactivated influenza vaccine — recommended for adults aged 65+
option_b: live attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV nasal spray) — only suitable for children 2–17
correct_answer: enhanced (high-dose or adjuvanted) inactivated influenza vaccine — recommended for adults aged 65+
explanation: Per UK Green Book and NHS England flu programme: for adults aged 65+, an enhanced flu vaccine is recommended — either high-dose quadrivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (QIV-HD), adjuvanted QIV (aQIV), or recombinant QIV (QIVr). For 18–64 years in at-risk groups: cell-based (QIVc) or standard QIV. For children 2–17 years: live attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV) nasal spray (Fluenz Tetra) is preferred. Community pharmacies deliver a large share of flu vaccinations through the NHS service.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75-year-old man with chronic kidney disease attends for pneumococcal vaccination. Per the UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine 23-valent (PPV23) — single dose for adults aged 65+ (and at-risk groups); revaccination only in certain high-risk groups
correct_answer: Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine 23-valent (PPV23) — single dose for adults aged 65+ (and at-risk groups); revaccination only in certain high-risk groups
explanation: Per UK Green Book: PPV23 (Pneumovax 23) is offered as a single dose to all adults aged 65+ and to those in clinical at-risk groups (chronic respiratory, cardiac, renal, liver, neurological disease, diabetes, immunosuppression, asplenia, cochlear implant, CSF leak). Revaccination every 5 years is only recommended for those at highest risk (asplenia, splenic dysfunction, CKD, immunosuppression). PCV13 (conjugate vaccine) is given to children in the routine schedule and to selected high-risk adults.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation attends the pharmacy. Per the UK Green Book, which vaccines are routinely offered during pregnancy?
option_a: inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester) and pertussis (whooping cough)-containing vaccine (typically offered from week 16, optimally 20–32 weeks); also RSV vaccine from 28 weeks gestation (new programme 2024)
correct_answer: inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester) and pertussis (whooping cough)-containing vaccine (typically offered from week 16, optimally 20–32 weeks); also RSV vaccine from 28 weeks gestation (new programme 2024)
explanation: Per UK Green Book: pregnant women are offered inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester) and pertussis vaccine (typically Boostrix-IPV; from week 16, optimal 20–32 weeks) — protects the newborn before they receive their own infant vaccinations. As of August/September 2024, a new RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, maternal) is offered to pregnant women from 28 weeks gestation to protect newborns against respiratory syncytial virus bronchiolitis. Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever) are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 19-year-old university student presents asking about vaccinations needed before going to university. Per UK Green Book, which vaccines should be offered/checked?
option_b: MMR (catch-up if not received 2 doses); MenACWY for those aged ≤25 attending university for the first time; check pneumococcal, hepatitis B if at-risk; influenza if at-risk; HPV catch-up if eligible
correct_answer: MMR (catch-up if not received 2 doses); MenACWY for those aged ≤25 attending university for the first time; check pneumococcal, hepatitis B if at-risk; influenza if at-risk; HPV catch-up if eligible
explanation: Per UK Green Book and Universities UK guidance: 'fresher's flu' isn't influenza but multiple infections among new students. Key vaccinations: MenACWY vaccine (Nimenrix or Menveo) for all aged 14–25 starting university for the first time — meningococcal disease risk is elevated in this group. MMR catch-up if no documentation of 2 doses (measles outbreaks linked to universities). Influenza, hepatitis B (lifestyle/healthcare students), HPV catch-up for those under 25 who missed school programme.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman attends the pharmacy. Per the UK Green Book and NHS shingles programme (2024 update), is she eligible for shingles vaccination?
option_b: no, shingles vaccine is now restricted to immunocompromised patients only
option_c: yes, she is eligible — shingles vaccine (Shingrix, 2-dose recombinant) offered to immunocompetent adults aged 65 and 70–79 (programme expanding to age 60–65 over time)
option_d: yes, only if she has previously had shingles
correct_answer: yes, she is eligible — shingles vaccine (Shingrix, 2-dose recombinant) offered to immunocompetent adults aged 65 and 70–79 (programme expanding to age 60–65 over time)
explanation: Per UK Green Book and the revised NHS shingles programme (September 2023 onwards): Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine, 2 doses 8 weeks–6 months apart) has replaced Zostavax (live attenuated). Eligibility (immunocompetent): turning 65 on/after 1 Sept 2023, and those aged 70–79. The programme is being phased in down to age 60 by 2033. Immunocompromised adults: eligible from age 50. Shingrix is safe and recommended in immunocompromised patients (unlike the previous live vaccine).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with severe egg allergy (anaphylaxis to egg) asks about flu vaccination. Per UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate advice?
option_a: no flu vaccine is safe — avoid
option_b: offer egg-free (cell-based) influenza vaccine (e.g. QIVc, Flucelvax Tetra) or recombinant vaccine (QIVr) — both manufactured without eggs
option_e: refer to immunology — no flu vaccine ever
correct_answer: offer egg-free (cell-based) influenza vaccine (e.g. QIVc, Flucelvax Tetra) or recombinant vaccine (QIVr) — both manufactured without eggs
explanation: Per UK Green Book influenza chapter: most inactivated flu vaccines (made in eggs) contain ovalbumin <0.12 micrograms/mL and can be safely given to patients with egg allergy in primary care (except those with severe egg allergy with anaphylaxis — specialist or hospital setting). However, egg-free options exist: cell-based vaccines (Flucelvax Tetra/QIVc, made in mammalian cells) and recombinant vaccines (Supemtek/QIVr) — these have NO egg protein and are fully suitable for egg-allergic patients including those with anaphylaxis history.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman is planning a trip to East Africa for 4 weeks. She will be travelling to rural areas. Per the travel health Green Book chapter and NaTHNaC, which vaccines should be considered?
option_a: yellow fever certificate (if entering yellow fever risk country, also legal requirement for entry to some countries), hepatitis A, typhoid; consider hepatitis B, rabies, meningococcal ACWY (if relevant area/season); check routine immunisations and tetanus
correct_answer: yellow fever certificate (if entering yellow fever risk country, also legal requirement for entry to some countries), hepatitis A, typhoid; consider hepatitis B, rabies, meningococcal ACWY (if relevant area/season); check routine immunisations and tetanus
explanation: Per UK Green Book travel chapter, NaTHNaC (TravelHealthPro), and Fitfortravel: travel vaccinations depend on destination, duration, activities, traveller age/health. Common considerations for East Africa: yellow fever (legal requirement for many countries), hepatitis A and typhoid (food/water), hepatitis B (>1 month or higher risk), rabies (pre-exposure for rural travel), MenACWY (Sahel and certain countries Dec-June). Malaria chemoprophylaxis (mefloquine, doxycycline, atovaquone/proguanil) is essential. Travel pharmacy services and NaTHNaC country profiles guide specifics.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman has just been bitten by a stray dog while travelling in India. The wound is bleeding. She received pre-exposure rabies vaccination 3 years ago (3-dose series). She is otherwise well. Per UK Green Book, what is the most appropriate post-exposure management?
option_a: immediate thorough wound washing (15 minutes with soap and water) and disinfection; two rabies post-exposure booster doses (day 0 and day 3); rabies immunoglobulin NOT required as she has had pre-exposure prophylaxis
correct_answer: immediate thorough wound washing (15 minutes with soap and water) and disinfection; two rabies post-exposure booster doses (day 0 and day 3); rabies immunoglobulin NOT required as she has had pre-exposure prophylaxis
explanation: Per UK Green Book rabies chapter and WHO: for patients with prior pre-exposure rabies vaccination, post-exposure prophylaxis is simplified — 2 booster doses on days 0 and 3 only. Human rabies immunoglobulin (HRIG) is NOT required. For unvaccinated patients: 4–5 doses plus HRIG (infiltrated around wound). Immediate wound care is the most important step — washing for 15 minutes with soap/water reduces viral load significantly. Tetanus and antibiotics may also be needed. Rabies is virtually 100% fatal once symptomatic — prevention is essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1-year-old child has received all routine vaccinations on schedule. The parents enquire about chickenpox vaccine, which is not routine in the UK NHS schedule. Per UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate information?
option_b: contact a GP who can prescribe it but it is generally only available privately for healthy children; routine NHS provision is for non-immune household contacts of immunocompromised individuals, and certain occupational groups (e.g. healthcare workers)
option_c: contact a GP — it is part of the routine schedule
correct_answer: contact a GP who can prescribe it but it is generally only available privately for healthy children; routine NHS provision is for non-immune household contacts of immunocompromised individuals, and certain occupational groups (e.g. healthcare workers)
explanation: Per UK Green Book varicella chapter: varicella (chickenpox) vaccine (Varivax) is not part of the routine UK childhood immunisation schedule — partly due to concerns about shifting age of infection. JCVI recommended introduction in November 2023 but timing of implementation is pending. NHS provision is currently restricted to non-immune household contacts of immunocompromised patients, and non-immune at-risk healthcare workers (post-exposure or pre-exposure). Other healthy children can access privately. This is a 2-dose live attenuated vaccine — contraindicated in immunocompromised and pregnancy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is being given an MMR vaccine in the pharmacy. Per UK Green Book, which of the following is a true contraindication (rather than a precaution)?
option_b: being unwell with a mild cold
option_c: confirmed anaphylaxis to a previous dose of MMR or any component (e.g. neomycin, gelatin); pregnancy; severe immunosuppression
correct_answer: confirmed anaphylaxis to a previous dose of MMR or any component (e.g. neomycin, gelatin); pregnancy; severe immunosuppression
explanation: Per UK Green Book: true contraindications to MMR (a live attenuated vaccine) are: (1) confirmed anaphylactic reaction to a previous dose or to a vaccine component (neomycin, gelatin); (2) pregnancy (avoid pregnancy for 1 month after MMR); (3) significant immunosuppression — e.g. high-dose corticosteroids (>40 mg/day prednisolone for >7 days), chemotherapy, untreated HIV with low CD4, primary immunodeficiency. EGG allergy is NOT a contraindication to MMR (different from yellow fever, influenza). Mild illness is a precaution but not contraindication.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 8-week-old infant is brought to the pharmacy for the first set of routine vaccinations. The pharmacist confirms the infant is well and ready to vaccinate. Per UK Green Book, which vaccines are given at 8 weeks?
correct_answer: 6-in-1 (DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB), MenB, rotavirus
explanation: Per UK Green Book and NHS childhood immunisation schedule: 8-week schedule is 6-in-1 (Infanrix Hexa or Vaxelis: diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, polio, Hib, hep B) + MenB (Bexsero) + rotavirus (Rotarix oral). Note: PCV13 was previously given at 8 weeks but moved to 12 weeks in the 2020 schedule revision. The 6-in-1 was introduced in 2017 (incorporating hep B for universal infant coverage). Rotavirus must be given before 15 weeks (not initiated later due to intussusception risk).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old healthcare worker has had a needlestick injury from a known HBV-positive patient. The healthcare worker had a full HBV vaccine course 5 years ago with confirmed anti-HBs >100 mIU/mL. Per UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate post-exposure management?
option_a: booster HBV vaccine dose; HBV immunoglobulin NOT routinely required as she is a documented vaccine responder
correct_answer: booster HBV vaccine dose; HBV immunoglobulin NOT routinely required as she is a documented vaccine responder
explanation: Per UK Green Book hepatitis B chapter and PHE/UKHSA post-exposure guidance: for documented HBV vaccine responders (anti-HBs >100 mIU/mL post-vaccination), the management of significant exposure is a single booster HBV vaccine dose — immunoglobulin is NOT required. For non-responders or unknown response, HBV immunoglobulin AND vaccine series are needed. Also: source HIV/HCV status, baseline bloods (anti-HBs, anti-HBc, anti-HCV, HIV testing), HIV PEP if indicated within 72h, follow-up testing.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 67-year-old man with stable COPD is referred to the pharmacy by his GP for an NHS-funded influenza vaccination. He has anaphylaxis to penicillin in the past. Per UK Green Book and PGD, which is the most appropriate action?
option_a: administer flu vaccine — penicillin allergy is not a contraindication; have anaphylaxis kit available
option_c: give vaccine only with IV access
correct_answer: administer flu vaccine — penicillin allergy is not a contraindication; have anaphylaxis kit available
explanation: Per UK Green Book and NHS PGDs: penicillin allergy is NOT a contraindication to any UK vaccine — vaccines do not contain penicillin. Contraindications are mainly: confirmed anaphylaxis to a previous dose or a specific vaccine component. Standard pre-vaccination check includes acute illness, allergy to vaccine components (rare), pregnancy considerations for live vaccines, immunocompromise for live vaccines. All vaccination settings must have anaphylaxis equipment (adrenaline 1:1000, oxygen, observation capability) and trained personnel.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman is concerned about MMR vaccine and autism after reading online claims. Per scientific consensus and Cochrane reviews, what is the most appropriate response?
option_e: there is no scientifically credible link between MMR vaccine and autism; the 1998 Wakefield paper that suggested this was retracted, fraudulent, and Wakefield was struck off the medical register; large epidemiological studies and Cochrane reviews have confirmed no link
correct_answer: there is no scientifically credible link between MMR vaccine and autism; the 1998 Wakefield paper that suggested this was retracted, fraudulent, and Wakefield was struck off the medical register; large epidemiological studies and Cochrane reviews have confirmed no link
explanation: Per Cochrane systematic reviews, large epidemiological studies (e.g. Hviid et al. 2019, over 650,000 children), GMC, WHO and UK Public Health: there is NO credible scientific evidence of a link between MMR and autism. The 1998 Wakefield paper that initiated the controversy was retracted by The Lancet (2010), found to be fraudulent, and Andrew Wakefield was struck off the UK medical register for misconduct. The pharmacist plays a key role in vaccine confidence — provide evidence-based information, address specific concerns, signpost to NHS resources.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man has been bitten by a fox in a UK rural area. He last had a tetanus booster 12 years ago. Per UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: immediate wound cleaning, tetanus booster (dT/IPV), risk assessment for rabies — UK is rabies-free in foxes (terrestrial mammals) but check bat exposure separately, antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. co-amoxiclav) if high-risk wound (deep, hand, comorbidities)
correct_answer: immediate wound cleaning, tetanus booster (dT/IPV), risk assessment for rabies — UK is rabies-free in foxes (terrestrial mammals) but check bat exposure separately, antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. co-amoxiclav) if high-risk wound (deep, hand, comorbidities)
explanation: Per UK Green Book and NICE NG184 (animal bites): tetanus risk assessment based on time since last booster and wound type — boost if >5–10 years depending on wound contamination. Rabies risk: in the UK, terrestrial mammals (foxes, dogs, etc.) are classified as rabies-free; only bats (specifically European Bat Lyssavirus, EBLV) carry rabies risk in the UK. Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for high-risk bite wounds — typically co-amoxiclav 5–7 days; doxycycline + metronidazole in penicillin allergy. Wound irrigation and debridement essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old woman is being given the first dose of HPV vaccine catch-up. 15 minutes after vaccination she becomes pale, dizzy, sweating and faints momentarily. She recovers within 2 minutes. Per Resuscitation Council UK, which is the most likely cause?
option_d: syncope (vasovagal) — common after vaccination, especially in young adults; recovery typically rapid; observe; reassure; should not preclude future vaccination
correct_answer: syncope (vasovagal) — common after vaccination, especially in young adults; recovery typically rapid; observe; reassure; should not preclude future vaccination
explanation: Per Resuscitation Council UK and immunisation guidance: vasovagal syncope (fainting) is the most common reaction post-vaccination, especially in adolescents and young adults. Features: pre-syncope (pale, sweating, dizzy, nausea) progressing to brief LOC, rapid spontaneous recovery on lying flat. Distinguished from anaphylaxis — which has bronchospasm/airway/circulation features, urticaria, ALL begin within minutes. Management: lay patient flat, elevate legs, reassure, observe. Patients should always be observed for 15 minutes post-vaccination (best done sitting or lying).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 40-year-old healthcare worker is starting work in haemodialysis where she will be exposed to blood. She has completed her primary HBV vaccine course but anti-HBs is 8 mIU/mL (low responder, <10 mIU/mL). Per UK Green Book, which is the most appropriate management?
option_a: additional 3 doses of HBV vaccine (consider double-dose) and recheck anti-HBs; if remains a non-responder, counsel about higher risk and need for HBIG plus vaccine in any future exposure
correct_answer: additional 3 doses of HBV vaccine (consider double-dose) and recheck anti-HBs; if remains a non-responder, counsel about higher risk and need for HBIG plus vaccine in any future exposure
explanation: Per UK Green Book HBV chapter: anti-HBs <10 mIU/mL after primary course indicates non-response. Management: additional 3 doses of HBV vaccine (consider double-dose Engerix B 40 micrograms or Fendrix), retest 1-2 months after final dose. About 50% of non-responders respond to additional doses. Persistent non-responders (after 6 total doses) are counselled — they require HBIG plus vaccine for any significant exposure. Healthcare workers handling blood need documented HBV immunity.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 19-year-old man received his second dose of Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine yesterday and now reports chest pain, shortness of breath and palpitations. He has no fever. Per MHRA and JCVI, which is the most likely concern and action?
option_a: myocarditis/pericarditis (rare side effect particularly in young men post mRNA vaccine, more common after second dose) — urgent A&E assessment for ECG, troponin and cardiology input
correct_answer: myocarditis/pericarditis (rare side effect particularly in young men post mRNA vaccine, more common after second dose) — urgent A&E assessment for ECG, troponin and cardiology input
explanation: Per MHRA Drug Safety Update and JCVI: myocarditis and pericarditis are rare but recognised adverse effects of mRNA COVID-19 vaccines (Pfizer, Moderna) — more common in young males (12–29 years), typically after the second dose, within 14 days. Presentation: chest pain, breathlessness, palpitations. Urgent A&E assessment for ECG, troponin, echocardiogram. Most cases are mild and self-limiting with NSAIDs/colchicine. Patients should be counselled to seek immediate help for chest pain/breathlessness post-vaccination.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old woman has just been diagnosed with stage III breast cancer (ER+, HER2-, premenopausal). After surgery, the oncologist plans adjuvant endocrine therapy. Per NICE NG101, which is the most appropriate first-line endocrine therapy?
option_e: tamoxifen 20 mg OD for 5 years (or 10 years extended therapy for higher-risk patients), with consideration of ovarian suppression in higher-risk pre-menopausal patients
correct_answer: tamoxifen 20 mg OD for 5 years (or 10 years extended therapy for higher-risk patients), with consideration of ovarian suppression in higher-risk pre-menopausal patients
explanation: Per NICE NG101 (early breast cancer): for ER-positive pre-menopausal breast cancer, tamoxifen (selective oestrogen receptor modulator, SERM) 20 mg OD for 5 years is standard adjuvant. Extended therapy to 10 years for higher-risk patients reduces recurrence further. For pre-menopausal women at higher risk, ovarian function suppression (GnRH agonist like goserelin) + aromatase inhibitor or + tamoxifen may be used. Aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole) are first-line for POST-menopausal patients (don't work in functioning ovaries without ovarian suppression).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man receiving the third cycle of FOLFOX chemotherapy for colorectal cancer presents to A&E with a temperature of 38.5°C. He looks well but reports rigors. His full blood count shows neutrophils 0.3 × 10⁹/L. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_b: immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam) — neutropenic sepsis is an oncological emergency, do NOT delay for cultures
correct_answer: immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam) — neutropenic sepsis is an oncological emergency, do NOT delay for cultures
explanation: Per NICE NG12, NG143 (sepsis), and the UK Acute Oncology Society: neutropenic sepsis (T >38°C with neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L, or <1.0 with falling trend, in a patient on chemotherapy) is a life-threatening oncological emergency. Empirical IV broad-spectrum antibiotics MUST be given within 1 hour of admission, regardless of clinical state (Sepsis Six bundle). First-line empirical agent: piperacillin-tazobactam (or meropenem if penicillin-allergic). Cultures should be taken BEFORE antibiotics but should NOT delay administration. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) may be considered. All cancer patients on chemotherapy should carry an 'alert card' with this information.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is on letrozole (aromatase inhibitor). Per BNF, what additional supportive measure should be considered?
option_b: bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonate or denosumab) and DEXA monitoring — aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss in postmenopausal women
correct_answer: bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonate or denosumab) and DEXA monitoring — aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss in postmenopausal women
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG101: aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) profoundly suppress oestrogen, accelerating bone loss in postmenopausal women — increasing fracture risk. Per the National Osteoporosis Guideline Group, bone health assessment (DEXA at baseline and every 2 years) and prophylactic measures: adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, lifestyle (weight-bearing exercise, smoking/alcohol reduction), and bone-protective drugs (oral bisphosphonate or denosumab or zoledronic acid IV) per fracture risk.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old patient with renal transplant has been on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisolone for 6 years. He attends the pharmacy with a non-healing skin lesion on his face — pearly papule with central ulceration. Which is the most likely concern?
option_b: BCC or SCC — chronic immunosuppression significantly increases skin cancer risk; urgent dermatology referral
correct_answer: BCC or SCC — chronic immunosuppression significantly increases skin cancer risk; urgent dermatology referral
explanation: Per BAD and transplant immunosuppression guidance: long-term immunosuppression (especially calcineurin inhibitors like tacrolimus/ciclosporin) substantially increases skin cancer risk — SCC and BCC, also malignant melanoma. SCC risk is up to 100x higher in transplant patients than general population. Annual dermatology surveillance is recommended for transplant patients. Pearly papule with telangiectasia and central ulceration suggests BCC; persistent scaly lesion suggests SCC. Urgent dermatology referral required. Patients counselled on rigorous sun protection.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 40-year-old woman with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma is starting bortezomib-based induction therapy. Per BNF and MHRA, which is the most important counselling point related to bortezomib?
option_b: herpes zoster reactivation risk — antiviral prophylaxis (aciclovir or valaciclovir) is standard; also peripheral neuropathy, GI upset, and thrombocytopenia are common ADRs
correct_answer: herpes zoster reactivation risk — antiviral prophylaxis (aciclovir or valaciclovir) is standard; also peripheral neuropathy, GI upset, and thrombocytopenia are common ADRs
explanation: Per BNF and BSH myeloma guidelines: bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor) is associated with reactivation of varicella-zoster virus (herpes zoster/shingles) — prophylactic aciclovir 400 mg BD or valaciclovir 500 mg OD recommended throughout treatment. Other ADRs: peripheral neuropathy (often dose-limiting, subcutaneous reduces incidence), thrombocytopenia, GI upset, fatigue, increased infection risk. Patients on myeloma therapy frequently also need VTE prophylaxis (especially with IMiD-containing regimens like lenalidomide).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 72-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer is started on enzalutamide. Per NICE TA377 and BNF, which is a key counselling point?
option_a: can cause seizures (1% incidence); avoid in patients with seizure history; also fatigue, hot flushes, hypertension, fall/fracture risk; CYP3A4 inducer (drug interactions)
correct_answer: can cause seizures (1% incidence); avoid in patients with seizure history; also fatigue, hot flushes, hypertension, fall/fracture risk; CYP3A4 inducer (drug interactions)
explanation: Per BNF and SmPC enzalutamide: enzalutamide is an androgen receptor antagonist used in metastatic prostate cancer. Counselling: seizure risk (~1%) — contraindicated in patients with prior seizures or risk factors; falls and fractures (counsel on fall prevention); fatigue (very common); hot flushes; hypertension (monitor BP); cognitive effects; PRES (rare). Significant interactions: enzalutamide is a CYP3A4 inducer — reduces concentrations of many co-administered drugs including warfarin (monitor INR), some antiepileptics, contraceptives.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with newly diagnosed Hodgkin lymphoma is starting ABVD chemotherapy (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine). Per BSH and BNF, which is the most important counselling point about bleomycin?
option_d: pulmonary toxicity (interstitial pneumonitis/fibrosis) — risk increased by cumulative dose, age >40, smoking, high inspired oxygen — long-lasting risk of harm from high-FiO₂ general anaesthesia even after treatment ends; baseline and ongoing lung function tests
correct_answer: pulmonary toxicity (interstitial pneumonitis/fibrosis) — risk increased by cumulative dose, age >40, smoking, high inspired oxygen — long-lasting risk of harm from high-FiO₂ general anaesthesia even after treatment ends; baseline and ongoing lung function tests
explanation: Per BNF and BSH Hodgkin lymphoma guidelines: bleomycin is associated with pulmonary toxicity (interstitial pneumonitis, fibrosis) — can be fatal. Risk factors: cumulative dose >300 units, age >40, smoking, renal impairment, high FiO₂ (especially during general anaesthesia — surgery anaesthetists must know about bleomycin exposure for life). Monitoring: baseline and serial lung function tests, careful clinical assessment. Patient should always inform anaesthetists about prior bleomycin and ideally carry a bleomycin alert card. Other ABVD ADRs: doxorubicin cardiotoxicity, vinblastine neuropathy.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man with chronic myeloid leukaemia is taking imatinib 400 mg OD. He attends with severe oedema, dry cough, and a recent BP reading of 170/100 mmHg. Which is the most likely cause?
option_b: imatinib-induced fluid retention/oedema and possible hypertension — common ADRs (and a recognised cardiac concern); dose review and additional supportive care needed
correct_answer: imatinib-induced fluid retention/oedema and possible hypertension — common ADRs (and a recognised cardiac concern); dose review and additional supportive care needed
explanation: Per BNF and BSH CML guidelines: imatinib (BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor) commonly causes fluid retention (peripheral oedema, periorbital, pulmonary), which can be significant — diuretics may be needed; cardiac toxicity is also recognised (consider ECG, cardiac monitoring). Other ADRs: nausea, muscle cramps, rash, cytopenias, transaminitis. Newer-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib, ponatinib) have different toxicity profiles — e.g. dasatinib pleural effusion, nilotinib cardiovascular events, ponatinib thrombosis.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man with metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer is starting pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor) immunotherapy. Per BNF and MHRA, which is the most important counselling point?
option_b: immune-related adverse events (irAEs) can affect any organ — pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal, pituitary, diabetes), nephritis, dermatitis; can occur at any time, sometimes after stopping; report new symptoms urgently — treatment typically with corticosteroids
correct_answer: immune-related adverse events (irAEs) can affect any organ — pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal, pituitary, diabetes), nephritis, dermatitis; can occur at any time, sometimes after stopping; report new symptoms urgently — treatment typically with corticosteroids
explanation: Per BNF, MHRA and oncology guidance: checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, ipilimumab) can cause immune-related adverse events affecting almost any organ. Common: pneumonitis (any new dyspnoea/cough — urgent CT), colitis (diarrhoea — withhold drug, steroids), hepatitis, endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism/hyperthyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, hypophysitis, type 1 diabetes), dermatitis, nephritis. Critical: irAEs can occur at any time (during treatment or even months after stopping). Patients carry alert cards. Management typically high-dose corticosteroids ± immunosuppressants for severe/refractory cases.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 38-year-old woman with breast cancer is taking ondansetron 8 mg PRN for chemotherapy-induced nausea. She becomes constipated after several doses. Per BNF, which counselling point is most appropriate?
option_a: constipation is a common ADR of 5HT3 antagonists — encourage hydration, fibre, and consider osmotic laxative (e.g. lactulose, macrogol) prophylactically
correct_answer: constipation is a common ADR of 5HT3 antagonists — encourage hydration, fibre, and consider osmotic laxative (e.g. lactulose, macrogol) prophylactically
explanation: Per BNF: 5HT3 antagonists (ondansetron, granisetron, palonosetron) commonly cause constipation and headache. Counselling: hydration, dietary fibre, prophylactic laxative (lactulose, macrogol) in patients prone to constipation. QT prolongation is a recognised but uncommon risk (especially with higher doses) — caution with other QT-prolonging drugs. Other antiemetics for chemotherapy-induced nausea: NK1 antagonists (aprepitant, fosaprepitant), dexamethasone, olanzapine, metoclopramide — used in combination based on emetogenic potential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old patient on cyclophosphamide chemotherapy presents with haematuria. Per BNF and BSH, which is the most likely cause and prevention?
option_a: acrolein-induced haemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide metabolite) — mesna (sodium 2-mercaptoethanesulfonate) co-administration with high-dose cyclophosphamide or ifosfamide, plus hydration, prevents this
correct_answer: acrolein-induced haemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide metabolite) — mesna (sodium 2-mercaptoethanesulfonate) co-administration with high-dose cyclophosphamide or ifosfamide, plus hydration, prevents this
explanation: Per BNF and BSH: cyclophosphamide and ifosfamide are metabolised to acrolein, which causes haemorrhagic cystitis (severe bladder irritation, haematuria). Prevention: mesna (binds acrolein in bladder, neutralising it), and IV hydration. Mesna is essential with high-dose IV cyclophosphamide and standard with all ifosfamide. Counsel patients to maintain high fluid intake and empty bladder frequently. Long-term cyclophosphamide also increases bladder cancer risk — surveillance for haematuria/cystoscopy in long-term users.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man with metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving fluorouracil (5-FU) chemotherapy. He develops severe diarrhoea, mucositis, and pancytopenia after the first dose. Per BNF, which is the most likely cause?
option_b: dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency — DPD metabolises 5-FU/capecitabine; DPD deficiency leads to severe toxicity; DPD testing (DPYD genotype) is now standard before treatment
correct_answer: dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency — DPD metabolises 5-FU/capecitabine; DPD deficiency leads to severe toxicity; DPD testing (DPYD genotype) is now standard before treatment
explanation: Per MHRA (2020) and NICE: DPD enzyme deficiency (homozygous: 0.1%, heterozygous: ~3-5% of UK population) causes severe and potentially fatal toxicity from fluoropyrimidines (5-FU, capecitabine, tegafur). DPD testing (DPYD variant genotyping) is now recommended before initiation per MHRA. Patients with deficiency may need dose reduction, alternative therapy, or close monitoring. Symptoms of toxicity: severe mucositis, diarrhoea, neutropenia, hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine). Uridine triacetate is an emergency antidote available for severe overdose/toxicity.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old woman is receiving doxorubicin as part of her chemotherapy. Per BNF, what is the most important long-term safety consideration?
option_a: cardiotoxicity (cumulative dose-related — risk increases above ~450 mg/m²) — including dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure; baseline and serial echocardiograms recommended; dexrazoxane is cardioprotective in high-risk patients
correct_answer: cardiotoxicity (cumulative dose-related — risk increases above ~450 mg/m²) — including dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure; baseline and serial echocardiograms recommended; dexrazoxane is cardioprotective in high-risk patients
explanation: Per BNF and BSH: anthracyclines (doxorubicin, epirubicin, daunorubicin) are dose-dependent cardiotoxic — risk of irreversible dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure increases significantly above lifetime cumulative doses (doxorubicin ~450 mg/m², epirubicin ~900 mg/m²). Pre-treatment cardiac assessment (LVEF on echo or MUGA), monitoring during/after. Dexrazoxane (iron chelator) is cardioprotective and can be given with high-risk regimens. Other long-term ADRs: secondary leukaemia (rare), infertility (especially with combination regimens including alkylating agents).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man receiving allopurinol for tumour lysis syndrome prevention while being treated for ALL also takes azathioprine for inflammatory bowel disease. Per BNF, which is the major concern?
option_a: allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase, which is required to metabolise azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine — co-administration significantly increases azathioprine levels and toxicity (myelosuppression); the azathioprine dose must be reduced to 25% of the usual dose, or an alternative immunosuppressant considered
correct_answer: allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase, which is required to metabolise azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine — co-administration significantly increases azathioprine levels and toxicity (myelosuppression); the azathioprine dose must be reduced to 25% of the usual dose, or an alternative immunosuppressant considered
explanation: Per BNF interactions appendix: this is a classic, severe interaction. Azathioprine is metabolised to 6-mercaptopurine then to inactive metabolites by xanthine oxidase. Allopurinol blocks XO, accumulating active metabolites — potentially fatal myelosuppression. If unavoidable, reduce azathioprine to 25% of usual dose with close FBC monitoring. Alternatives: febuxostat has similar XO-inhibiting effect; switch azathioprine to a different immunosuppressant. Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) doesn't have this interaction.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukaemia is starting induction chemotherapy. The team initiates allopurinol and aggressive hydration before treatment. Per BSH and BNF, what is the rationale?
option_b: tumour lysis syndrome prophylaxis (hyperuricaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, AKI) from rapid leukaemic cell breakdown — allopurinol (or rasburicase if very high-risk) plus IV hydration reduce uric acid burden and prevent acute kidney injury
correct_answer: tumour lysis syndrome prophylaxis (hyperuricaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, AKI) from rapid leukaemic cell breakdown — allopurinol (or rasburicase if very high-risk) plus IV hydration reduce uric acid burden and prevent acute kidney injury
explanation: Per BSH and BNF: tumour lysis syndrome is a major risk in haematological malignancies with high tumour burden (acute leukaemias, high-grade lymphomas) at chemotherapy initiation. Prevention: aggressive IV hydration (2–3 L/m²/day) + allopurinol 100–300 mg OD starting 24–48h before chemotherapy. For very high-risk patients (very high WCC, bulky tumour, raised LDH): rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) 0.2 mg/kg IV — rapidly degrades uric acid. Monitor U&Es, uric acid, phosphate, calcium, LDH closely during induction.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is taking palbociclib (CDK4/6 inhibitor) plus letrozole. She has been started on a 6-week course of oral fluconazole 50 mg OD for severe candidiasis. Per BNF and SmPC, which is the major concern?
option_a: fluconazole is a CYP3A4 inhibitor — significantly increases palbociclib levels with increased risk of severe neutropenia and other toxicity; dose adjustment or alternative antifungal (e.g. micafungin) needed
correct_answer: fluconazole is a CYP3A4 inhibitor — significantly increases palbociclib levels with increased risk of severe neutropenia and other toxicity; dose adjustment or alternative antifungal (e.g. micafungin) needed
explanation: Per BNF and SmPC palbociclib: palbociclib is a major CYP3A4 substrate. Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, fluconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, grapefruit) significantly increase exposure — palbociclib dose should be reduced or alternative non-interacting antifungal/antibiotic used. Conversely, strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St John's wort) reduce palbociclib levels — avoid. Counsel patients on grapefruit avoidance. Similar interactions apply to ribociclib and abemaciclib.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old man on long-term ciclosporin for renal transplant is started on simvastatin for high cholesterol. Per BNF, which is the major concern?
option_a: ciclosporin is a CYP3A4 inhibitor and increases simvastatin levels — significantly increased risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis; simvastatin should be avoided or used at very low dose, OR an alternative statin (e.g. fluvastatin or low-dose pravastatin) used
correct_answer: ciclosporin is a CYP3A4 inhibitor and increases simvastatin levels — significantly increased risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis; simvastatin should be avoided or used at very low dose, OR an alternative statin (e.g. fluvastatin or low-dose pravastatin) used
explanation: Per BNF and SmPC simvastatin: ciclosporin is a CYP3A4 and OATP1B1 inhibitor — significantly increases statin levels and risk of rhabdomyolysis. Simvastatin contraindicated with ciclosporin (per SmPC). Atorvastatin: lower-dose only, increased risk. Preferred statins: fluvastatin, pravastatin, rosuvastatin (lower-dose) — less CYP3A4 dependence. Counsel: any muscle pain, weakness or dark urine — stop statin and seek medical advice, check CK and renal function.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old woman is receiving end-of-life care with metastatic cancer. She is in pain (oral morphine 30 mg every 4 hours), and is unable to swallow. Per BNF and Palliative Care Formulary, what is the appropriate SC daily dose of morphine for a continuous subcutaneous infusion (CSCI) via syringe driver?
option_c: 90 mg (oral 24-hour dose = 30 mg × 6 = 180 mg; convert PO→SC by dividing by 2 = 90 mg SC over 24h)
correct_answer: 90 mg (oral 24-hour dose = 30 mg × 6 = 180 mg; convert PO→SC by dividing by 2 = 90 mg SC over 24h)
explanation: Per BNF and Palliative Care Formulary (PCF): PO to SC morphine conversion ratio is 2:1 (SC is twice as potent as PO due to first-pass). Total 24-hour PO morphine = 30 mg × 6 doses = 180 mg/day. SC equivalent = 180 ÷ 2 = 90 mg SC over 24h via syringe driver. PRN breakthrough doses = 1/6 of 24-hour total = ~15 mg SC PRN. Conversion to other opioids (e.g. diamorphine, oxycodone, fentanyl patch) requires further calculation — diamorphine SC dose = PO morphine ÷ 3. Specialist palliative care input is valuable.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man with rapidly worsening dyspnoea after starting nivolumab (anti-PD-1) for metastatic melanoma 3 weeks ago. He has a non-productive cough. CXR shows new bilateral infiltrates. Which is the most likely diagnosis and management?
option_b: immune-related pneumonitis — withhold nivolumab and start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day); exclude infection; permanent discontinuation if grade ≥2
correct_answer: immune-related pneumonitis — withhold nivolumab and start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day); exclude infection; permanent discontinuation if grade ≥2
explanation: Per BNF, MHRA and oncology guidance: immune checkpoint inhibitor pneumonitis is a serious irAE that can be fatal if missed. Presentation: progressive dyspnoea, cough, hypoxia, often within first weeks-months. CXR/CT shows ground-glass changes or infiltrates (variable patterns). Differential: infection (PCP especially in immunosuppressed), heart failure, progression of malignancy. Management: withhold checkpoint inhibitor, exclude infection (bronchoscopy if needed), corticosteroids (prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day; IV methylprednisolone if severe). Grade 1 may permit rechallenge; ≥2 usually permanent cessation. Infliximab or mycophenolate for steroid-refractory cases.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old woman has just been diagnosed with stage I Hodgkin lymphoma. She wishes to preserve fertility before chemotherapy. Per BSH and NICE guidance, which is the most appropriate fertility consideration?
option_c: referral to fertility specialist BEFORE chemotherapy for oocyte/embryo cryopreservation (or ovarian tissue cryopreservation); alkylating-agent containing regimens (e.g. BEACOPP) substantially impair fertility
correct_answer: referral to fertility specialist BEFORE chemotherapy for oocyte/embryo cryopreservation (or ovarian tissue cryopreservation); alkylating-agent containing regimens (e.g. BEACOPP) substantially impair fertility
explanation: Per BSH, NICE NG12 and BFS: all patients of reproductive age starting cancer treatment that may affect fertility (chemotherapy with alkylating agents, pelvic radiotherapy, or surgery affecting reproductive organs) should be offered fertility preservation discussion BEFORE treatment, with referral to a fertility specialist. Options for women: oocyte or embryo cryopreservation (requires ovarian stimulation, takes 2–3 weeks), ovarian tissue cryopreservation (option if urgent treatment), GnRH agonist co-administration during chemo. For men: sperm cryopreservation. This is funded by NHS for cancer patients per NICE.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22-year-old woman attends the pharmacy 8 hours after taking 32 paracetamol 500 mg tablets in a suicide attempt. She is alert, has no symptoms, weighs 60 kg (total dose 16 g = 267 mg/kg). Per BNF and TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate action?
option_b: advise to drink milk and reassess in 4 hours
option_d: prescribe N-acetylcysteine to take at home
option_e: refer urgently to A&E for assessment and likely N-acetylcysteine infusion
correct_answer: refer urgently to A&E for assessment and likely N-acetylcysteine infusion
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: any paracetamol overdose >75 mg/kg (or staggered overdose) requires assessment at A&E. This patient has ingested 267 mg/kg. Plasma paracetamol must be measured at 4h post-ingestion and plotted on the treatment nomogram. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote and is most effective when given within 8h of ingestion. Activated charcoal is only useful within 1h. The risk of fulminant hepatic failure makes urgent A&E referral essential.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old man taking 5 mg warfarin OD presents with INR 9.5 and no bleeding. Per BNF and NICE NG196, which is the most appropriate management?
option_e: stop warfarin, give phytomenadione (vitamin K1) 1–5 mg PO; restart warfarin when INR <5.0; investigate cause (interaction, illness, dietary changes)
correct_answer: stop warfarin, give phytomenadione (vitamin K1) 1–5 mg PO; restart warfarin when INR <5.0; investigate cause (interaction, illness, dietary changes)
explanation: Per BNF and NICE NG196 warfarin overdose management: INR 5–8 with no bleeding: withhold 1–2 doses, restart at lower dose. INR ≥8 with no bleeding: stop warfarin AND give phytomenadione 1–5 mg PO (preferably orally for non-bleeding cases due to lower risk of anaphylactoid reaction). Recheck INR daily until in range. INR ≥5 with any bleeding: IV phytomenadione + PCC. Investigate cause: missed doses, dose error, drug interaction (antibiotics, amiodarone, SSRIs, NSAIDs), liver dysfunction, alcohol, dietary vitamin K change.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 12-year-old boy is brought to A&E 3 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of his grandmother's iron tablets. He has vomited several times and has abdominal pain. Per TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: desferrioxamine IV — chelates iron; supportive care including IV fluids, oxygen; FBC, U&Es, LFTs, glucose, iron level at 4h; gastric lavage / whole-bowel irrigation considered if large radiopaque ingestion
correct_answer: desferrioxamine IV — chelates iron; supportive care including IV fluids, oxygen; FBC, U&Es, LFTs, glucose, iron level at 4h; gastric lavage / whole-bowel irrigation considered if large radiopaque ingestion
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE iron poisoning: iron toxicity has 5 phases — GI (vomiting, abdo pain, bleeding within 6h), apparent recovery (false reassurance), shock/metabolic acidosis (6–48h), hepatotoxicity, GI strictures. Serum iron at 4–6h post-ingestion guides treatment. Specific antidote: desferrioxamine IV (chelates free iron, excreted as ferrioxamine in urine — turns urine 'rosé wine' colour). Activated charcoal does NOT bind iron. Abdominal X-ray may show radiopaque tablets. Whole-bowel irrigation may be considered for large overdose with tablets visible on X-ray.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 25-year-old man comes to A&E acutely intoxicated with alcohol after a single binge. He has hypoglycaemia, vomiting, ataxia, and reduced GCS. Per TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_c: supportive care: airway/breathing/circulation, IV glucose for hypoglycaemia, thiamine (Pabrinex 1+2 IV) — particularly in chronic alcoholics or malnourished; consider fluid resuscitation; protect airway
correct_answer: supportive care: airway/breathing/circulation, IV glucose for hypoglycaemia, thiamine (Pabrinex 1+2 IV) — particularly in chronic alcoholics or malnourished; consider fluid resuscitation; protect airway
explanation: Per TOXBASE and emergency medicine guidance: acute alcohol intoxication management is largely supportive. Key concerns: airway (risk of aspiration with vomiting + reduced GCS), hypoglycaemia (especially in chronic alcoholics with depleted glycogen), Wernicke's encephalopathy (give IV thiamine BEFORE glucose in malnourished). Pabrinex is high-strength IV thiamine + other B vitamins. Always consider co-ingestion. Severe intoxication may need ICU. Long-term: assess for alcohol dependence, offer specialist input, withdrawal management.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman is brought to A&E 4 hours after taking 60 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets. She has tachycardia, dilated pupils, hyperthermia, hypotension, and an ECG shows widened QRS. Per BNF and TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: specific antidote — none — but IV sodium bicarbonate is given for QRS widening (>100 ms) or significant arrhythmias to alkalinise plasma and reduce free drug; supportive care, monitor ECG; activated charcoal within 1h; benzodiazepines for seizures; intralipid emulsion if cardiac arrest
correct_answer: specific antidote — none — but IV sodium bicarbonate is given for QRS widening (>100 ms) or significant arrhythmias to alkalinise plasma and reduce free drug; supportive care, monitor ECG; activated charcoal within 1h; benzodiazepines for seizures; intralipid emulsion if cardiac arrest
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE tricyclic antidepressant overdose: TCAs (amitriptyline, dosulepin, clomipramine) cause anticholinergic syndrome plus cardiotoxicity (Na channel blockade → QRS widening, arrhythmias, hypotension; seizures). IV sodium bicarbonate (1–2 mmol/kg) for QRS >100 ms or arrhythmias — corrects acidosis, raises pH, reduces free drug. Charcoal within 1h. Benzodiazepines for seizures. Avoid Class I antiarrhythmics (worsen toxicity). Intralipid emulsion if cardiac arrest. Continuous cardiac monitoring for 12–24h.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 4-year-old boy ingests an unknown quantity of his mother's ibuprofen 200 mg tablets. Per TOXBASE, ibuprofen ingestion of >400 mg/kg requires what?
option_c: observation, supportive care (treat any electrolyte imbalance, AKI, GI bleed), monitor renal function and clotting; activated charcoal within 1h of high-dose ingestion
correct_answer: observation, supportive care (treat any electrolyte imbalance, AKI, GI bleed), monitor renal function and clotting; activated charcoal within 1h of high-dose ingestion
explanation: Per TOXBASE NSAID overdose: most NSAID overdoses cause mild GI symptoms only. Significant toxicity (AKI, metabolic acidosis, seizures, coma, GI bleeding) more likely with ibuprofen >400 mg/kg, mefenamic acid (seizures even at moderate doses). Management: supportive care, monitor U&Es, FBC, clotting, LFTs; activated charcoal within 1h. No specific antidote. Mefenamic acid overdose: benzodiazepines for seizures. Severe cases may need renal support.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 40-year-old man has taken 25 of his diazepam 10 mg tablets in a suicide attempt. He is drowsy but rousable, respirations 14/min, sats 96% on air. Per TOXBASE, what is the most appropriate management?
option_d: observation, supportive care, monitor for respiratory depression; flumazenil reversal is rarely used (only for severe iatrogenic toxicity or selected paediatric cases) — risk of seizures, especially if mixed overdose with TCAs/proconvulsants or chronic benzodiazepine use
correct_answer: observation, supportive care, monitor for respiratory depression; flumazenil reversal is rarely used (only for severe iatrogenic toxicity or selected paediatric cases) — risk of seizures, especially if mixed overdose with TCAs/proconvulsants or chronic benzodiazepine use
explanation: Per TOXBASE and BNF: pure benzodiazepine overdose is rarely fatal in isolation (usually fatal in combination with alcohol/opioids/other CNS depressants). Management: airway/breathing/circulation, supportive care, monitor for respiratory depression. Flumazenil (specific GABA-A antagonist) is reserved for severe respiratory depression in selected cases — NOT routine use due to risks: seizures (especially in chronic benzodiazepine users, mixed overdose with proconvulsants), arrhythmias. Short half-life means re-sedation possible.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old man is brought to A&E unconscious. He has pinpoint pupils, respiratory rate 6/min, oxygen sat 86%. A needle is found by his side. Per BNF and Resuscitation Council UK, which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_d: immediate naloxone IV/IM/IN — start at 400 micrograms IV (IM if no IV access), repeat as needed every 2–3 min as naloxone has shorter half-life than most opioids; supportive care: airway, ventilation, oxygenation; observe and consider naloxone infusion
correct_answer: immediate naloxone IV/IM/IN — start at 400 micrograms IV (IM if no IV access), repeat as needed every 2–3 min as naloxone has shorter half-life than most opioids; supportive care: airway, ventilation, oxygenation; observe and consider naloxone infusion
explanation: Per BNF and Resuscitation Council UK: opioid toxicity triad — reduced consciousness, respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils. Naloxone is the specific antidote: IV 400 micrograms (children 5 micrograms/kg), repeat every 2–3 min, then naloxone infusion may be needed (half-life shorter than most opioids — risk of recurrent toxicity). Supportive care: airway/ventilation/oxygenation. Methadone, fentanyl patches and modified-release opioids can cause prolonged toxicity. Take-home naloxone (Prenoxad, nasal spray) is provided to opioid users at risk of overdose.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man with atrial fibrillation has been on digoxin 125 micrograms OD. He presents with confusion, nausea, vomiting and yellow-green vision. His digoxin level is 4.5 micrograms/L (toxic; therapeutic 0.5–2.0). His potassium is 6.2 mmol/L. ECG shows AV block. Per BNF and TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate management?
option_b: digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab) — for severe toxicity (life-threatening arrhythmia, hyperkalaemia >5 mmol/L, level >6 in chronic toxicity or massive acute ingestion); stop digoxin, supportive care, correct electrolytes (caution with IV calcium in hyperkalaemia from digoxin)
correct_answer: digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab) — for severe toxicity (life-threatening arrhythmia, hyperkalaemia >5 mmol/L, level >6 in chronic toxicity or massive acute ingestion); stop digoxin, supportive care, correct electrolytes (caution with IV calcium in hyperkalaemia from digoxin)
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE digoxin toxicity: features include GI symptoms, visual disturbance (yellow-green vision, halos), arrhythmias (any type — AV block, ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation), hyperkalaemia (poor prognostic marker — toxic effect on Na/K-ATPase). Severe toxicity is reversed with digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab, formerly Digibind) — dose based on body burden. Indications: life-threatening arrhythmia, hyperkalaemia >5 mmol/L from digoxin, very high serum levels, massive acute ingestion. Note: IV calcium for hyperkalaemia in digoxin toxicity is controversial — some sources avoid due to historical 'stone heart' concern, although recent evidence suggests it may be safe.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man on lithium 800 mg ON for bipolar affective disorder develops vomiting, tremor, confusion, and ataxia. His lithium level is 3.2 mmol/L (target 0.6–1.0). His creatinine has risen from 80 to 180 micromol/L. Per BNF, which is the most appropriate management?
option_c: no antidote available; stop lithium, IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride) to enhance renal clearance, monitor electrolytes, ECG, neurological status; urgent haemodialysis indicated for severe toxicity (levels >2.5–4 with symptoms, renal failure, or persistent symptoms); seek toxicology advice
correct_answer: no antidote available; stop lithium, IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride) to enhance renal clearance, monitor electrolytes, ECG, neurological status; urgent haemodialysis indicated for severe toxicity (levels >2.5–4 with symptoms, renal failure, or persistent symptoms); seek toxicology advice
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE lithium toxicity: severe lithium toxicity (>2.5 mmol/L with symptoms, or any level with renal failure or neurological features) requires hospital admission and consideration of haemodialysis — lithium is renally cleared and dialysable. Stop lithium, IV normal saline for volume expansion (enhances renal lithium clearance), correct electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities, neurological observation. Risk of permanent neurological deficits ('SILENT' syndrome — Syndrome of Irreversible Lithium-Effectuated NeuroToxicity). Precipitating factors: dehydration, NSAID/ACEi co-use, renal impairment, intentional overdose.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man is brought to A&E unresponsive after his wife found him with empty bottles of metformin and gliclazide. Capillary glucose is 1.8 mmol/L. Per BNF and TOXBASE, which is the most appropriate immediate management?
option_b: IV glucose (e.g. 150 mL of 10% glucose or 75 mL of 20%, then ongoing infusion); octreotide can be considered for sulfonylurea-induced refractory hypoglycaemia (inhibits insulin release); monitor glucose closely; intensive observation as risk of recurrent hypoglycaemia (sulfonylureas have long half-lives)
correct_answer: IV glucose (e.g. 150 mL of 10% glucose or 75 mL of 20%, then ongoing infusion); octreotide can be considered for sulfonylurea-induced refractory hypoglycaemia (inhibits insulin release); monitor glucose closely; intensive observation as risk of recurrent hypoglycaemia (sulfonylureas have long half-lives)
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: sulfonylurea overdose (gliclazide, glipizide, glimepiride, glibenclamide) causes severe and prolonged hypoglycaemia due to insulin release from pancreatic beta-cells. Management: IV glucose for acute correction (10% or 20% glucose), then ongoing glucose infusion. Octreotide IV/SC suppresses sulfonylurea-induced insulin release in refractory cases. Observation for 24–48h minimum (long-acting sulfonylureas have prolonged effects). Avoid hypotonic fluids (cerebral oedema risk). Metformin overdose: lactic acidosis risk if significant amount — may need dialysis if severe.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old man with bipolar affective disorder on lithium and a recent flu develops persistent vomiting and dehydration. His next blood test shows lithium 1.8 mmol/L (was 0.7 previously). Per BNF, what is the most likely explanation?
option_a: dehydration from vomiting + reduced renal clearance from intercurrent illness leads to lithium retention — provide rehydration, dose-reduce or temporarily withhold lithium, recheck level; counsel patient on lithium sick-day rules
correct_answer: dehydration from vomiting + reduced renal clearance from intercurrent illness leads to lithium retention — provide rehydration, dose-reduce or temporarily withhold lithium, recheck level; counsel patient on lithium sick-day rules
explanation: Per BNF lithium monograph: lithium is renally cleared with a narrow therapeutic index. Dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, hot weather, reduced fluid intake), drug interactions (NSAIDs, ACEi, ARBs, diuretics — especially thiazides), renal impairment all reduce clearance and increase lithium levels. Sick-day rules for lithium: maintain hydration, withhold lithium for vomiting/severe diarrhoea, seek prompt advice. Patient should carry a lithium alert card and have routine monitoring. Avoid NSAIDs for analgesia (use paracetamol).

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old woman is admitted with confusion, neuromuscular irritability, and seizures. Her serum sodium is 116 mmol/L (low). She is on chronic SSRI (sertraline 100 mg OD) and recently started indapamide 2.5 mg OD. Per BNF, what is the most likely cause?
option_a: drug-induced hyponatraemia from SIADH (SSRI) and/or thiazide-like diuretic (indapamide) effects — both common in older patients; stop the offending drug(s), fluid restriction, careful sodium correction (avoid >10 mmol/L/24h to prevent central pontine myelinolysis)
correct_answer: drug-induced hyponatraemia from SIADH (SSRI) and/or thiazide-like diuretic (indapamide) effects — both common in older patients; stop the offending drug(s), fluid restriction, careful sodium correction (avoid >10 mmol/L/24h to prevent central pontine myelinolysis)
explanation: Per BNF and MHRA: SSRIs cause SIADH (hyponatraemia), especially in older adults, women, low BMI, concurrent diuretic use, renal impairment. Thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics (bendroflumethiazide, indapamide) also commonly cause hyponatraemia. Combination is high-risk. Management: investigate and correct severe symptomatic hyponatraemia carefully (rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination — central pontine myelinolysis); stop offending drug(s); fluid restrict; replace electrolytes cautiously. Severe (Na <120 mmol/L): consider hypertonic saline in specialist setting.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old runner is brought to A&E unconscious after a hot day with high humidity and prolonged exertion. He is hyperthermic (core temp 41.5°C), dry skin, tachycardic, confused. Which is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?
option_a: heat stroke — immediate aggressive cooling (cold water immersion, evaporative cooling, ice packs in groin/axilla), IV fluids, electrolyte correction, ABCDE, ITU support; specific drug treatment NOT effective
correct_answer: heat stroke — immediate aggressive cooling (cold water immersion, evaporative cooling, ice packs in groin/axilla), IV fluids, electrolyte correction, ABCDE, ITU support; specific drug treatment NOT effective
explanation: Per emergency medicine guidance: heat stroke (T >40°C, CNS dysfunction) is a medical emergency. Aggressive cooling is the priority — every minute counts. Methods: cold water immersion (most effective), evaporative cooling (spray + fan), ice packs to groin/axilla. IV fluids, ABCDE. Antipyretics (paracetamol, NSAIDs) are NOT effective in heat stroke (the mechanism is loss of thermoregulation, not infection). Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, AKI, DIC, multi-organ failure. ITU for severe cases.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man takes 30 tablets of his bisoprolol 10 mg in a suicide attempt. He is bradycardic (HR 35), hypotensive (BP 70/40) and feels faint. Per BNF and TOXBASE, what is the most appropriate management?
option_a: atropine 500 micrograms IV (may need repeat doses), IV fluids, IV glucagon 5–10 mg bolus then infusion (for severe beta-blocker toxicity refractory to atropine — increases cAMP independent of beta-receptors), high-dose insulin and dextrose if severe; consider pacing/intralipid; ICU input
correct_answer: atropine 500 micrograms IV (may need repeat doses), IV fluids, IV glucagon 5–10 mg bolus then infusion (for severe beta-blocker toxicity refractory to atropine — increases cAMP independent of beta-receptors), high-dose insulin and dextrose if severe; consider pacing/intralipid; ICU input
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE beta-blocker overdose: severe bradycardia, hypotension, hypoglycaemia, AV block. Management: atropine 500 micrograms IV (up to 3 mg), IV fluids, glucagon IV is the specific antidote (5–10 mg bolus then infusion 50 micrograms/kg/h — bypasses beta-receptor blockade by activating Gs proteins → cAMP). High-dose insulin/euglycaemic therapy and intralipid for severe refractory toxicity. Pacing for refractory bradycardia. Activated charcoal within 1h. ICU support.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old woman attends A&E 6 hours after taking 75 paracetamol 500 mg tablets and 30 of her partner's tramadol 50 mg tablets. She is now drowsy but rousable, RR 12. The team starts N-acetylcysteine. Per BNF and TOXBASE, what additional toxicology consideration is most important?
option_a: additional naloxone may be needed for opioid (tramadol) component; tramadol overdose can cause respiratory depression, seizures, serotonin syndrome; supportive care, monitor closely, ABCDE
correct_answer: additional naloxone may be needed for opioid (tramadol) component; tramadol overdose can cause respiratory depression, seizures, serotonin syndrome; supportive care, monitor closely, ABCDE
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: mixed overdoses are common and clinically complex. Tramadol toxicity causes opioid effects (respiratory depression — give naloxone), seizures (especially in overdose or co-ingestion with SSRIs/SNRIs), and serotonin syndrome (with serotonergic drugs). Naloxone reverses respiratory depression but may not prevent seizures. Plus full paracetamol management with NAC. Always check for co-ingestion (ECG for QT prolongation in mixed overdoses; salicylates in chronic users). TOXBASE provides specific guidance on each agent.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 28-year-old man is admitted to A&E with deliberate ingestion of an unknown number of his mother's methadone tablets. He is comatose, RR 4/min, pinpoint pupils. Per BNF and TOXBASE, what is the most important consideration after initial naloxone?
option_c: methadone has a very long elimination half-life (15–60 hours) — single naloxone dose may not be sufficient, repeated doses or naloxone infusion required; prolonged observation in critical care; risk of recurrent respiratory depression once naloxone wears off
correct_answer: methadone has a very long elimination half-life (15–60 hours) — single naloxone dose may not be sufficient, repeated doses or naloxone infusion required; prolonged observation in critical care; risk of recurrent respiratory depression once naloxone wears off
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: methadone has a very long half-life (15–60 hours), much longer than naloxone (~1 hour). Single naloxone doses can cause apparent recovery followed by recurrent severe respiratory depression as naloxone wears off. Naloxone infusion is usually needed (start at 60% of dose that produced response, titrate). Patients must be observed in monitored bed (often ITU) for at least 24–48 hours. Similar consideration with fentanyl patches and modified-release opioids. ECG: methadone causes QT prolongation and torsades.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man with newly diagnosed Parkinson's disease on co-careldopa is brought to A&E with high fever (40°C), severe muscle rigidity, autonomic instability and reduced consciousness. He had his Parkinson's medication held for 3 days due to surgery. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
option_c: neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like syndrome / Parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome — caused by abrupt cessation/reduction of dopaminergic medication; restart Parkinson medication, supportive care, IV fluids, cooling, dantrolene/bromocriptine may help
correct_answer: neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like syndrome / Parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome — caused by abrupt cessation/reduction of dopaminergic medication; restart Parkinson medication, supportive care, IV fluids, cooling, dantrolene/bromocriptine may help
explanation: Per BNF and Parkinson's UK guidance: abrupt cessation of dopaminergic drugs (e.g. levodopa, dopamine agonists) in Parkinson's disease can precipitate a syndrome similar to neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) — hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness, raised CK. This is life-threatening and emphasises the importance of NOT stopping Parkinson medication around surgery or admission. Management: restart Parkinson medication urgently (NG tube if NBM), supportive care, dantrolene/bromocriptine. Pharmacists have a critical role advocating for time-critical Parkinson medication.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 30-year-old man has taken 30 tablets of his oxycodone modified-release 20 mg. He is found unconscious. RR 5/min. After 800 micrograms naloxone, he wakes briefly then becomes drowsy again. Per BNF and TOXBASE, what is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: accept that naloxone has not worked
option_d: naloxone infusion (e.g. 60% of waking dose per hour) or repeated boluses; HDU/ICU admission; modified-release opioid overdoses have prolonged toxicity (continued absorption from GI tract over 12+ hours); consider whole-bowel irrigation; monitor for rebound respiratory depression
correct_answer: naloxone infusion (e.g. 60% of waking dose per hour) or repeated boluses; HDU/ICU admission; modified-release opioid overdoses have prolonged toxicity (continued absorption from GI tract over 12+ hours); consider whole-bowel irrigation; monitor for rebound respiratory depression
explanation: Per BNF and TOXBASE: modified-release opioid overdose presents unique challenges — slow GI absorption causes prolonged toxicity (12–24h). Naloxone has shorter duration than the opioid, requiring infusion (rule of thumb: 60% of the waking bolus dose given per hour). Critical care monitoring essential. Whole-bowel irrigation (polyethylene glycol electrolyte solution via NG) can be considered to clear unabsorbed drug. Activated charcoal less useful beyond 1h. Patients should be observed for at least 24 hours post-final naloxone dose.

section_title: Clinical Scenarios for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
